assumptions: Sites A, B and C are illustrative archetypes; real sites mix these factors, which is why a single condition is a prompt to compare rather than to switch.

Under a beam-and-slab the slab between beams is normally cast on fill used as formwork, not on engineered void formers; the fill is not permanent and can settle or wash out, leaving the slab spanning beam-to-beam over what is effectively a void. Engineered void formers under a beam-and-slab are the exception (very high reactivity), not the rule.

Under a flat plate the void former is a placed blanket that supports the wet pour and then collapses as the soil takes up moisture, leaving the plate isolated on its piles; buoyancy and set-out during the pour are part of its cost.

The 250–300 mm figure for a flat plate heading thick is indicative of where the pour itself becomes a heavy, hot, reinforcement-dense job and beams plus a thin (~100 mm) infill slab often become the easier build; it is not a fixed threshold.

data.evidence:
Flat plate and punching. Concrete Society guidance on pile-supported floors establishes the core mechanism — load can transfer directly into the piles without beams, but punching around the heads may govern and local thickening may be needed, with span-to-depth limits and cracking recognised risks. Hulett's work on pile-supported concrete floors is the caution against treating the plate as tidy-and-done: cracking over piles, mid-span deflection and crack-avoidance margins all become live once a slab spans to pile heads. Together they support the lever that the flat plate is the cleaner build only while it stays structurally clean at the heads — and that a plate driven to thickenings has become a flat slab.

Beam-and-slab as the conventional suspended floor. Across UK residential practice the suspended ground floor is overwhelmingly built on beams: beam-and-block on ground beams (or in-situ ground beams with an infill slab) is the dominant new-build ground floor where the floor cannot bear directly on the ground, and ground beams are the standard way to span between piles for low-rise work. The flat plate spanning supports, by contrast, is essentially a commercial and industrial slab technique rather than a residential one. This is the basis for treating beam-and-slab as the habit and the flat plate as the under-considered option — and it is established without leaning on a residential-slab standard.

Heave isolation and the gap. A suspended floor is isolated from heaving clay by being lifted off the ground; where heave is a risk, practice guidance has a compressible material or void former provided below the floor so the swelling soil never loads it. The constructability reading carried here — that under a flat plate this gap is a placed void-former blanket, while under a beam-and-slab it is fill used as formwork that self-voids — is buildability reasoning consistent with that mechanism, not a measured rule, and the structural design of the isolation stays with the engineer.

Span and grid economics. Comparative cost work on flat-plate versus beam-and-slab floors finds flat plate cheaper at tight spans but beam-and-slab cheaper as spans widen, with flat-slab dimensions commonly driven to where punching governs and reinforcement densities well above those of a solid slab. This is from elevated building floors rather than small piled ground floors, so it is carried as direction, not proof: removing beams simplifies the build only while the slab does not turn structurally demanding.

What was not found. No source directly compares a residential pile-supported flat plate against a residential pile-supported beam-and-slab on build time, spoil, steel-fixing hours, concrete volume, cost or defect rate, and none sets the grid spacing or slab depth at which the answer flips. The 0/1/2/3 condition-count bands are constructability judgement, not a measured rule.

References
1. The Concrete Society. Pile-supported floors (technical guidance).
2. Hulett, T. (2011). Design and construction of pile-supported concrete floors. Concrete, September 2011.
3. UK suspended ground-floor practice: NHBC / LABC technical guidance and industry references on beam-and-block and ground-beam suspended floors; Designing Buildings, Ground beams in construction and Beam and block floor construction.
4. Practice guidance on void formers / compressible material below suspended floors over reactive or heave-prone ground.
5. Comparative Cost Study of Reinforced Concrete Flat Slab and Slab-with-Beam Floor Systems (elevated floors).

supportingImages.title:
Pile-supported floor system — beam-and-slab vs flat plate

Both options are pile-supported. The beam-and-slab option collects load through ground beams between pile heads, which introduces ground-beam trenches and beam cages. The flat-plate option keeps the working platform flatter by removing ground-beam trenches, but pile-head punching shear becomes a key design check and void formers may be needed below the slab.

evidenceSupport.summary: Each lever is supported on point for its mechanism — Concrete Society and Hulett on the flat plate transferring directly to piles but becoming punching- and cracking-critical at the heads; UK suspended-floor practice on beam-and-slab being the conventional residential system and the flat plate the under-considered import; void-former / compressible-layer guidance on heave being handled by lifting the floor off the ground; and flat-plate-versus-beam cost work on the span-based crossover and the depth and reinforcement penalty when punching governs. The limitation is that no source directly compares the two systems for small pile-supported residential ground floors, the cost comparison is from elevated floors, and the fill-as-formwork-versus-void-former-blanket framing is buildability reasoning — so the 0/1/2/3 condition-count bands are reasoned judgement rather than measured thresholds.

relatedEntries.note: The decision one level up. Settles whether to pile below reactive ground at all; this entry then settles how the floor connects to the piles, with heave still live.
Upstream pile-method choice; affects spoil, access, tolerance and the plant already on site. Weigh together.
If the piles are screw piles, the as-built verification and head-connection burden applies whichever floor sits above, and bears on uplift fixity on reactive ground.

items.name: Reactive-ground strategy: found below vs. stiffen on top

data.question: On reactive ground, is it more buildable to pile down below the active zone, or to stiffen a slab and ride the movement on top of it?
data.parameter: How many of the three conditions favouring founding below apply.
data.background:
On reactive ground — clay that heaves and shrinks as its moisture changes — there are two broad ways to carry a light floor, and they are different construction jobs, not just different structural designs. One is to found below the active zone: pile or pier down to a more stable layer so the floor no longer relies on the moving surface clay. The other is to stiffen on top: build a more rigid ground-bearing slab — a deeper-ribbed raft or waffle — and ride the movement, controlling it with rigidity, perimeter detailing, drainage and articulation. This entry is the front door to the slab-and-foundation cluster: it settles the deep-versus-shallow question first, and the downstream entries then pick up whichever branch you land on.

The default is to stay shallow, because shallow is the simpler build: a shallow or above-ground pour, no rig, no spoil run, no founding verification and no slab-to-pile integration. Founding below buys a floor that is largely indifferent to the surface clay, but it brings the piling rig, the spoil, the founding check and the pile-head connection onto the job. Neither is free — the question is which set of construction problems you would rather own — so the entry counts how many of three conditions push you off the shallow default. None present means stiffening on top is the cleaner build; all three means the shallow slab is solving a problem the site will not let it solve, and founding below is the more honest job.

Movement too big to ride. Where reactivity is high or the active zone is deep, slab stiffness stops being a credible answer on its own. You can keep deepening ribs, but past a point you are buying rigidity the floor still cannot fully use, and the residual movement lands on perimeter detailing and articulation to manage for the life of the floor.

You are piling anyway. The whole saving of staying shallow is that you avoid the rig and the spoil. Where soft or problem ground already forces internal piers through the footprint — not just around the perimeter — that saving has already evaporated: the rig is mobilised, the spoil is going off site, and the floor is part-piled regardless. At that point founding properly below is often the tidier build than a stiffened slab threaded around piers it was trying to avoid.

The structure cannot tolerate residual movement. Stiffening on top is a manage-the-movement strategy, not an eliminate-it one. Where the floor level is fixed, an accessibility threshold is in play, or the finishes are brittle or movement-sensitive, 'ride it and detail around it' is not an acceptable answer, and the case for taking the floor off the moving ground altogether strengthens.

One trap on the shallow branch worth carrying here. If you stay shallow and the answer to reactivity is to keep thickening the raft, watch that the pour itself does not quietly become the construction problem — a raft driven thick enough is a large heat-generating pour that needs mix control, pour planning, possibly staging and thermal monitoring to build well. That creeping pour burden is itself a quiet nudge back toward founding below: if stiffening on top is only 'winning' by becoming a difficult mass pour, the shallow saving it was meant to protect is already being spent.

So the count routes you. Land on staying shallow and the next question is which ground-bearing slab — raft versus waffle pod. Land on founding below and the next questions are which pile method, then how the floor gets from the piles to the slab. This entry stops at the fork; it does not adjudicate the branches.

Scope guard: this entry is about the buildability of the system choice, not how to design for reactive soil. It compares the construction consequences of going deep versus staying shallow; the reactive-soil assessment and the structural design itself stay with the engineer.

thresholds.guidance: None of the three conditions is present: reactivity is mild-to-moderate and rideable, no internal piling is otherwise forced, and the structure can tolerate the residual movement a stiffened slab leaves. Staying shallow is the simpler build and the right default — a shallow or above-ground pour with no rig, spoil run or founding verification. Carry on to the ground-bearing slab choice: raft slab vs waffle pod. Do not reach for piles out of caution on a site a stiffened slab can ride.

One condition is present. Staying shallow is still the likely build, but pressure-test it rather than defaulting — confirm the single trigger is real and not just conservative, and check it is not the leading edge of the other two (high reactivity often brings forced internal piers and tight movement tolerance with it). If it holds as a genuine one-off, proceed shallow to raft vs waffle pod, but note the deep branch exists.

Two conditions are present. This is now a genuinely balanced, deliberate system choice, not a default either way — the shallow saving is half-spent and founding below is a real contender. Decide it consciously and early, with the engineer's reactive-soil assessment in hand, rather than letting habit pick the stiffened slab. Whichever way it lands, hand off to the matching branch.

All three conditions are present: the movement is too big to ride, the ground is forcing piles regardless, and the structure cannot tolerate residual movement. The simple shallow slab is effectively off the table — stiffening on top would be solving a problem the site will not let it solve. Found below: settle the pile method at bored vs screw piles, then how the floor meets the heads at pile-supported floor — flat plate vs beam-and-slab.

alternatives.title: Default to staying shallow, then count the go-deep triggers
alternatives.description: The simpler build is a ground-bearing stiffened slab — a shallow or above-ground pour with no rig, spoil run, founding verification or pile-head connection. Treat that as the default and move off it only as the three conditions stack: count genuine triggers, and do not switch to piling on a single conservative worry.

alternatives.title: Trigger one — is the movement too big to ride?
alternatives.description: Where reactivity is high or the active zone is deep, deepening ribs buys rigidity the floor cannot fully use, and the residual movement lands on perimeter detailing and articulation for the floor's life. If stiffness alone is not a credible answer, that is one trigger toward founding below. The reactive-soil assessment itself stays with the engineer.

alternatives.title: Trigger two — are you piling anyway?
alternatives.description: The shallow saving is that you avoid the rig and the spoil. Where soft or problem ground already forces internal piers through the footprint, that saving is gone — the rig is on site and the floor is part-piled regardless. Founding properly below is often tidier than a stiffened slab threaded around piers it was trying to avoid.

alternatives.title: Trigger three — can the structure tolerate residual movement?
alternatives.description: Stiffening on top manages movement, it does not remove it. A fixed floor level, an accessibility threshold, or brittle, movement-sensitive finishes mean 'ride it and detail around it' is not acceptable, and the case for taking the floor off the moving ground strengthens.

alternatives.title: Watch the thickening-raft trap
alternatives.description: If you stay shallow and keep thickening the raft to chase rigidity, check the pour has not become the problem — a raft driven thick is a large heat-generating pour needing mix control, pour planning, possibly staging and thermal monitoring. A stiffened slab that only 'wins' by becoming a difficult mass pour has already spent the saving that justified staying shallow, which is itself a nudge back toward founding below.

alternatives.title: Settle this fork before the downstream entries
alternatives.description: This is the front door, not the slab-selection itself. Land on shallow and read raft vs waffle pod; land on deep and read bored vs screw piles, then pile-supported floor. Decide deep-versus-shallow first so the downstream entries answer the right question — do not start sizing a waffle pod before you have confirmed you are staying ground-bearing at all.

alternatives.title: Keep it a buildability call, not reactive-soil design
alternatives.description: The rating counts construction consequences — rig, spoil, founding verification, pile integration, perimeter detailing and the pour burden — not site reactivity numbers. The reactive-soil classification and the structural design stay with the engineer; use the count as a review prompt for which construction job to commit to.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to light, low-rise floors — typically residential — on reactive (shrink-swell) ground, where the foundation choice is between a ground-bearing stiffened slab (raft or waffle) that rides the movement and founding below the active zone on piles or piers. It is the system-selection fork upstream of the individual slab- and pile-choice entries.

checks.title: Source of the reactivity and active-zone depth
checks.description: The count leans on how reactive the ground is and how deep the active zone runs. That comes from the geotechnical assessment, not from this entry — confirm it before reading a high reactivity as a trigger.

checks.title: Whether internal piers are already forced
checks.description: If soft or problem ground already requires piers through the footprint rather than only at the perimeter, the shallow saving is largely gone and trigger two is live. Check this against the geotechnical picture, not just the perimeter conditions.

checks.title: Floor-level and finish movement tolerance
checks.description: A fixed floor level, an accessibility threshold, or brittle finishes can make residual movement unacceptable even where reactivity alone is rideable. Confirm what the structure above can actually tolerate.

checks.title: Regional plant, spoil and practice
checks.description: The cost of founding below depends on local rig availability, spoil disposal and common practice. The deep build is a smaller step where piling plant is routine and a larger one where it must be mobilised specially — weigh the location tag before applying the guidance elsewhere.

applicability.note: Use the count as a constructability review prompt for which construction job to commit to, not as a substitute for the reactive-soil assessment. The reactive-soil design stays with the engineer; this entry only weighs the buildability of going deep versus staying shallow.

workedExample.title: Worked example — two sites, the same three triggers
workedExample.caveat: Site A and Site B are generic archetypes for illustration, not a real project. The verdict comes from counting the go-deep triggers, not from any drawing — you can run the same three checks on a site from words alone.
workedExample.headline: The same three triggers, opposite answers — and the count routes you to the next entry.
tableHeaders: Site A — new house, moderately reactive level block
Site B — extension on highly reactive, soft ground
tableRows: No — moderate reactivity a stiffened slab can ride with normal perimeter detailing
Yes — high reactivity and a deep active zone; stiffness alone is not a credible answer
No — firm, uniform ground; no internal piers forced
Yes — soft spots already force piers through the footprint, so the rig and spoil are on site regardless
No — new build, flexible finishes, floor level free to sit where it suits
Yes — must match the existing floor level and tie into brittle finishes; residual movement is not acceptable
0 → stay shallow → raft vs waffle pod
3 → found below → bored vs screw piles, then pile-supported floor
workedExample.howToUse: Run the same three checks on your own site, count the genuine triggers, and read the band: 0 keeps you shallow and routes you to raft vs waffle pod; 3 takes the shallow slab off the table and routes you to the pile method then the pile-supported floor; 1 or 2 is a deliberate, decide-it-early call rather than a default. Note that Site B trips all three for different reasons — movement, forced piling and a fixed floor — which is exactly when stiffening on top stops being the simpler build.
assumptions: Site A and Site B are illustrative archetypes; real sites mix reactivity, ground strength and floor-level constraints in their own way, which is why one or two triggers is treated as 'decide deliberately', not an automatic switch.
The reactivity and active-zone depth behind trigger one come from the geotechnical assessment, not from this entry. Confirm them before reading a high reactivity as a trigger.
Trigger two turns on whether piers are forced through the footprint, not just at the perimeter — perimeter-only edge thickening or isolated piers do not on their own spend the shallow saving.
This entry stops at the deep-versus-shallow fork. It does not size a slab or a pile — the downstream entries do that once the branch is settled.

data.evidence:
Mechanism — stiffening on top. The industry and training basis for riding reactive movement with a more rigid ground slab is the same depth-and-stiffness lever set out in the raft-versus-waffle entry: deeper ribs give a more rigid slab that better resists heave and shrink movement, which is why pod depth is treated there as a rigidity lever on reactive ground. Stiffening on top is therefore a real and common answer — but a manage-the-movement one, leaving residual movement to perimeter detailing, drainage and articulation, which is the limit this entry turns on.

Mechanism — founding below. Founding below the active zone removes the floor's reliance on the moving surface clay, at the cost of bringing the piling rig, the spoil run, the founding verification and the slab-to-pile integration onto the job. These are the same construction burdens weighed in the pile-method and pile-supported-floor entries, applied here one level up to the decision of whether to take them on at all.

Practitioner evidence — piling tends to multiply on problem ground. Practitioner guidance (Cornell Engineers) describes how soft or problem ground tends to force internal piers through the footprint rather than only at the perimeter — the same observation the raft-versus-waffle entry uses to show piling eroding the pod's advantage. Carried up to this fork, it is the basis for the second trigger: once the ground is forcing piers anyway, the shallow saving is already spent and founding properly below is often the tidier build.

Confidence and limits. There is no direct comparative buildability study that sets the deep-versus-shallow call by reactivity for small Australian residential work, so the three-trigger count is reasoned, default-and-deviate constructability judgement rather than a measured threshold. The mechanism legs are each supported — slab stiffness against movement, the construction burden of founding below, and the tendency of problem ground to force piers — but the entry should be read as a structured review prompt for which construction job to own, not a proven decision rule. The reactive-soil classification and the structural design sit with the engineer.

supportingImages.title:
Reactive ground strategy — found below vs stiffen on top

The first decision on reactive ground is whether the primary support is taken below the movement zone, or whether the slab is stiffened to work within the active zone. This decision sets up the next constructability review: pile-supported floor system selection, or ground-bearing slab selection between stiffened raft and waffle pod options.

evidenceSupport.summary: The mechanism legs are each supported: the stiffness-against-movement lever (the same depth/rigidity basis used in the raft-vs-waffle entry, from Australian industry and training sources), the construction burden of founding below (rig, spoil, founding verification and slab-to-pile integration, as weighed in the pile-method and pile-supported-floor entries), and the tendency of soft or problem ground to force internal piers through the footprint (practitioner guidance, Cornell Engineers). No direct comparative buildability study sets the deep-versus-shallow call by reactivity for small Australian residential work, so the three-trigger count is reasoned default-and-deviate constructability judgement rather than a measured threshold. Treat the entry as the system-selection front door and a review prompt, not a proven decision rule; the reactive-soil assessment and structural design stay with the engineer.

relatedEntries.note: The shallow branch. If this entry lands on staying ground-bearing, that entry picks which slab — stiffened raft vs waffle pod.
The deep branch, part one. If this entry lands on founding below, that entry settles which pile method suits the site.
The deep branch, part two. Once piling is chosen, that entry settles how the floor gets from the piles to the slab — flat plate vs beam-and-slab.
Relevant on the deep branch if the piles are screw piles — the as-built verification and head-connection burden bears on the cost of founding below, and on uplift fixity on reactive ground.

items.name: Non-combustible substructure and floor in bushfire areas

data.question: In a bushfire area, is a combustible floor or pier undermining a non-combustible substructure?
data.parameter: The bushfire attack level, and whether any load-bearing or subfloor element in the attack path is combustible.
data.background:
Two different requirements both get called ‘fire’ and are easily confused. One is the structural fire resistance an element needs against a fully developed fire inside the building. The other — the subject of this entry — is resistance to a bushfire attacking from outside. In a bushfire-prone area that external exposure is rated as a bushfire attack level, from low up to flame zone, driven by the surrounding vegetation, slope and separation. A building is attacked by three mechanisms: wind-driven embers (firebrands), radiant heat, and direct flame contact. Embers are the dominant cause of structure loss, arriving ahead of the fire front and continuing after it passes, and lodging wherever they can collect.

The constructability concern is a mismatched material scheme. A building can be given a durable, non-combustible concrete substructure — piles or footings — and then have its bushfire performance handed straight back to the weakest combustible link sitting on top: timber piers or stumps, timber bearers, or a suspended timber floor. A raised, open subfloor is precisely where wind-driven embers accumulate and lodge, so a combustible element in that position is an ignition point no matter how good the concrete beneath it is. The performance of the assembly is set by its most combustible structural element, not by its best one. Standing concrete piles on timber piers, or topping a non-combustible substructure with a combustible floor, partly wastes the very spend that made the substructure non-combustible.

The honest qualification is that timber is not categorically banned in bushfire areas. It can be used with appropriate detailing — bushfire-resisting species, enclosed subfloors and the like — and the requirements ramp up with the attack level. But where the rest of the substructure is already non-combustible, a combustible structural or subfloor element is usually the avoidable weak link, and a non-combustible substructure and floor is the naturally coherent answer to the exposure.

As with the structural-fire case, the number lives elsewhere. The bushfire attack level comes from a bushfire assessment prepared under the architect or a bushfire consultant, not from the structural set, so the structural engineer choosing the substructure material may do it without the attack level in front of them. This entry is a concept-stage material-coherence prompt to raise that question early; assessing the attack level and detailing any combustible element are the bushfire consultant’s responsibility, not this entry’s.

thresholds.guidance: Substructure and floor are non-combustible throughout — for example concrete piles or footings carrying a concrete floor — with no combustible structural or subfloor element in the attack path. A coherent scheme that suits both the exposure and the durable substructure, and concrete’s natural fit. Confirm the attack level with the bushfire assessment and proceed.

A low attack level applies, or any combustible element is limited and appropriately detailed for the exposure. Plausibly fine, but confirm the attack level from the bushfire report and that the detailing matches it, rather than assuming a low exposure. Where the substructure is otherwise non-combustible, check whether a combustible element is the avoidable weak link.

A combustible load-bearing or subfloor element sits in the attack path at a meaningful attack level — timber piers, bearers or a suspended timber floor — often above an otherwise non-combustible concrete substructure. The assembly’s bushfire performance is being set by its most combustible element, and a raised open subfloor is where embers accumulate. The more severe the exposure the more pressing this is, and at flame zone the most severe exposure is being met by the least suitable material, leaving any non-combustible substructure beneath it partly wasted. Resolve the material before the substructure is fixed — keep the load path and subfloor non-combustible — or confirm with the bushfire consultant that the detailing genuinely carries the exposure. This flags an incoherent material scheme, not a prohibition on timber where it is properly detailed for the exposure.

alternatives.title: Get the attack level before fixing the substructure
alternatives.description: At concept stage, obtain the site’s bushfire attack level from the bushfire assessment (under the architect or bushfire consultant) before choosing the substructure and floor material. The attack level decides how exposed the elements are and whether a combustible one is acceptable.

alternatives.title: Keep the load path and subfloor coherent with the substructure
alternatives.description: Where the substructure is non-combustible (concrete piles or footings), keep the elements above it — piers, bearers, floor — non-combustible too, so the assembly’s bushfire performance is not set by a single combustible link. A concrete or other non-combustible floor system continues the substructure’s logic.

alternatives.title: Treat the raised subfloor as an ember trap
alternatives.description: An open, raised subfloor is where wind-driven embers collect and lodge. Avoid combustible structural or subfloor elements there; where a subfloor is unavoidable, enclosing or protecting it against ember entry is a recognised mitigation, to be coordinated with the bushfire consultant.

alternatives.title: Don't undermine a non-combustible substructure
alternatives.description: Spending on concrete piles or footings and then standing them on timber piers, or topping them with a timber floor, in a bushfire area partly wastes that spend, because the most combustible structural element governs the result. If concrete is justified below, it is usually justified above.

alternatives.title: Coordinate with the bushfire consultant
alternatives.description: Bushfire compliance — the attack-level assessment and the detailing of any combustible element — is the bushfire consultant’s responsibility. Use this prompt to raise the material-coherence question early and confirm any retained timber is genuinely adequate for the attack level, rather than discovering the conflict late.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to the choice of substructure and floor material for a building in a bushfire-prone area, where a non-combustible substructure (such as concrete piles or footings) could be undermined by a combustible load-bearing or subfloor element above it. It is a concept-stage material-coherence prompt for the structural engineer. It does not assess the bushfire attack level or design the bushfire detailing, which are the bushfire consultant’s responsibility, and it is not a prohibition on timber where that material is properly detailed for the exposure.

checks.title: Where the attack level comes from
checks.description: The bushfire attack level is set by a bushfire assessment under the architect or bushfire consultant, not by the structural design. Obtain it before choosing the substructure and floor material.

checks.title: The most combustible element governs
checks.description: An assembly’s bushfire performance is set by its weakest link in the attack path. A non-combustible substructure does not protect the building if a combustible pier, bearer or floor sits above it in the path of embers, radiant heat or flame.

checks.title: Raised subfloors collect embers
checks.description: Open, raised subfloors are recognised ember-accumulation zones. Combustible elements there are ignition points; weigh whether the subfloor can be non-combustible or enclosed.

checks.title: Timber is allowed if detailed for the exposure
checks.description: Combustible materials are not categorically barred in bushfire areas; they can be used with appropriate detailing, with requirements increasing as the attack level rises. The concern is an avoidable combustible weak link above a non-combustible substructure, not timber as such.

checks.title: Coherence of the spend
checks.description: Where concrete is justified for the substructure (durability, exposure, founding), continuing non-combustible above it usually produces a more coherent and better-value scheme than reverting to combustible elements.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a concept-stage review prompt, not a rule that bushfire-area buildings must be entirely concrete. The bands scale with how severe the exposure is and whether a combustible structural or subfloor element sits in the attack path. The bushfire-attack assessment and the detailing of any combustible element remain the bushfire consultant’s responsibility under the relevant requirements.

data.evidence:
How bushfires reach structures. Fire spreads to buildings in the wildland–urban interface by three pathways — radiant heat, direct flame contact, and ember (firebrand) attack — and across post-fire investigations embers are found to be directly or indirectly responsible for the majority of structure losses, travelling ahead of and behind the fire front and igniting wherever they accumulate (Cohen, 2000; Caton, Hakes, Gorham, Zhou, & Gollner, 2017).

Why combustible substructure elements are the weak link. Reviews of building response in these fires identify components and assemblies such as decks, subfloors and vents as key ignition vulnerabilities, with combustible elements in ember-exposed positions acting as ignition points and non-combustible materials reducing the likelihood of ignition (Hakes, Caton, Gorham, & Gollner, 2017; Quarles, Valachovic, Nakamura, Nader, & De Lasaux, 2010). Because the assembly ignites at its most exposed combustible element, a non-combustible substructure is undermined by a combustible pier, bearer or floor placed above it in the attack path.

Basis of the bands. The bands scale with how severe the external exposure is and whether a combustible structural or subfloor element sits in the path of attack. The exposure tiers and their cut-points are reasoned practitioner judgement; the actual attack level for a given site is an output of a bushfire assessment, not of this entry.

References
Caton, S. E., Hakes, R. S. P., Gorham, D. J., Zhou, A., & Gollner, M. J. (2017). Review of pathways for building fire spread in the wildland urban interface Part I: Exposure conditions. Fire Technology, 53, 429–473.
Cohen, J. D. (2000). Preventing disaster: Home ignitability in the wildland–urban interface. Journal of Forestry, 98(3), 15–21.
Hakes, R. S. P., Caton, S. E., Gorham, D. J., & Gollner, M. J. (2017). A review of pathways for building fire spread in the wildland urban interface Part II: Response of components and systems and mitigation strategies in the United States. Fire Technology, 53, 475–515.
Quarles, S. L., Valachovic, Y., Nakamura, G. M., Nader, G. A., & De Lasaux, M. J. (2010). Home Survival in Wildfire-Prone Areas: Building Materials and Design Considerations (ANR Publication 8393). University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources.

evidenceSupport.summary: The core points are strongly and independently supported by wildland–urban interface research: ember attack drives the majority of structure losses (Cohen, 2000; Caton et al., 2017), and combustible building components including decks and subfloors are key ignition pathways while non-combustible materials reduce ignition (Hakes et al., 2017; Quarles et al., 2010). The ‘most combustible element governs’ framing follows from this. The specific banding by attack level and the cut-points are reasoned practitioner judgement, and the actual attack level comes from a bushfire assessment, so treat the bands as a prompt.

relatedEntries.note: The companion fire entry: that one is about internal structural fire resistance (the rating a steel beam must hold in a fire inside the building); this one is about external bushfire attack on the substructure.

items.name: Fire protection cost of steel beams in concrete floors

data.question: Will a steel beam in a concrete floor still pay once its fire protection is costed?
data.parameter: The fire rating required of the beam, and how much applied protection bare steel needs to reach it.
data.background:
A concrete floor does not force concrete beams beneath it. Steel beams are often dropped into a concrete floor for good reasons: they avoid forming and bracing a concrete downstand, they let a slender (often post-tensioned) slab bear on a shallow support, and they erect dry and out of sequence so the deck lands on a beam that is already up. The trouble is that one consequence of that choice is easy to leave out of the comparison — fire.

Structural steel loses strength quickly with temperature. It retains only about 60% of its ambient yield at around 550 °C and falls away sharply above that, so a fully stressed unprotected member can reach a critical temperature within minutes of fire exposure. To achieve a required fire rating, bare steel therefore needs an applied protection system — intumescent coating, board encasement, or sprayed cementitious or vermiculite — and each one adds a trade, a thickness, an inspection regime and sometimes a recoating liability over the building’s life. Concrete gets its fire resistance for nothing in comparison: it heats slowly, and the cover already provided for durability keeps the bars below their critical temperature for the rated period. The fire resistance of a concrete beam is intrinsic to the section as drawn; the fire resistance of a steel beam is an add-on that has to be bought.

So the saving a steel beam offers is a gross saving. The formwork and depth it removes are real, but the net saving has to subtract the fire protection it then needs. Where the required rating is low or absent, steel wins comfortably. Where the rating is high, that subtraction can turn negative and quietly cancel the very reason the beam was made steel.

The awareness gap is structural. The required rating almost never originates on the structural set — it comes from the building’s fire strategy and lives on the architectural or fire-and-life-safety drawings, in a fire engineer’s report, or in the building surveyor’s assessment. A structural engineer can therefore pick a steel beam without the rating in front of them, design it structurally, and only find downstream that it needs protection — by which point the formwork saving is already spent. Two levers move the rating before it is fixed: the building’s construction type and whether it is sprinkler-protected, since a sprinkler concession can reduce or remove the rating entirely and change the whole calculation.

The choice is not simply steel versus concrete. Concrete-encasing the section, or using a concrete-filled hollow section — where the tube does double duty as permanent formwork and as protection — keeps part of the steel programme benefit while letting concrete carry the fire. This entry is a concept-stage prompt to weigh those options with the rating known; setting the required rating and the fire design itself remain the fire engineer’s and building surveyor’s work.

thresholds.guidance: The element carries no required fire rating — for example some open-deck carparks, certain single-storey or low-rise cases, or a sprinkler or performance concession that removes it. Bare steel needs no protection, so the formwork- and depth-saving rationale stands on its own. Confirm the no-rating position against the fire strategy, then specify the steel beam with confidence.

A modest rating applies (around 30–60 minutes). Bare steel needs only light applied protection, often a thin-film intumescent. The formwork saving probably still nets positive, but price the protection — including the inspection and any recoating life — and confirm it before assuming steel is the cheaper route.

A moderate rating applies (around 90 minutes). Protection becomes substantial — thicker intumescent, board or sprayed systems — adding a trade, thickness, inspection and cost that eat into the formwork saving. A concrete beam reaches this rating largely through the cover it already has, and concrete-encased or concrete-filled steel is a hybrid worth pricing. Compare the net cost, not the gross.

A high rating applies (around 120 minutes or more). Heavy applied protection is needed on every steel member. Concrete attains this rating inherently through its cover, so bare structural steel is usually the dearer and more complex route once fire is counted, and specifying it to save formwork is likely a false economy. Confirm the rating from the fire strategy and reprice before committing: keep the beam concrete, encase or fill the steel, or proceed with steel only if a clear reason survives the protection cost.

alternatives.title: Get the required rating before fixing the material
alternatives.description: At concept stage, obtain the beam’s required fire rating from the project fire strategy (or the fire engineer or building surveyor) rather than designing the member structurally and discovering the rating later. The material choice should be made with the rating in front of you, because it is the rating that decides whether steel still pays.

alternatives.title: Compare net cost, not gross
alternatives.description: The formwork and depth saved by a steel beam is a gross figure. Subtract the applied fire protection — material, the extra trade, thickness, inspection and any recoating over the building life — before concluding steel is cheaper. The comparison that matters is net of protection.

alternatives.title: Carry the rating onto the structural drawings
alternatives.description: The required fire rating usually lives on the architectural or fire drawings, not the structural set. Note it against the member on the structural drawings so the requirement travels with the beam to the contractor and certifier, rather than being discovered late or missed altogether.

alternatives.title: Consider encasing or filling the steel
alternatives.description: Where a steel section is wanted but the rating is onerous, concrete-encasing the section or using a concrete-filled hollow section lets the concrete provide the fire resistance. A filled tube doubles as both permanent formwork and protection, capturing part of the steel programme benefit while avoiding a separate applied-protection system.

alternatives.title: Keep the beam concrete where the rating is high
alternatives.description: Where a substantial rating applies and no clear reason ties the member to steel, a concrete beam achieves the rating through its cover with no added protection system. Reverting to concrete is often the simpler and cheaper answer once fire is counted.

alternatives.title: Use the sprinkler and construction-type lever knowingly
alternatives.description: Whether the element needs a rating, and how high, depends on the building’s construction type and whether it is sprinkler-protected. Where the fire strategy is still open, a sprinkler concession may reduce or remove the rating and make the steel option viable; treat this as a coordinated decision with the fire strategy, not an assumption.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies where a steel beam is being considered within a concrete floor — typically to avoid beam formwork or to keep a slender (often post-tensioned) slab — and the beam may carry a required fire rating. It is a concept-stage material-choice prompt. It does not apply where the element has no fire-resistance requirement, and it does not set the required rating or perform the fire design, which belong to the fire engineer and building surveyor.

checks.title: Where the rating comes from
checks.description: The required rating is set by the building’s fire strategy (construction type, storeys, sprinklers), not by the structural design. Get it from the fire-and-life-safety documentation or the fire engineer before deciding the material.

checks.title: Whether the building is sprinkler-protected
checks.description: Sprinkler protection and the construction type can lower or remove the required rating. The same beam can be unprotected steel in one fire strategy and need substantial protection in another.

checks.title: Gross saving vs net saving
checks.description: The formwork and depth a steel beam saves is real, but the applied protection it then needs is a cost in the other direction. Judge the choice on the net position, including inspection and any recoating liability.

checks.title: Hybrid options
checks.description: Concrete-encasing the section, or a concrete-filled hollow section, can keep a steel member while letting concrete provide the fire resistance. These sit between bare steel and a concrete beam and are worth pricing.

checks.title: Protection has a maintenance tail
checks.description: Some applied protection systems carry an inspection and recoating obligation over the building life. This is a whole-of-life cost the bare-steel option imports and the concrete option does not.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a concept-stage review prompt, not a rule against steel beams. Steel in a concrete floor is often the right answer where the rating is low or absent; the flag marks the case where an unexamined ‘steel saves formwork’ default imports a fire-protection cost that can outweigh the saving. The required rating and the fire design remain the responsibility of the fire engineer and building surveyor under the relevant requirements.

data.evidence:
Why bare steel needs added protection. Structural steel loses strength rapidly as it heats: it retains roughly 60% of its ambient yield strength at about 550 °C and continues to fall steeply above that, so a fully stressed unprotected member can reach a critical temperature within minutes of fire exposure (Buchanan & Abu, 2017; Wang, 2002). Achieving a fire-resistance rating therefore requires an applied protection system — intumescent coating, board encasement, or sprayed cementitious or mineral protection — each of which is an additional passive-fire system carrying its own cost, installed thickness, inspection and, for some systems, a recoating obligation over the building life (Purkiss & Li, 2014; Wang, 2002).

Why concrete carries fire intrinsically. Concrete has low thermal conductivity and high thermal mass, so a section heats slowly and the reinforcement is shielded by its cover; the cover provided for durability also delays the bars reaching their critical temperature, allowing reinforced concrete members to achieve substantial fire-resistance periods without an applied protection system (Kodur, 2014; Buchanan & Abu, 2017). The fire resistance is a property of the section as detailed rather than an added trade.

Why the entry flags a net-versus-gross trap. Because the steel beam removes formwork and depth but then imports an applied protection cost, the constructability comparison must be made net of that protection, not on the formwork saving alone. The bands scale with how much protection bare steel needs to reach the required rating; the minute cut-points are reasoned practitioner judgement aligned with common rating tiers rather than measured thresholds, and the required rating itself is an output of the project fire strategy, not of this entry.

References
Buchanan, A. H., & Abu, A. K. (2017). Structural Design for Fire Safety (2nd ed.). John Wiley & Sons.
Kodur, V. (2014). Properties of concrete at elevated temperatures. ISRN Civil Engineering, 2014, Article 468510.
Purkiss, J. A., & Li, L.-Y. (2014). Fire Safety Engineering Design of Structures (3rd ed.). CRC Press.
Wang, Y. C. (2002). Steel and Composite Structures: Behaviour and Design for Fire Safety. Spon Press.

evidenceSupport.summary: The underlying physics is textbook and internationally established: steel loses strength rapidly with temperature and needs an applied protection system to achieve a rating, while concrete achieves fire resistance intrinsically through cover and low conductivity (Buchanan & Abu, 2017; Wang, 2002; Kodur, 2014; Purkiss & Li, 2014). The net-versus-gross framing follows directly. The minute cut-points between bands are reasoned practitioner judgement aligned with common rating tiers rather than measured thresholds, and the required rating is an output of the project fire strategy, so treat the bands as a prompt and refine the boundaries with project cost examples.

relatedEntries.note: Companion material-choice prompt: that entry weighs vertical members (columns and walls) on formwork repetition; this one weighs floor beams on fire-protection cost.

The other fire entry, and a useful contrast: this one is about internal structural fire resistance (the rating a beam must hold from a fire inside the building); that one is about external bushfire attack on the substructure.

dimensions.name: Detailing
dimensions.description: Standardisation, repetition, and simplification of element detailing
generalGuidance: Prefer consistent reinforcement layouts across top and bottom steel where structurally appropriate - this simplifies ordering, reduces placement errors, and supports safety
Simplify formwork geometry wherever possible - regular layouts and repetition improve productivity and reduce cost
Evaluate whether complex geometry is structurally necessary - simpler details that achieve comparable performance are easier to build and less prone to error

items.name: Suspended slab level changes (folds)

data.question: When a design requires a change in suspended slab levels, is monolithic forming or an alternative method (blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours) the more constructable approach?
data.parameter: Fold depth in suspended slab (mm)
data.background: A change in suspended slab level can be achieved by forming the slab as a monolithic profile (a continuous concrete fold) or by separating the levels using blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours. Before sizing a fold at all, the designer should consider whether the level change can be eliminated entirely by thickening the slab to a flat soffit; for small setdowns (e.g. ≤ 50 mm) this is often the simpler construction outcome. Where a fold is genuinely required, the default constructability-aware approach is generally to use an alternative method rather than monolithic forming, because alternatives use standard trades, standard formwork and conventional construction sequences, while a monolithic fold requires custom edge formwork, additional bracing and propping, and a forming approach that is not part of normal slab construction. The amount of labour required to form a vertical face increases with the depth of that face (Jarkas, 2010), so a monolithic fold becomes progressively more difficult and labour-intensive as depth increases. The thresholds below indicate how strongly alternatives should be preferred at different fold depths, based on formworker feedback about where monolithic forming becomes problematic. Waterproofing is the most common objection to separate pours, but this can generally be managed using a waterstop and standard cold joint detailing and is not usually a reason to abandon the alternative approach.

thresholds.guidance: Monolithic forming can generally be accommodated by standard formwork systems at this depth. Consistent with 3-5 stair risers within a structure. Alternatives (blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours) are still usually the simpler approach because they use standard trades and standard formwork, but monolithic forming remains a reasonable option at this depth where there is a specific reason for it (e.g. architectural finish, watertight element, or a short isolated fold).

Monolithic forming may be achievable but formwork complexity, bracing and labour increase substantially within this range. Alternatives should be strongly preferred unless there is a specific reason for monolithic forming. Where monolithic forming is retained, the fold length, pour volume and formwork access should be reviewed with the contractor before construction.

Monolithic forming is likely impractical using standard formwork systems. A real project example required a 1050 mm fold in a suspended RC slab. During construction, the detail was found to be impractical due to the difficulty and cost of forming with adequate bracing. The design was revised to incorporate blockwork and separate concrete pours. Alternatives should be adopted at this depth unless a specific project condition makes monolithic forming the only viable option, and even then the construction approach should be confirmed with the contractor at the design stage.

alternatives.title: Use blockwork infill with separate concrete pours
alternatives.description: Form the level change using blockwork or masonry infill, then pour the concrete elements separately using conventional formwork. This is generally the default constructability-aware approach because it uses standard trades and standard formwork rather than custom edge formwork and bracing. Where the joint needs to be watertight, this can be detailed using a waterstop and appropriate cold joint treatment. Check structural load transfer, jointing, durability and finish requirements before adopting this option. Document this alongside the monolithic detail on the drawing, not as a replacement for it — the contractor selects the method on site based on available trades and formwork, and notifies the engineer prior to construction. Both details should be fully detailed (reinforcement, cold joint treatment, waterstop where required) so the structural design covers whichever path is built.

alternatives.title: Use permanent formwork (e.g. Dincel) with separate pours
alternatives.description: Use a permanent formwork system (such as Dincel or similar) to form the vertical element of the level change, then pour the slabs separately using conventional formwork. This can be particularly useful where access for stripping is limited or where a stable, self-supporting vertical formwork is preferred. The system should be checked for structural suitability, fire and durability requirements, finish expectations and compatibility with the surrounding concrete detail. Document this alongside the monolithic detail on the drawing, not as a replacement for it — the contractor selects the method on site based on available trades and formwork, and notifies the engineer prior to construction. Both details should be fully detailed (reinforcement, cold joint treatment, waterstop where required) so the structural design covers whichever path is built.

alternatives.title: Redesign to reduce or eliminate the level change
alternatives.description: Review whether the fold depth can be reduced, relocated or removed while still satisfying the architectural, structural and serviceability requirements. Simplifying the slab profile is often the most effective constructability improvement because it reduces custom formwork, reinforcement complexity, sequencing issues and construction risk at the source.

alternatives.title: Retain monolithic forming only where specifically justified
alternatives.description: Monolithic forming may still be appropriate where the fold is short and isolated, where a continuous unbroken concrete face is required for architectural reasons, where the element must be watertight and a joint introduces unacceptable risk, or where the structural condition is one in which a cold joint is not appropriate. Where monolithic forming is retained, confirm the construction method with the contractor at the design stage rather than assuming it can be built.

data.evidence:
This entry is supported by formwork literature, industry consultation and practical project experience.

Formwork literature. Jarkas (2010) measures labour productivity for edge formwork as a function of vertical face depth, confirming that the labour required to form a vertical face increases with depth.

Industry consultation. On-site consultation with formwork subcontractors and a building contractor identified monolithic folds above approximately 600 mm as becoming progressively more difficult to form using standard panel systems, with blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours the more common construction outcome.

Practical project experience. Two project examples support the entry. A 1050 mm fold in a suspended RC slab was found impractical to form monolithically during construction and was revised to blockwork with separate pours. A drop edge beam — a vertical concrete element subject to the same forming choice — documented both monolithic and blockwork alternatives on the same drawing. The contractor used both on site across different sections of the element. This demonstrates that documenting both methods is best practice — it allowed the contractor to use whichever suited each section.

References
Jarkas, A. M. (2010). Analysis and measurement of buildability factors affecting edge formwork labour productivity. Journal of Engineering Science and Technology Review, 3(1), 142–150.

examples.caption: Original detail: 1050 mm monolithic fold in suspended RC slab. Found to be impractical during construction due to difficulty and cost of forming with adequate bracing using conventional formwork.
Revised detail: blockwork infill with separate concrete pours. Achieves the required change in slab level using standard formwork and conventional construction methods.
Drop edge beam with monolithic (left) and blockwork (right) shown on the same drawing. Contractor used both on site.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to suspended slabs and similar reinforced concrete elements where a vertical level change is being designed, whether to be formed monolithically as a continuous concrete profile or by alternative methods such as blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours.

checks.title: Consider eliminating the fold before sizing it
checks.description: The simplest constructability outcome is often no fold at all. For small setdowns (e.g. ≤ 50 mm) it is generally easier and cheaper to thicken the slab on the higher side and keep a flat soffit than to introduce a fold and form a vertical face. The extra concrete volume is usually small relative to the construction cost of forming, bracing and finishing a fold, and a flat soffit removes the constructability question entirely. For larger setdowns the trade-off shifts as the extra concrete volume becomes more significant and a separated construction approach (blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours) is usually more appropriate. The designer needs to make a call based on the area, depth and project context; the point is that eliminating the level change should be considered before choosing how to form it, not after.

checks.title: Waterproofing is not usually a deal-breaker
checks.description: The most common objection to using separate pours is waterproofing of the cold joint between the two pours. This can generally be managed using a waterstop (PVC, hydrophilic or similar) and appropriate cold joint detailing, and is not usually a reason to retain monolithic forming. Waterproofing should be detailed and specified as part of the alternative, not used as a default justification for monolithic forming.

checks.title: Fold length and pour volume
checks.description: A short local fold is not the same construction problem as a long continuous fold. A deep fold over a few lineal metres may still be manageable monolithically, while the same depth over a long length is much more likely to require an alternative. The construction effort scales with the area of vertical formwork, not just the depth.

checks.title: Function of the level change
checks.description: The appropriate response depends on why the level change exists. Architectural set-downs, structural steps, waterproofing zones, fire or acoustic separation, retained fill and support for other elements may each require different detailing. In some cases a continuous unbroken concrete face is required for architectural or watertightness reasons, and monolithic forming may be justified despite the additional construction effort.

checks.title: Whether the alternative introduces disproportionate complexity
checks.description: Blockwork, permanent formwork or separate pours can introduce extra trades, additional concrete pumps, waterproofing detailing or sequencing constraints. In most cases these are simpler to manage than custom monolithic forming, but the alternative should still be assessed against the full construction sequence rather than assumed to always be simpler.

checks.title: Formwork system and contractor capability
checks.description: The thresholds reflect conventional formwork and standard contractor capability. Specialist formwork systems, highly repetitive geometry, experienced formwork crews or project-specific access arrangements may change what is practical, and the construction method should be confirmed with the contractor at the design stage.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a prompt to consider alternatives, not as a rule that monolithic forming above a particular depth is automatically unacceptable. A monolithic fold may still be the most practical solution where there is a specific reason for it; the framework's intent is to make sure that monolithic forming is a deliberate decision rather than an unexamined default.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by formwork productivity literature, contractor consultation, and real project examples where monolithic folds were revised or alternatives were documented.

relatedEntries.note: All involve changes in slab level (folds, setdowns, falls) and the geometry the following trades must build and waterproof against.
All involve changes in slab level (folds, setdowns, falls) and the geometry the following trades must build and waterproof against.

items.name: Flat soffit detailing at minor slab setdowns

data.question: When a slab needs a top setdown (e.g. for wet areas), should the soffit be folded to follow it, or should the slab be poured to the higher level with the top stepped down to keep the soffit flat?
data.parameter: Top setdown depth (mm)
data.background:
Top setdowns are often required for wet areas, balconies, falls, thresholds or waterproofing build-ups. The setdown itself is required and is not the issue. The constructability question is what happens to the soffit. If the slab is poured at the setdown thickness throughout and stepped up around it, the soffit folds to follow the setdown, introducing additional formwork, bracing, reinforcement variation and inspection. The alternative is to pour the slab up to the higher level and step the top down over the setdown zone, so the setdown zone keeps its required thickness while the surrounding slab is simply a little thicker and the soffit stays flat.

For example, a slab that needs to be 200 thick with four wet areas can be poured 250 throughout, with the top stepped down 50 over the wet areas. The wet areas are still 200 where it matters, the rest of the slab is 250, and the soffit is dead flat. The extra concrete in the surrounding zone is not a penalty to be justified — it gives a flat soffit and a thicker, simpler slab. Formwork dominates concrete-structure labour (roughly 49% versus roughly 11% for concrete operations — Löfgren et al., 2001), so keeping the soffit flat is generally worth the extra concrete. As the setdown depth grows, the extra concrete needed to keep the soffit flat across the whole slab grows with it, and at some point following the fold makes more sense. There is no fixed number for where that point sits — it depends on the slab.

thresholds.guidance: Pour the slab up to the higher level and step the top down over the setdown zone, keeping the soffit flat. The setdown zone keeps its required thickness; the surrounding slab is simply a little thicker. At this depth the extra concrete is minor and almost always worth it to avoid a soffit fold and keep a clean, flat formwork run.

Stepping the top down to keep the soffit flat is often still the better outcome, but the extra concrete is no longer trivial and starts to depend on the slab. A small slab with a reasonable setdown area is a clear case to keep the soffit flat; a large slab where the setdown covers only a small part of the area means a lot of extra concrete to flatten a small zone. Weigh the volume of extra concrete against the length of fold avoided.

The added depth needed to keep the soffit flat is now significant across the slab, and this is really a level change rather than a minor setdown. Refer to the Suspended slab level changes (folds) entry for guidance on monolithic forming, blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours.

alternatives.title: Pour to the higher level and step the top down
alternatives.description: Pour the slab up to the higher level and step the top down over the setdown zone so the soffit stays flat. The setdown zone keeps its required thickness; the surrounding slab is simply a little thicker. Best where the setdown is shallow and the slab is not so large that flattening adds an unreasonable amount of concrete.

alternatives.title: Show both options and let the contractor choose
alternatives.description: Where it is not obvious whether flattening or following the fold is better, the drawing can show the formed soffit fold and also permit stepping the top down, letting the contractor choose based on access, formwork setup, pour size and sequencing.

alternatives.title: Treat it as a level change for deeper setdowns
alternatives.description: Where the setdown is deep enough that keeping the soffit flat would add a significant amount of concrete across the slab, stop treating it as a minor setdown and treat it as a level change. Refer to the Suspended slab level changes (folds) entry for monolithic forming, separate pours, blockwork or permanent formwork.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to suspended reinforced concrete slabs and similar elements where a top setdown is required and the designer is choosing between following it with a soffit fold or stepping the top down to keep the soffit flat.

checks.title: The setdown stays required
checks.description: This entry is about soffit geometry only. Wet-area, balcony, threshold, falls and waterproofing setdowns remain exactly as designed. The question is only whether the soffit beneath them follows the setdown as a fold or stays flat with the top stepped down.

checks.title: It depends on the slab, not a ratio
checks.description: The decision turns on how much extra concrete keeping the soffit flat actually adds, compared with the length of fold avoided. That is an absolute comparison, not a percentage. A small slab with a reasonable setdown area is an easy case to keep flat; a large slab where the setdown covers only a small part of the area means a lot of extra concrete to flatten a small zone, and following the fold may be the better call even at a shallow depth. There is no fixed area or percentage threshold — it depends on the volume of extra concrete relative to the length of fold avoided.

applicability.note: Use the bands as a prompt, not a rule. Below 50 mm, keeping the soffit flat is almost always sensible. Between 50 and 100 mm it is a genuine judgement that turns on slab size. Above 100 mm, treat it as a level change. The thicker slab that comes from keeping the soffit flat is generally a benefit, not a penalty.

data.evidence:
Evidence is based on practical project experience and formwork/buildability literature.

Practical project experience. In the project example, the top setdown remained required, but the revised detail poured the slab up to the higher level and stepped the top down to maintain a flat soffit, simplifying formwork and reinforcement detailing. This demonstrates the intended constructability response: retain the functional top setdown, but avoid an unnecessary soffit fold where practical.

Formwork and buildability literature. Löfgren et al. (2001) support the broader principle that minimising material quantity does not necessarily minimise finished construction cost. They identify simplicity and repetition as key principles for efficient construction, and their man-hour breakdown attributes approximately 49% of concrete-structure work to formwork compared with approximately 11% to concrete operations. This is the basis for treating the extra concrete from a flat soffit as generally worthwhile against the formwork it avoids.

References
Löfgren, I., Gylltoft, K., & Kutti, T. (2001). In-situ concrete building - Innovations in Formwork. Department of Structural Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology / Thomas Concrete Group.

examples.caption: Original detail showing a required top setdown while maintaining constant slab thickness. This creates a matching soffit step/fold, adding local formwork and reinforcement detailing complexity.

Revised detail retaining the required top setdown but pouring the slab up to the higher level and stepping the top down. This maintains a flat soffit and simplifies formwork and reinforcement detailing.

supportingImages.title:
Wet-area setdowns shown on plan
A 250 slab with the wet areas (dotted) set down to 200 MIN. The surrounding slab is poured thicker so the wet-area low point still meets the plan thickness, exactly the case described in this entry.

Typical wet-area set-down detail (section)
The top surface steps down and falls to the outlet (up to 50 MAX) while the soffit stays flat. The slab is not less than the plan thickness at the low point.

Löfgren et al. (2001) - concrete structure man-hour breakdown
Approximate man-hour breakdown for a concrete structure, showing formwork as the dominant labour activity at 49%, compared with concrete operations at 11%. Supports reviewing minor soffit folds against flat-soffit alternatives.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by practical project examples and labour/productivity logic, but the thresholds depend on slab-specific judgement about extra concrete volume versus formwork simplification.

relatedEntries.note: All involve changes in slab level (folds, setdowns, falls) and the geometry the following trades must build and waterproof against.

items.name: Chamfered exposed external corners

data.question: On exposed external 90° corners of formed concrete, has a chamfer been specified, or has the sharp corner been left as an unconsidered default?
data.parameter: Treatment of exposed external 90° corners on formed concrete (chamfered or not chamfered).
data.background:
Wherever two formed concrete surfaces meet at an external 90° angle, the resulting corner (technically called an ‘arris’) is fragile. Sharp corners are vulnerable to chipping during formwork stripping, handling, transport, impact in service, and even later trades working nearby. A chipped corner is irregular, unintended and obvious, and looks worse than a deliberate chamfer would have. Sharp corners at body-contact height (column corners, upturn tops, exposed nibs near walkways) are also a contact-injury concern for trades, occupants and people on ladders.

The default constructability response is to specify a small chamfer (typically 15–25 mm) on exposed external corners using a triangular chamfer strip in the formwork. The size is not what matters; what matters is that a chamfer is specified at all, so the corner is deliberately formed rather than left sharp by default.

It is reasonable for an architect to remove a chamfer where they want a sharper visual line, or to use an alternative such as a fine silicone bead in the formwork to produce a very small radius. The point of this entry is not to prohibit either option, but to make sure the corner treatment is a deliberate decision — chamfer specified by the engineer as the default, removed only if the architect actively chooses to take on the appearance and chip-risk consequences.

The recommended way to document the chamfer is through a general drawing note that makes a chamfer the default for all external corners (e.g. ‘all external concrete corners chamfered 20 x 20 unless noted otherwise’), rather than calling a chamfer on every individual detail. Where the architect or designer has decided not to chamfer a specific corner, the location is marked ‘NCF’ (No Chamfer or Fillet) on the relevant detail. This keeps the drawing clean, makes the engineer’s default position unambiguous, and forces deviations to be deliberate, located and visible.

thresholds.guidance: A chamfer (typically 15–25 mm) is specified on exposed external 90° corners. The corner has been deliberately formed rather than left sharp by default. The size is not critical; specifying the chamfer at all is what matters.

Exposed external 90° corners are shown sharp without an explicit decision recorded. Sharp corners chip during stripping, handling and service, are more dangerous on contact, and a chipped sharp corner looks worse than a deliberate chamfer. The detail is not prohibited — an architect may consciously choose a sharp arris — but the choice should be deliberate and documented, not an oversight.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to exposed external 90° corners on formed reinforced concrete elements — columns, walls, upturns, nibs, beam edges, parapets and similar — where a chamfer strip can be incorporated into the formwork. It applies whether the corner is visible in service or hidden, because the chipping risk during stripping and handling exists regardless of whether the corner is later seen.

checks.title: External corners only
checks.description: This entry covers external 90° corners only. Re-entrant (internal) corners are a separate problem — they concentrate stress and crack under load or shrinkage, which is a structural issue addressed by trim reinforcement, not by chamfering.

checks.title: Inaccessible corners are the strongest case
checks.description: Unformed concrete corners can be rounded after the pour using an edging tool. Formed concrete corners cannot — once the formwork is stripped the corner is whatever the formwork gave it. The case for a chamfer is strongest where the corner is inaccessible for finishing after stripping, such as the bottom of a fold or the back of an upturn against an obstruction. There is no opportunity to correct a chipped sharp corner after the fact.

checks.title: Architect’s prerogative
checks.description: An architect may legitimately prefer a sharp arris for a specific visual effect, or specify a very small radius using a silicone bead in the formwork. Where this is the case, the chamfer can be removed from the engineering detail — but the decision should be recorded and the architect should accept the chip and contact-safety consequences as part of that choice.

checks.title: Contact-safety prominence
checks.description: The safety argument is strongest for corners at body-contact height (column corners up to about shoulder height, upturn tops, nibs at walkway level). It applies less to corners well above or below human reach, although chip resistance and aesthetics still apply.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a prompt to make the corner treatment a deliberate decision, not a rule against sharp arrises. Best practice is to specify a chamfer by default; removing it is allowed but should be a documented choice.

alternatives.title: Set the default with a general drawing note
alternatives.description: Make a chamfer the default for all exposed external corners through a single general note on the structural drawings (e.g. ‘all external concrete corners chamfered 20 x 20 unless noted otherwise’), rather than calling a chamfer on every individual detail. This is the cleanest and most reliable way to specify the default: the drawing stays uncluttered, and every external corner is covered unless explicitly excepted.

alternatives.title: Mark deliberate exceptions explicitly with NCF
alternatives.description: Where the architect or designer has decided not to chamfer a particular corner, mark that location with ‘NCF’ (No Chamfer or Fillet) on the relevant detail, and include NCF in the drawing’s abbreviation list. This makes the omission a deliberate, located, visible decision rather than an oversight, and shifts the chip-risk and contact-safety responsibility to the party making the aesthetic choice.

alternatives.title: Use a silicone bead or similar small-radius formwork detail where a chamfer is too prominent
alternatives.description: Where a 20 x 20 chamfer would be visually too large but a fully sharp arris is undesirable, a fine silicone bead or small-radius profile in the formwork can produce a controlled small chamfer. This is a workable compromise but should be detailed and specified explicitly, not assumed to be on the formwork by default.

data.evidence:
This entry is supported by Australian concrete joint guidance and drawing practice. The evidence is deliberately limited to sources that directly support the constructability point: formed sharp concrete edges should be deliberately treated, and chamfers/fillets can be documented without cluttering every detail.

Damage reason. CCAA Technical Note 63 Joints in Concrete Buildings includes a precast cladding joint detail with a chamfered front edge. The accompanying construction guidance states that a 12-mm chamfer is desirable on the front edge of the vertical joint to minimise damage. This supports the basic principle that an exposed formed concrete arris is vulnerable to damage and should be deliberately resolved where it is likely to be seen, handled, stripped or exposed to impact.

Documentation practice. RTA concrete detailing guidance treats chamfers and fillets as standard concrete detailing items. It states that standard chamfers and fillets are 20 x 20 mm, that they should not be shown on small-scale views or sections, and that they should be shown to correct size on views or sections at 1:10 or larger. The same guidance states that non-standard fillets greater than 20 x 20 mm should be shown in all instances and either called up or dimensioned. This supports the drawing approach used in the entry: the intent can be set by a general note for standard chamfers/fillets, while non-standard or omitted edge treatment should be specifically identified.

Project drawing example. A stamped bridge drawing set for the Bridge over Talyawalka Creek uses the same convention in its concrete notes: edges are to be chamfered 20 x 20 and re-entrant angles filleted 20 x 20 unless specified otherwise. The abbreviation list also defines NCF as no chamfer or fillet. This shows the convention being used in actual project documentation, not only in a drafting manual.

Constructability logic. The issue is strongest where the corner is formed inside the formwork, such as folds, nibs, upturns, recesses and vertical concrete edges. Unlike an accessible slab edge, these corners cannot be rounded later with an edging tool. If a chamfer is required, it must be specified before the pour and formed by the formwork or covered by a clear drawing note.

References
Cement Concrete & Aggregates Australia. (2004). Joints in Concrete Buildings (Technical Note 63). CCAA.
Roads and Traffic Authority of NSW. (2007). Structural Drafting and Detailing Manual: Concrete Detailing (Section 22, OTB005 Issue 1 Revision 0). RTA Bridge Engineering.
Metaline Engineering Group. (2022). Bridge over Talyawalka Creek at Tintinalogy Station: Technical drawings / engineering plans (Project No. 6283, issue for construction Rev G).
