The reason it lands in the structural lane, and not just as a coordination courtesy, is cover. A wet-area set-down is a local reduction in slab depth. Designed in from the start, the engineer sets cover, bar positions and effective depth at the recess correctly. Resolved late, the recess is cut into a slab that was detailed flat — the top steel was placed for the original surface, so lowering that surface strips cover off it, a durability problem and, at the limit, a capacity one — or a topping is built up instead, adding load the slab was never designed for and breaking the threshold level. Either way, the as-built section is no longer the as-designed section.

This is also where the framework’s purpose bites: get the first pass right so the work doesn’t come back during construction. A house almost always has an architect or a draftsperson on the drawings, but rarely a hydraulic engineer or a waterproofing specialist — so the gap is not that nobody draws it, but that nobody with waterproofing expertise is in the room at design stage. The engineer is well placed to flag that specialist input is needed before the pour. The alternative sequence is the costly one: issue the slab, the builder engages the waterproofer mid-construction, the waterproofer returns with the falls, set-down and upstand they need, and the engineer is reworking a design that is already on site. Treating this as a professional-duty matter rather than a code one matters: under the NSW Design and Building Practitioners Act, waterproofing is a regulated building element whose statutory duty of care cannot be delegated, so a generic ‘falls by others’ or ‘building envelope by others’ note does not make an unbuildable detail buildable, and does not discharge the responsibility for the substrate the membrane depends on.

thresholds.guidance: The set-downs, the falls (formed in the slab, or a coordinated screed depth), the upstand height and the termination room at doorways, penetrations and junctions are resolved in the structural design and coordinated with the architectural finished-floor levels, the drainage design and the waterproofing system. The set-down is accounted for in cover, bar positions and effective depth at the recess. The membrane has the room it needs, designed in before the pour, and nothing about the waterproofing forces a change to the slab later.

The geometry is broadly right, but one element is drawn without being reconciled — a set-down nominated but not dimensioned against the finished-floor build-up, a fall shown but not tied to the drainage outlet, or a threshold level not reconciled with the membrane turn-up. The risk is a late adjustment that eats into cover or head height. Reconcile the loose element before issue. Where there is no hydraulic engineer or waterproofing specialist on the job, confirm that specialist input is obtained before the pour, not after, so the coordination is closed while it is still cheap to close.

The geometry is not there: a flat or level slab with no fall and no termination room, or a ‘falls / building envelope by others’ note standing in for the detail. The membrane is being asked to perform on a surface it cannot work on, and resolving it later forces one of the bad fixes — grinding falls into the cured slab, a topping build-up that breaks the threshold and head height, or a recess cut into already-placed steel that strips cover. Resolve the geometry before the drawings are issued.

alternatives.title: Resolve the geometry in the structural set, don’t shed it
alternatives.description: Show the set-downs, falls and termination room, and account for the recess in cover and reinforcement. A ‘by others’ note doesn’t make an unbuildable detail buildable, and the duty over a regulated waterproofing element isn’t discharged by delegating the substrate it relies on.

alternatives.title: Get waterproofing input before the pour, not after
alternatives.description: Where there is no hydraulic engineer or waterproofing specialist on the job — common in residential — flag early that specialist input is needed, so the first pass carries the right geometry and the slab isn’t reworked on site.

alternatives.title: Coordinate the set-down against the finished-floor build-up
alternatives.description: A set-down is only right once it is dimensioned against the finishes, the drainage outlet and the membrane turn-up. Nominate it and reconcile it.

alternatives.title: Decide where the fall lives
alternatives.description: Formed in the slab, or in a screed or topping — and if it is a topping, confirm it does not re-create the threshold or head-height problem, or add load the slab wasn’t designed for.

data.evidence:
The consequence is recorded, not hypothetical. The NSW Building Commission’s 2023 strata defects research found waterproofing to be the single most prevalent serious defect, present in 42% of affected buildings — the most common serious defect category across the survey. Independent academic work points the same way: Johnston and Reid’s (2019) analysis of 212 building audit reports found that almost all buildings carried at least one defect, with water penetration among the most common. The hazard this entry addresses is one of the most frequently realised in the building stock, which is what gives the geometry decision its stakes.

The cause sits at the design-stage coordination seam, and the research names this exact geometry. The Victorian Building Authority’s 2024 water-ingress research identifies the causes of waterproofing failure as poor coordination and planning between practitioners from the initial design stage, together with an absent or unclear chain of responsibility — and where that coordination did not happen, the waterproofing systems failed. The regulator’s own catalogue of what goes wrong names incorrect set-downs, incorrect falls and ponding, and missing terminations as recurring failure points, and concludes that water ingress is rarely a single error but a weakness running from design and documentation through to construction. This is a design-stage coordination story, not a site-workmanship one — which is precisely the seam this entry asks the structural engineer to close.

The responsibility cannot be shed with a note (duty). Under the NSW Design and Building Practitioners Act 2020, waterproofing is a named regulated building element: a registered design practitioner must prepare and declare its design, and the Act’s statutory duty of care is non-delegable. The High Court confirmed in Pafburn Pty Ltd v The Owners – Strata Plan No 84674 (2024) that this duty cannot be discharged by delegating or entrusting the work to another competent person. That is the legal shape of why a generic ‘by others’ note does not resolve the substrate a membrane depends on — the reflex the framework is correcting. This is used as the governing principle; the entry’s specific claim is the constructability one, that the geometry has to be carried in the structural pour because that is the only place it can be formed.

Whose design is whose (scope). This entry stays in the structural lane deliberately. The waterproofing system is the architect’s or building designer’s responsibility — the NSW design-practitioner classes include fire, facade, drainage, mechanical and structural specialties but no waterproofing class, so in practice it falls to the architectural design practitioner, developed with builders and specialist waterproofing consultants. The falls and drainage are the hydraulic engineer’s. The structural engineer’s role, as Australian guidance on watertight concrete makes clear, is collaboration on the concrete body — important especially for exposed and graded slabs, where a successful result depends on the architect, the structural engineer and the hydraulic engineer working together. The entry asks the structural engineer to own and coordinate the substrate geometry, not to author the waterproofing.

What this entry does not claim. It does not claim the structural engineer designs the waterproofing system or the drainage — those sit with the architect, the waterproofing consultant and the hydraulic engineer. It puts no numbers on falls, set-down depth or termination heights; those come from the waterproofing design and the relevant standards, which the engineer already works to. It does not claim that good geometry waterproofs on its own — the system still has to be designed and installed — only that the membrane cannot work without the geometry. And it does not claim that Pafburn decides a structural engineer’s liability for a ‘by others’ note: Pafburn concerns a developer and head contractor and the proportionate-liability regime, and the position of downstream design consultants is less settled. It is used for the principle that a non-delegable duty over a regulated element is not discharged by entrusting it to others.

References
NSW Building Commission. Research on serious building defects in NSW strata communities (2023 Strata Defects Survey).
Johnston, N., & Reid, S. (2019). An examination of building defects in residential multi-owned properties. Deakin University & Griffith University.
Victorian Building Authority (2024). Water ingress in buildings: balconies, roofs and drainage — research insights, and the associated investigation of water leakage in concrete balconies.
Design and Building Practitioners Act 2020 (NSW), ss 36–39.
Pafburn Pty Ltd v The Owners – Strata Plan No 84674 [2024] HCA 49.

supportingImages.title:
Wet area set-down for waterproofing buildability

Wet areas usually need waterproofing, screed falls and tiling. A slab set-down leaves room for that build-up so the finished floor can fall to the waste without creating a step up. If no set-down is anticipated, the wet area build-up may create a step up or force slab cutting and rework.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to wet-area and external above-ground slabs and decks that carry a membrane — bathrooms, laundries, balconies, terraces, podiums, planters and plant decks — and, in passing, to bare drained slabs that simply have to shed water, such as garages and outdoor pavement, which need adequate falls cast in even without a membrane. It is about the slab surface geometry that decides whether water can be shed and a membrane can fall, turn up and terminate. It does not cover the wall construction joint (a separate entry), the waterproofing system design itself (architect or waterproofing consultant), or the drainage design (hydraulic engineer) — this entry is the substrate those designs depend on.

checks.title: Is the set-down in the structural design, with cover accounted for?
checks.description: Confirm the set-down recess is in the structural design and that cover, bar positions and effective depth are set for it — rather than a late recess cut into already-placed steel.

checks.title: Is the fall formed, or only assumed in a screed?
checks.description: Decide where the fall lives. If it is in a screed or topping, check it does not break the threshold or head height, or add load the slab was not designed for.

checks.title: Is there termination room at every junction?
checks.description: Check the turn-up height and clearances at every doorway, penetration and junction, not just across the field of the slab.

checks.title: Who is giving the waterproofing input, and when?
checks.description: If there is no hydraulic engineer or waterproofing specialist on the job, confirm the geometry input is obtained before the pour rather than after, so the design is right the first time.

evidenceSupport.summary: The call — resolve the set-downs and falls a membrane needs in the structural design and coordinate them, rather than defer them to a ‘by others’ note or a post-pour fix — is backed by regulator research that names these exact geometry failures and locates the cause at the design-stage coordination seam (VBA), by recorded consequence (waterproofing the most prevalent serious defect: NSW Building Commission, corroborated by Johnston & Reid), and by a non-delegable statutory duty over waterproofing as a regulated building element (Design and Building Practitioners Act), confirmed by the High Court in Pafburn as not dischargeable by entrusting the work to others. The main limitation is that allocating the substrate geometry to the structural engineer specifically is a constructability and coordination position rather than a settled legal allocation — the waterproofing design sits with the architect or consultant and the falls and drainage with the hydraulic engineer — and Pafburn is a developer/head-contractor proportionate-liability case, so its application to a structural engineer’s ‘by others’ note is reasoned rather than decided.

relatedEntries.note: Sibling waterproofing entry on a different surface: this entry covers the slab and deck surface geometry (falls, set-downs, upstands, membrane terminations); that one covers the wall construction joint. Coordinate so the slab-edge and wall-joint details meet cleanly and don’t overlap in scope.

The same wet-area top setdown seen from the soffit side: that entry weighs folding the soffit against keeping it flat and stepping the top. Here the setdown is about giving the membrane its drainage and termination room — consider the two together where one setdown drives both faces.

dimensions.name: Logistics
dimensions.description: Material and equipment availability, site access, and supply chain considerations
generalGuidance: Specify materials that are readily available from multiple suppliers - single-source dependency creates procurement risk and potential delays
Design within the capabilities of equipment that can realistically access and operate on the site
Consider site adaptability - materials and details that can be adjusted on site when conditions change reduce program risk

items.name: Stirrup bar size availability and site adaptability

data.question: Is the specified stirrup, ligature or tie size practical for ground footings, considering supplier availability, site adaptability and reinforcement cage stability?
data.parameter: Specified stirrup, ligature or tie bar size in ground footings or ground beams
data.background:
Stirrups, ligatures and ties in ground footings are often used not only for structural capacity, but also to hold longitudinal reinforcement in position during fixing, inspection, concrete placement and vibration. This is particularly important in groundworks, where footing or ground beam dimensions may change during construction because of excavation tolerance, founding conditions, pile position, set-out issues or cover requirements.

The bar sizes that are practically available for a footing depend on what concrete contractors can obtain through their normal reinforcement supply channels, not only on what is theoretically manufactured. In Australian reinforced concrete construction, N12 is widely used for longitudinal reinforcement, starter bars, supplementary reinforcement, perimeter bars and site-bent reinforcement. This makes N12 a practical option where reinforcement may need to be supplemented, adjusted or bent on site.

R6 and R10 can perform similar fitment roles in many footing applications, and an R10 tie at wider spacing can often substitute for an R6 tie at closer spacing where structurally acceptable. R10 is generally the more practical footing ligature size because it is more commonly used and more consistently available through reinforcement processors. R6 may be structurally adequate, but it is a narrower-use fitment size and can be more difficult to source. Where R6 is specified without an approved alternative, procurement issues may lead to substitution requests, construction delays or undocumented site changes.

thresholds.guidance: R10 at up to 900 mm centres is the default practical choice for footing ligatures, subject to structural requirements. N12 is preferred where the cage may need site-bent additions or adjustment to suit final excavated dimensions. Closer spacing improves cage rigidity and is encouraged where the cage is heavy or spans further between supports.

May be suitable where structurally required, but is less practical for site adjustment in groundworks. Avoid using N16 only to reduce the number of ties where R10 or N12 at closer spacing would provide a more practical, adaptable and stable cage.

May be structurally adequate in some footing applications, but should not be assumed to be universally available. R6 ligatures are stocked by some specialist reinforcement suppliers, but availability is inconsistent compared with R10. Where R6 is specified, provide an equivalent R10 option where structurally acceptable.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to reinforced concrete groundworks such as pad footings, strip footings, raft footings, ground beams and pile-supported footings where stirrups, ligatures or ties may need to be sourced, supplemented, adjusted, substituted or doubled up during construction.

checks.title: Groundworks dimensional variability
checks.description: This guidance is most relevant to groundworks, where final footing or ground beam dimensions may need to change during construction due to founding conditions, excavation tolerance, pile position, set-out issues or cover requirements. The same concern is less significant for formed above-ground elements where geometry is usually fixed and controlled before reinforcement is placed.

checks.title: Structural requirements remain controlling
checks.description: Tie size and spacing must first satisfy structural design requirements. This guidance does not justify increasing tie spacing beyond what is required for shear, torsion, confinement, crack control, bar support, minimum reinforcement, cover or any other structural requirement.

checks.title: Tie spacing and cage rigidity
checks.description: Tie spacing must first satisfy structural design requirements. Where the structural design permits wider spacing, footing tie spacing should generally not exceed 900 mm centres unless specifically justified. CCAA guidance for strip and raft footings identifies 600-900 mm centres as typical spacing for supports, ligatures and bar chairs, depending on reinforcement rigidity. Contractor consultation for this framework also indicated that footing cages can become noticeably less rigid above approximately 800 mm. The 900 mm value should therefore be treated as the practical upper limit for this guidance, not as a target spacing.

checks.title: Choosing between R6, R10 and N12
checks.description: Where ties are not governed by high structural demand and are mainly required for cage formation, support and reinforcement positioning, an R6 detail with a documented R10 equivalent is often the most practical approach. For example, documenting R6-600 or R10-900 gives the contractor a readily available alternative if R6 is difficult to source. N12 may also be used where it is already available on site, where additional site-bent reinforcement is needed, or where footing dimensions are likely to change, provided cover, bend dimensions and fit within the footing are maintained.

checks.title: Local supplier availability
checks.description: The availability ratings are based on Australian reinforcement supply conditions, contractor consultation, and supplier product information reviewed for Sydney, NSW. The suppliers reviewed were Mesh & Bar, ARC Reinforcing, Ausreo and Best Bar, selected because they are larger reinforcement-specific suppliers/processors with multiple branches, trade service centres or bar processing capability. Designers should confirm local supplier availability before applying the same ratings in other regions.

checks.title: Structural substitution must be checked
checks.description: Substituting R6 with R10, using N12 instead of R10, changing bar diameter, doubling up ligatures or changing tie spacing must still satisfy structural design requirements, cover, durability and detailing constraints.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a logistics and site-adaptability review prompt, not as a rule that R6, R10, N12 or N16 ties should never be specified. The preferred detail is the one that balances structural adequacy, supplier availability, site adaptability and reinforcement cage rigidity.

alternatives.title: Specify R6 with an R10 equivalent where structurally suitable
alternatives.description: Where R6 ties are structurally adequate, document a readily available R10 equivalent on the drawing, such as R6-600 or R10-900, where appropriate. This keeps the original lightweight detail while giving the contractor a practical alternative if R6 is difficult to source.

alternatives.title: Use N12 only where additional adaptability or structural capacity is needed
alternatives.description: N12 may be appropriate where the tie is structurally required, where site-bent supplementary reinforcement may be needed, or where footing dimensions are likely to change. Do not specify N12 by default if R6 or R10 ties provide an adequate and more practical footing cage.

alternatives.title: Avoid using N16 only to reduce tie numbers
alternatives.description: N16 ties may be appropriate where structurally required, but should not be used only to reduce the number of ligatures in groundworks. Where R10 or N12 at closer spacing would provide a more practical, adaptable and stable cage, that option should be preferred.

data.evidence:
Evidence is based on CCAA guidance for residential strip and raft footings, which identifies 600-900 mm centres as typical spacing for supports, ligatures and bar chairs depending on reinforcement rigidity, supplemented by contractor consultation undertaken for this framework. Contractor consultation indicates that R6 ligatures are commonly substituted with R10 during construction because R6 is less consistently available through normal reinforcement supply channels.

Supplier product information
Supplier product information was reviewed from four larger reinforcement-specific suppliers/processors that service Sydney, NSW construction work:

  • Mesh & Bar - major offices in Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane, with regional branches including Newcastle and Wollongong.
  • ARC Reinforcing - 40+ locations from Tasmania to Cairns, with Sydney-area branches including Revesby, Wetherill Park and Narellan.
  • Ausreo - 21 trade service centres across NSW, VIC and QLD, including a Sydney head office, prefabrication and bar processing facility at Wetherill Park.
  • Best Bar - 9 branches across major Australian cities, with its Sydney operation supplying reo bar, mesh and cut-and-bend services from two locations.

These suppliers were selected because they are larger reinforcement-specific suppliers with multiple branches, trade service centres or bar processing capability. This makes the review more relevant to normal Sydney/NSW trade procurement than a review of single-location suppliers, while still acknowledging that many smaller suppliers may operate locally with narrower stock ranges.

Supplier review findings
The reviewed supplier information shows three practical outcomes:

  • Two suppliers list pre-formed ligatures as stock-type products. ARC lists standard 6 mm and 10 mm ligatures in multiple rectangular sizes, while Ausreo lists R10 trench mesh ligatures across a broad range of sizes in bundles of 10.
  • Only one reviewed supplier clearly lists R6 pre-formed ligatures. ARC lists 6 mm ligatures, but Ausreo's reviewed ligature range is R10 only.
  • Two suppliers list plain round bar rather than pre-formed ligatures. Mesh & Bar lists R6, R10 and larger plain round bars as 6 m stock lengths, while Best Bar lists round rebar from R10 upward. In these cases ligatures would need to be scheduled, cut and bent rather than treated as an immediately available pre-formed ligature product.

Taken together, this supports the practical guidance that R6 should not be treated as universally available as a pre-formed ligature, and that an R10 equivalent should be documented where structurally acceptable. R6 may still be available from some specialist reinforcement suppliers, but availability depends on the supplier, branch, required size and whether the contractor is ordering stock ligatures or scheduling bars to be bent up.

Procurement context
Concrete contractors typically source reinforcement through established supplier relationships and normal trade procurement channels rather than sourcing each item from the entire theoretical market. Research on construction supplier selection supports this procurement logic. Nguyen et al. (2022) found that 50.4% of surveyed contractors selected material suppliers primarily on the basis of existing relationships, and that 73% of surveyed enterprises had specialised departments involved in supplier selection. Price, delivery time and product quality were the highest-rated supplier selection criteria. Although the study is not specific to Australian reinforcement supply, it supports the broader principle that practical material availability is governed by established procurement channels, supplier relationships, delivery time and product quality rather than theoretical manufacture alone.

References
Nguyen, Q. T., Nguyen, H. T. M., Nguyen, V. T., Pham, X. A., Dinh, T. H. & Nguyen, T. T. (2022). How do contractors select material suppliers for construction projects? Evidence from Vietnam. Archives of Civil Engineering, LXVIII(3), 273-288. https://doi.org/10.24425/ace.2022.141885

examples.caption: R6-600 stirrups specified with no alternative shown. If R6 cannot be sourced, the contractor must request a substitution, adding administrative time and potential delays.
R6-600 or R10-900 shown on the drawing. The contractor can source whichever is available, eliminating procurement delays and substitution requests.

supportingImages.title:
Nguyen et al. (2022) - factors influencing supplier selection
Existing relationships (50.4%) dominated supplier selection ahead of price quotations (40.2%) and formal analysis (9.4%). 73% of surveyed enterprises had a specialised procurement department.

Nguyen et al. (2022) - importance of selection criteria
Price (4.4), delivery time (4.2) and product quality (4.1) ranked highest on a 1-5 Likert scale across eight criteria.

Mesh & Bar - plain round bar stock
R6, R10 and larger plain round bars listed as 6 m stock lengths. Bar processing (cut, bend, tag) shown as a separate service.

ARC Reinforcing - R6 and R10 standard ligatures
Pre-formed R6 and R10 ligatures listed in multiple rectangular sizes, supplied in packs of 25.

Ausreo - R10 trench mesh ligatures
R10 trench mesh ligatures listed across a broad range of sizes in bundles of 10. R6 not shown in the reviewed range.

Best Bar - plain round bar stock
Round rebar listed from R10 upward in 6 m lengths. R6 not shown in the stock-size table.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by supplier availability review, contractor input, practical groundworks logic, and clear reinforcement cage stability and site-adaptability considerations.

relatedEntries.note: Both are about choosing bar sizes that are readily available and can be handled or adjusted on site.

items.name: Rock sockets vs. likely available drilling plant

data.question: Where a rock socket is required, can it realistically be drilled with the plant likely to be available on a job of this type and scale — or does the design quietly assume rock-drilling or piling equipment that a builder is unlikely to bring to site?
data.parameter: The class of drilling plant the required rock socket implies — standard excavator-mounted auger, dedicated rock auger, or piling rig / core barrel — assessed against the plant realistically available for a project of this type and scale. Socket depth, rock strength, hole diameter and the number of repeated sockets are read together to infer the plant class, not read as a depth figure on its own.
data.background:
Once a rock socket is confirmed as necessary (see the related Construction Methods entry), the constructability question is which class of drilling plant the socket implies, and whether that plant is realistically likely on a job of this type and scale. The same socket can be a non-issue on one project and a real problem on another — not because the depth changes, but because the plant the job brings changes. A deep socket on a large job that already has a piling rig on site is routine; a shallower but harder socket on a small job with only an excavator auger can be the bigger problem. So the rating is about the method, not the millimetre count.

A designer cannot confirm what plant a builder will bring — that is a construction decision. What a designer can do is design for the plant realistically likely for the project, and where the design needs more than that, make the requirement explicit rather than leaving it to be discovered at auger refusal.

On small and low-rise projects the likely plant is an excavator, mini-loader or skid-steer with an auger attachment. A standard earth auger suits soil and weak or fractured rock; a rock auger with replaceable teeth can take harder material but may not be part of a contractor's normal kit; a core barrel, casing or restricted-access piling rig is specialist plant. These are different equipment classes, and moving between them changes cost, programme and the access and spoil handling the site must provide.

Two things push a required socket from one plant class to the next: depth (with rock strength and hole diameter) and repetition. A shallow socket near the limit of a standard auger may be manageable, and a slightly deeper one may be tolerated on a job with only two or three piers, where mobilising specialist plant for a handful of holes is uneconomic. The same socket repeated across many piers is a different proposition — a small per-hole difficulty becomes a programme and cost problem, and a dedicated rock auger or piling rig starts to make sense. A builder is unlikely to bring a piling rig to a small job for a few piers, so a design that quietly assumes one risks being discovered only when the auger refuses.

Refusal itself is not always the founding surface. An auger may refuse on a boulder or floater above true bedrock, and sloping rock gives different founding depths across a site, so the actual socket depth — and the plant effort to reach it — may not be known from the drawing. This is why the required socket should be designed against likely plant and made explicit, not left as an assumption.

thresholds.guidance: No rock socket is required, or only a shallow key (around 150 mm or less) into weathered or fractured rock that a standard excavator-mounted auger with the correct teeth can reasonably achieve. This stays within the plant likely available on any site — small or large — subject to geotechnical inspection and founding requirements.

The socket is near the practical limit of a standard auger (around 150 mm), or needs a rock-auger attachment for a small number of piers (say two or three) that a contractor can still manage with an excavator-mounted machine. Do not assume this from the drawing. Design for the rock tooling actually likely on a job this size, and note the rock-drilling requirement so the builder can price and plan for it.

The work is beyond what a standard auger setup should be assumed to drill — a deeper socket (beyond around 200 mm), harder rock, or a larger-diameter hole — or a moderate socket (around 300 mm) is repeated across many piers (say ten or more) so that per-hole difficulty becomes a programme and cost issue. This points to a dedicated rock auger, higher-torque drive or larger machine. Make the required plant explicit before tender, and check it is realistic for the project's scale, access and spoil handling.

The required socket implies a restricted-access piling rig, core barrel or specialist drilling subcontractor — a deep socket, hard competent rock, or many repeated sockets. This is a constructability flag only where that plant is unlikely to be on the job: on a small or low-rise project with a handful of piers, a builder is unlikely to mobilise a piling rig, so the requirement risks being discovered at auger refusal. Where the project already justifies that plant — a larger or deep-foundation job with a piling rig on site — the same socket is routine work, not a concern, and this rating does not apply to you. Where it is not realistic for the job, reconsider the foundation system, reduce the socket requirement, or make the specialist-plant requirement explicit and check it against site access, working platform, headroom, spoil handling and cost before the footing layout is locked in.

alternatives.title: Design for the plant realistically likely on a job of this scale
alternatives.description: A designer cannot confirm the builder's plant, but can match the socket requirement to what is plausible for the project. On a small job with a few piers, assume an excavator-mounted auger or rock auger rather than a piling rig; do not specify a socket that quietly relies on specialist plant a small builder is unlikely to mobilise.

alternatives.title: Keep the socket within standard-auger reach where the design allows
alternatives.description: Where necessity permits, keep the socket shallow or found on cleaned rock so the work stays within the plant likely available. The shallower the required penetration, the more likely it can be achieved with ordinary site plant and the lower the risk of refusal-driven delay.

alternatives.title: Change the foundation system where likely plant cannot reach
alternatives.description: Where the plant likely to be available cannot achieve the required socket, review alternatives such as pads or strips founded on cleaned rock, ground beams spanning between fewer foundations, screw piles where suitable, or fewer larger piles in accessible positions — any geotechnically appropriate arrangement that suits the plant the project can realistically provide.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to bored piers, bored piles, post holes and small foundation excavations where the design requires drilling into rock, especially on residential and low-rise projects where the builder is likely to use an excavator-mounted auger rather than a dedicated piling rig.

checks.title: Plant likely for the project scale
checks.description: The designer cannot confirm the builder's plant, so design against what is realistically likely. A few piers on a small job suggests an excavator auger or rock auger; a large or deep-socket job may justify a piling rig. Match the socket requirement to the plausible plant rather than assuming the most capable equipment is available.

checks.title: Depth as a cue to plant class, not a rating in itself
checks.description: A shallow key into weathered or fractured rock may suit a standard auger; deeper penetration or hard competent rock points to a rock auger or specialist plant. Read the depth together with the geotechnical description and the plant likely for the job — the same depth can sit in different bands on a small job and a large one.

checks.title: Number and repetition of sockets
checks.description: A one-off shallow socket may be handled as a difficult excavation item. The same socket repeated across many piers turns a small per-hole difficulty into a programme and cost problem and can justify a dedicated rock auger or specialist plant.

checks.title: Rock type and strength
checks.description: Weathered or fractured sandstone is a different drilling problem from hard competent rock, basalt or very high-strength material. The plant required depends heavily on rock strength, not only socket depth.

checks.title: Hole diameter
checks.description: A shallow 300 mm hole is a different task from a 450 mm, 600 mm or larger bored pier. Larger diameters increase torque demand, spoil handling, verticality control and the likelihood that piling plant is needed.

checks.title: Access, working platform and spoil handling
checks.description: Rock augers, larger excavators and piling rigs need physical access, working room, suitable ground bearing, overhead clearance, and space for spoil, reinforcement and concrete placement. Restricted access does not necessarily mean standard excavator only — restricted-access piling rigs exist — but they must be planned, not assumed.

checks.title: Refusal, floaters and sloping rock
checks.description: Refusal is not always the founding surface. An auger may refuse on a boulder or floater above true bedrock, and sloping rock gives variable founding depths, so socket depth and the plant effort to reach it may not be knowable from the drawing.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a constructability prompt about the plant the socket implies. Locate the entry by the class of plant the required socket needs and whether that plant is realistic for a job of this type and scale — not by the depth figure on its own. A deep socket on a job that already justifies a piling rig is routine; a shallow-but-hard socket on a small job with only an excavator auger can be the bigger problem. The aim is to design the required socket against the plant realistically available and to make the requirement explicit — not to confirm the builder's plant, which the designer cannot do.

data.evidence:
Standard excavator/post-hole auger envelope. Hire-style post-hole attachments for mini-loaders and similar plant are marketed for shallow foundation and post-hole work, with depths around 2 m when an extension is used. This frames the ordinary small-site augering envelope against which a rock socket requirement should be checked.

Standard augers are not hard-rock tools. Specialist manufacturers distinguish rock augers from standard augers. Digga describes tapered rock augers as extreme rock-drilling tools that reach ground unachievable with a standard auger. A normal earth auger should not be assumed to drill meaningful sockets into hard rock.

Specialist piling uses different tooling. Bored-pile guidance treats augers, rock augers, buckets, casing and core barrels as separate tools selected for ground conditions. Soilmec identifies the core barrel as the suitable tool for very hard rock. Keller describes bored piles from around 450 mm diameter upward across a wide range of soil and rock. This supports the distinction between excavator augering and specialist bored-pile/rock-drilling practice.

Rock drilling is slower and costlier, and refusal is not always founding. NCHRP Synthesis 360 notes that drilling and excavation in rock is generally more expensive and time-consuming than in soil and may require specialised techniques, equipment and experience, and distinguishes rock augers, core barrels and downhole hammers as different tool classes. It also illustrates that an auger may refuse on a boulder above true bedrock and that sloping rock produces variable founding depth — so socket depth, and the plant to reach it, cannot be read off the drawing alone.

Band basis. The bands are keyed to plant class — standard auger, dedicated rock auger, and piling rig / core barrel — a distinction the equipment and piling references above support directly. The depth, rock-strength and pier-count cues used to place a socket into a plant class (around 150 mm for a standard auger, up to around 200 mm on a job with only a few piers, around 300 mm repeated across many piers pointing to a rock auger, and deeper or harder rock pointing to specialist plant) are practical design-stage prompts based on contractor and project knowledge, not code or manufacturer limits. They should be refined with regional contractor feedback and project examples.

References
Kennards Hire. Mini Loader Post Hole Attachment. Accessed 2026.
Digga Australia. Tapered Rock Augers. Accessed 2026.
Soilmec. Bored Piles. Accessed 2026.
Keller Australia. Bored Piles. Accessed 2026.
Turner, J. (2006). Rock-Socketed Shafts for Highway Structure Foundations. NCHRP Synthesis 360. Transportation Research Board.
Virnig Manufacturing. Auger Bits product page. Source for standard and rock auger comparison image.
ToolTuff Direct. Tool Tuff Earth Ogre Industrial-Duty Rock Auger product page. Source for rock auger cutting teeth image.

supportingImages.title:
Standard auger compared with rock auger
Standard and rock auger bits may fit the same general drive system, but they are intended for different ground conditions. Source: Virnig Manufacturing.

Why drilled depth is uncertain: refusal, floaters and sloping rock
An auger may refuse on a boulder above true bedrock, and sloping rock gives different founding depths across a site. Required socket depth — and the plant needed to achieve it — cannot be read off the drawing alone. Source: NCHRP Synthesis 360 (Turner, 2006).

Rock auger cutting teeth
Rock augers use specialised cutting heads and replaceable teeth for harder material. Source: ToolTuff Direct.

evidenceSupport.summary: The bands are keyed to plant class — standard auger, rock auger, core barrel and bored-pile rig — a distinction supported directly by equipment and piling references (Kennards, Digga, Soilmec, Keller) and by NCHRP practice guidance on rock-drilling cost and refusal, with product images of standard versus rock auger tooling. The depth, rock-strength and pier-count cues used to assign a socket to a plant class are practitioner/project judgement rather than published limits and should be refined with regional contractor feedback.

relatedEntries.note: Companion Construction Methods entry. Decide there whether the socket is necessary; this entry covers whether the required socket can be drilled with the plant likely available on the site.
Both concern whether the design quietly assumes plant, attachments or access that may not be part of the normal construction setup — check equipment and access together.
Both concern the constructability consequences of the chosen pier or pile detailing method.

items.name: Slab bar diameter and on-site bending equipment

data.question: Where slab reinforcement uses bars greater than 16 mm diameter, has the design recognised that any on-site bending of those bars is unlikely to be handled with ordinary low-cost site bending tools?
data.parameter: Nominal slab reinforcement diameter and implied on-site bending equipment class.
data.background:
The detailing entry linked to this one asks whether slab reinforcement can be kept to 12 mm or 16 mm bars where structurally practical. This logistics entry explains why that matters on site.

The issue is not cutting. Bars can often be cut on site using grinders, saws or other cutting methods. The real logistics change occurs when bars need to be bent. SRIA guidance places ordinary manual bending tools at up to about 16 mm diameter. Above that size, cold bending requires considerably more force and generally moves into powered or specialist bending equipment.

This matters because 16 mm bending equipment is still within normal site-tool territory. A simple manual lever cutter/bender is relatively low-cost and portable. A portable powered bender is more expensive, but the example reviewed is still only rated to 16 mm and is mainly useful where bars are already fixed or cast in, such as starters projecting from a wall that need to be bent down into a slab.

Once bending moves into 20 mm, 24 mm, 32 mm or larger bars, the equipment class changes sharply. The tools become larger, heavier, more expensive and much less likely to be casually available on a normal site. In practice, larger bars are far more likely to be bent off site by the reinforcement supplier or processor. That makes them much less adaptable once they arrive on site.

This entry is not saying that 20 mm or larger slab bars should be avoided where they are structurally required. The design engineer chooses bar diameter and spacing. The logistics prompt is that using larger bars has a consequence: if the detail later needs bending or local adaptation, the site may not have realistic equipment to do it. For general slab reinforcement, this is another reason to use 12 mm and 16 mm bars where structurally practical, and to keep larger bars localised to where they are genuinely needed.

thresholds.guidance: Slab reinforcement remains within the ordinary 16 mm site bending equipment range. These bars can be handled with simple manual lever benders or portable powered tools where proper bending equipment is used. This gives the reinforcement higher on-site adaptability and reduces reliance on off-site bending for minor changes.

Slab reinforcement uses bars greater than 16 mm diameter. These bars are outside the ordinary manual lever-bender range and, if they need to be bent on site, generally require powered or specialist bending equipment. This equipment is much more expensive and less likely to be casually available on site. Larger bars may still be structurally necessary, but they should be treated as low-adaptability site items and not used broadly where 12 mm or 16 mm bars would work.

alternatives.title: Keep general slab reinforcement within the 16 mm bending-equipment range where structurally practical
alternatives.description: Where 12 mm or 16 mm bars can achieve the design, prefer those sizes for general slab reinforcement. This keeps the detail within the ordinary site bending equipment class and avoids unnecessary reliance on off-site bending or specialist tools.

alternatives.title: Treat 20 mm and larger bars as low-adaptability site items
alternatives.description: Do not assume bars greater than 16 mm can be conveniently bent or adjusted on site. If 20 mm or larger bars are used, the design should assume they will usually arrive in their final required shape or be used straight, rather than relying on casual site bending.

alternatives.title: Keep larger bars localised to genuine demand
alternatives.description: Where 20 mm or larger bars are structurally required, keep them to the strips, bands, supports, transfer zones or local details where the demand exists. Use 12 mm or 16 mm bars elsewhere where structurally practical.

alternatives.title: Do not confuse cutting with bending
alternatives.description: The logistics problem is bending, not simply cutting. A site may be able to cut larger bars with grinders or saws, but bending those bars accurately and safely is a different task and usually requires a different class of equipment.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to slab reinforcement where the designer is choosing bar diameter and spacing, especially where a choice exists between smaller bars at closer spacing and larger bars at wider spacing.

checks.title: Relevant slab types
checks.description: This entry is most relevant to suspended slabs, raft slabs, podium slabs, balconies, setdowns, slab thickenings and local reinforcement zones where reinforcement may need to be coordinated with site conditions.

checks.title: Larger bars may still be structurally required
checks.description: This entry does not apply where larger bars are structurally required and are deliberately detailed for that purpose. It is not a rule against 20 mm, 24 mm, 32 mm or larger reinforcement.

checks.title: Logistics prompt, not a method statement
checks.description: This entry does not ask the design engineer to nominate the contractor’s bending tool, bending method or construction procedure. The engineer controls bar diameter and spacing. The contractor controls means and methods. The constructability issue is that the engineer’s bar-size choice can shift the work from ordinary site tooling to specialist or off-site bending.

checks.title: Bending is the issue, not cutting
checks.description: A site may still cut larger bars with grinders, saws or other cutting methods. The equipment-class jump discussed in this entry is about bending bars accurately and safely, not merely cutting them to length.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a logistics prompt about the equipment class implied by the bar diameter. Bars greater than 16 mm are not prohibited, but they should be treated as lower-adaptability site items unless suitable bending equipment or off-site fabrication has been allowed for by the construction team.

data.evidence:
SRIA guidance identifies 16 mm as the practical hinge for on-site bending. SRIA states that manual bending tools are recommended for bars up to about 16 mm diameter, with electric bending tools recommended for larger bars. SRIA Technical Note 4 also states that cold bending bars greater than 16 mm requires considerable force, that 12 mm and 16 mm bars should be bent cold, and that 20 mm and larger bars preferably require power-operated bending tools.

The reviewed tool examples support the logistics consequence of that hinge. A simple manual lever cutter/bender rated to 16 mm was shown at $509.95. A portable powered bender rated to 4–16 mm was shown at $1,798.50. However, moving into larger bar bending equipment caused a major cost and equipment-class jump: an industrial 6–32 mm electric bender/cutter was shown at $12,618.00, and a heavy-duty 6–46 mm bender was shown at $36,995.00 and required 400–415 V power.

This supports the entry’s constructability conclusion: 12 mm and 16 mm slab bars remain within ordinary site bending equipment territory, while 20 mm and larger bars should be treated as low-adaptability items that are more likely to require off-site bending or specialist equipment if bending is needed.

References
Steel Reinforcement Institute of Australia. (2016). Bending Reinforcement on Site (Issue No. 42/4). SRIA.
Steel Reinforcement Institute of Australia. (2007). Technical Note 4: Fabrication and Site Handling of Reinforcing Bars. SRIA.
Sydney Tools. (2026). HK Porter 0590RBJN 1320mm/52" Rebar Cutter & Bender. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.
Sydney Tools. (2026). RAPIDTOOL ERB-16 4mm–16mm Rebar Bender. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.
Sydney Tools. (2026). RAPIDTOOL CRBC-32A 6mm–32mm Electric Industrial Rebar Bender & Cutter. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.
Sydney Tools. (2026). RAPIDTOOL PRB-46X Premium 400–415V Heavy Duty 6–46mm Rebar Bender. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.

supportingImages.title:
Manual lever rebar cutter/bender — up to 16 mm
A simple lever-type cutter and bender is low-cost site equipment, shown here at $509.95. It represents the ordinary bending-tool class available for smaller bars. SRIA guidance places manual bending tools at up to about 16 mm diameter, which aligns with the practical 12/16 mm slab reinforcement range. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

Portable powered rebar bender — up to 16 mm
A portable powered bender is a more expensive equipment class, shown here at $1,798.50, but this example is still limited to 4–16 mm bars. It may be useful where bars are already fixed or cast in, such as 16 mm starters projecting from a wall that need to be bent into a slab. It does not solve the 20 mm and larger bar-bending issue. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

Industrial electric rebar bender/cutter — up to 32 mm
Once bending moves into the 20–32 mm range, the equipment class changes dramatically. This 6–32 mm electric industrial bender/cutter is shown at $12,618.00, far above ordinary 16 mm site tools. This supports treating 20 mm and larger bars as low-adaptability site items unless specialist equipment or off-site bending is planned. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

Heavy-duty rebar bender — up to 46 mm
Large-capacity bending equipment is a specialist plant item rather than normal site tooling. This 6–46 mm heavy-duty bender is shown at $36,995.00 and requires 400–415 V power. At this equipment class, the bar is effectively a planned or fabricated item rather than something expected to be conveniently adapted on site. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

evidenceSupport.summary: The 16 mm equipment hinge is directly supported by SRIA guidance, and the reviewed tool examples clearly show the cost and equipment-class jump from 16 mm tools to larger-bar bending equipment. The exact cost ratios are based on a small set of current supplier screenshots and should be treated as illustrative rather than universal.

relatedEntries.note: Companion Detailing entry. Decide there whether 20 mm and larger slab bars are genuinely required; this entry explains the equipment and availability consequences if those larger bars need to be bent on site.
Both concern how reinforcement size choices affect site adaptability, ordinary equipment availability and the likelihood of substitution or delay.

items.name: Piling plant on access-constrained sites

data.question: On a height- or width-constrained site, can common piling plant install the pile?
data.parameter: Whether the pile detail stays within common plant or needs specialist restricted-access plant.
data.background:
This entry only matters when there is a real constraint at the pile — a low eave, carport, undercroft, basement or services directly overhead, or a narrow side passage or gate. On an open site, ordinary plant reaches almost any normal pile and there is nothing here to weigh.

The distinction that does the work is operating constraint vs transit constraint. What rules plant in or out is the room the machine has where it drills. A constraint only on the route to an open pile pad is usually a transit problem, and transit problems are commonly solved with ordinary machines: an excavator's ROPS canopy folds or unbolts to duck under a low approach and is refitted to work, and many mini excavators retract their tracks to pass a tight gate then expand them to dig. So a low approach to an open backyard is not a headroom problem; piling under an existing slab is.

For common plant the operating headroom needed is set by auger extraction. A continuous-flight auger has to be drawn clear of the hole, so the clearance over the pile needs to be roughly the drilled depth plus about half a metre. A 1.5 m pile wants about 2 m of clearance; a 1.6 m hole under a 2.5 m ceiling is comfortable. Headroom for common augering therefore scales with depth — a shallow hole under a normal ceiling is fine, a deep hole under a low one is not.

The common, readily-available setup is a small excavator (~1.5–5 t) with a hire auger — the most prevalent earthmoving plant in the country, the kind almost any residential or light-commercial contractor already has or hires for a day rate. These machines are slim: the common range runs about 1–2 m wide — the popular 1.7 t class about 0.99 m, up to about 1.96 m at 5.5 t — with tracks that retract for transit. Sub-1 m micro excavators (down to about 0.75 m) exist and will pass a doorway, but they are less common and lower in auger torque, so 1–2 m is the safer common assumption. Even so, the common range is about as narrow as a restricted-access rig, which is why width alone rarely forces specialist plant. Off-the-shelf hire augers bore to roughly 2–3 m and up to ~450 mm diameter on these machines, reaching toward ~6 m on larger ones.

The trap on a tight site is that specialist restricted-access plant exists which will drill deep holes in ~2 m of headroom through a ~1 m gap — because it uses sectional tooling added and removed in short lengths, breaking the link between depth and headroom. So "is it possible?" is the wrong test; the answer is almost always yes. The right test is "is it common?" Once the detail needs that specialist plant, the work leaves the broad pool of contractors who turn up at a normal price and enters a small pool of specialist piling subcontractors with their own mobilisation. That is a real cost-and-programme step, and on a tight site it is a legitimate outcome to concede deliberately — sometimes there is no common-plant option (for example underpinning inside a basement) and the right answer is to design the specialist method in, not discover it on site.

thresholds.guidance: The pile location is workable for ordinary plant: passable to the pile (slim machine, ROPS folded or removed and refitted, tracks retracted as needed), and the clearance over the pile is at least roughly the drilled depth plus about half a metre, so a continuous-flight auger draws clear — for example about 2 m of clearance for a 1.5 m pile. A common small excavator (~1.5–5 t) with a hire auger reaches the detail: roughly ≤ 2–3 m deep, ≤ ~450 mm diameter. No specialist trade, no mobilisation premium; design the pile to sit here where you can.

The detail sits at the upper edge of common kit: it needs the next machine up with auger extensions (toward ~6 m or larger diameter), or a screw-pile torque head suited to low-spoil tight access. Extensions also raise the headroom the auger needs to clear, so re-check the clearance over the pile against the drilled depth. Still plant most contractors can source, but no longer the default — confirm the assumed machine, auger reach and diameter, the narrowest access along the route, and the headroom at the pile before relying on it.

The clearance over the pile is less than the drilled depth plus its extraction allowance — a deeper pile under a low ceiling, an undercroft or a basement — and/or depth or diameter is beyond common reach. A continuous-flight auger can no longer be drawn clear, so the detail is pushed into restricted-access plant: a low-headroom mini-piling rig or sectional CFA that works in ~2.0–2.6 m regardless of depth because its tooling is added and removed in sections. It is buildable, but it means a specialist subcontractor, mobilisation and a small contractor pool, growing with depth. Either pull the detail back toward common plant (shallower or smaller piers, closer spacing, screw piles off a common excavator), or, where there is no common-plant option, concede the specialist method and record it and its cost/programme basis at design stage.

workedExample.title: Worked example — common plant vs specialist plant on a tight site
workedExample.caveat: The figures are screening values from advertised hire and supplier envelopes, not measured limits. The "drilled depth plus about half a metre" headroom rule is a practical auger-extraction allowance; real reach and clearance vary with machine geometry, auger type and length, extension handling, spoil clearance and ground. Use the table to see where common kit ends and specialist kit begins, not as a fixed capability.
workedExample.headline: Common excavator augering needs headroom over the pile of about the drilled depth plus half a metre, because the continuous-flight auger must be drawn clear. A specialist restricted-access rig works in ~2.0–2.6 m regardless of depth, because its tooling is added and removed in sections — which is exactly what you are paying the specialist for.
tableHeaders: Specialist restricted-access kit (mini-piling rig / sectional CFA)
workedExample.howToUse:
Take a 1.5 m deep, 400 mm bored pier on a tight infill block, with a 2.5 m carport over the pile position. The clearance needed to draw the auger out is about 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0 m; the 2.5 m carport clears it, the pier is within ~≤ 3 m and ≤ 450 mm, and the machine reaches the pile by folding its ROPS to pass under the carport and retracting its tracks at the side gate. This sits squarely in the common column — the earthworks contractor already on site installs it with a day-rate excavator and auger, with no specialist trade and no mobilisation premium.

Now change one thing. Drop that same pier under a 1.8 m undercroft, or take its depth to 4 m: the clearance needed (2.0 m, or 4.5 m) now exceeds the headroom over the pile, a continuous-flight auger can no longer be drawn clear, and the job crosses into the specialist column — a restricted-access rig that drills the depth in ~2 m of headroom using sectional tooling, but brings a specialist subcontractor and mobilisation with it. The detail did not become impossible; it became uncommon, and that is the cost flag.

alternatives.title: Judge the constraint at the pile, not the route
alternatives.description: Operating headroom and working room where the machine drills decide the plant class. A low approach or a tight gate is usually transit — beaten by folding or removing the ROPS and refitting it, or retracting the tracks — and does not by itself force specialist plant.

alternatives.title: Size the headroom from the depth
alternatives.description: For common continuous-flight augering, the clearance over the pile needs to be about the drilled depth plus half a metre so the auger draws clear. Check the pile depth against the actual ceiling at the pile, not a fixed headroom number.

alternatives.title: Test "common", not "possible"
alternatives.description: Specialist plant makes almost anything buildable on a tight site, so feasibility is not the question. Ask whether the readily-available excavator-plus-auger setup covers the detail.

alternatives.title: Confirm the route and the work point separately
alternatives.description: Measure the narrowest gate or passage and the lowest clearance along the route (transit), and separately the headroom and working room at the pile (operating). Pass them on so the right plant is assumed.

alternatives.title: Design the specialist method in, or design it out
alternatives.description: Where the detail forces specialist plant, either pull it back toward common kit (shallower or smaller piers, closer spacing, screw piles off a torque head), or record the specialist assumption and its cost and programme consequence rather than leaving it to be found on site.

applicability.appliesWhere:
This entry applies to building foundations with a genuine constraint at the pile — low operating headroom (eaves, carports, undercrofts, basements, internal works, overhead services) or restricted working room and access (narrow side passages, gates, infill blocks) — where the designer is setting pile depth, diameter, type or layout. It is most relevant to residential, low-rise, small-commercial, extension and underpinning work.

It does not apply where there is no real constraint at the pile (ordinary plant reaches it, and a low approach to an open pad is transit rather than an operating limit), where the question is whether to move off bored piles at all (that is the Construction Methods entry, Bored concrete piles vs screw piles), or where the issue is the rock-specific escalation into rock-drilling plant (that is the rock-socket Logistics entry). It is not a contractor plant-selection instruction — it flags when a tight-site detail quietly requires plant beyond the common, readily-available kit.

checks.title: Is there a real constraint at the pile?
checks.description: If the headroom and working room at the pile are open and only the approach is tight, it is usually transit and solvable with common plant — stop here.

checks.title: Read the binding limit
checks.description: Establish the operating headroom and working room at the pile, and the narrowest access along the whole route to it.

checks.title: Size the detail against common plant
checks.description: Compare depth and diameter to what the common excavator-plus-auger setup reaches, and check the clearance over the pile against the drilled depth plus about half a metre.

checks.title: Treat specialist plant as a commitment, not a clearance
checks.description: Buildability via restricted-access plant means a specialist subcontractor is being designed in — do it knowingly, and record it.

data.evidence:
What common plant needs and reaches. The small-excavator-plus-auger setup is the most prevalent earthmoving plant in Australia: market reporting by Arizton puts compact excavators at close to half of all construction-equipment sales, with the ~1.5 t class the most popular and the ~1.8 t the single highest-selling model, and these machines routinely run auger attachments. Standard excavator hire augers, such as Eastern Plant Hire's, span roughly 200–1500 mm diameter and ~1.5–6 m depth, placing ordinary small-machine work at ~≤ 2–3 m and ~≤ 450 mm. The common machines are also slim, running about 1–2 m wide: the popular 1.7 t class is about 0.99 m and retracts its tracks before expanding to dig (Diggermate), up to about 1.96 m at 5.5 t (Express Hire, Kubota U55-4). Smaller micro excavators (about 0.75 m, passing a standard doorway) exist (Hire Express) but are less common and lower in auger torque, so 1–2 m is the safer assumption. Either way the common range is about as narrow as a restricted-access rig, so width alone rarely forces specialist plant. Because a continuous-flight auger must be drawn clear of the hole, the operating headroom needed scales with depth — roughly the drilled depth plus a short extraction allowance — so a shallow pile under a normal ceiling is fine while a deep one under a low ceiling is not. Transit height and width on the route are commonly engineerable rather than disqualifying: as For Construction Pros describes, ROPS canopies fold or unbolt for low clearance and are refitted to work, and retractable undercarriages narrow the track to pass tight access then expand to dig.

When you are into specialist plant. Restricted-access piling is a different, specialist tier that breaks the depth–headroom link with sectional tooling. Belpile (Australia) passes through narrow openings and works in ~2.0 m headroom using sectional CFA to install 250–450 mm piles 10–14 m deep — depth and low-headroom capability ordinary excavator augering cannot reach. The residential reality that sends a job there is well described by Anchorpile in the Australian market: side passages often as narrow as 900 mm, and a conventional rig standing 4–5 m tall cannot work under a 2 m eave; the practical step is to measure the narrowest point of the access route, including gates, and the minimum headroom, and pass them to the installer. Stepping into this tier means a specialist subcontractor and mobilisation rather than the general hire fleet — a real cost step, and on a tight site sometimes the only option, which is why it should be designed in deliberately.

References
Arizton. Australia Compact Construction Equipment Market. Accessed 2026.
Eastern Plant Hire. Excavator hydraulic augers (200–1500 mm; 1.5–6 m). Accessed 2026.
Diggermate. 1.7 t Mini Excavator (990 mm wide; tracks expand to 1240 mm). Accessed 2026.
Hire Express. 1 t Mini Excavator (~920 kg; passes through a standard doorway). Accessed 2026.
Express Hire. Kubota U55-4 5.5 t Excavator (machine width 1960 mm). Accessed 2026.
For Construction Pros. Tailor Compact Excavators for Indoor Construction Sites (foldable ROPS; retractable undercarriages). Accessed 2026.
Belpile. Limited Access / Restricted Headroom Piling (~2.0 m headroom; 250–450 mm; 10–14 m; sectional CFA). Accessed 2026.
Anchorpile SA. Restricted Access Sites: How Screw Piles Work When Space Is Tight (900 mm side passages; measure the access route). 2026.

supportingImages.title:
Piling plant headroom is part of the operating envelope

A small excavator-mounted auger may fit through the site access but still be unable to work if there is not enough headroom to raise and extract the auger. Check the full operating envelope before assuming the pile detail can be installed with typical small-site plant.

evidenceSupport.summary: The common-vs-specialist direction is well supported: the prevalence of the small-excavator baseline, its machine widths and auger reach, the restricted-access headroom and width figures, the transit workarounds (foldable ROPS, retractable tracks) and the specialist-trade cost step are all backed by market, hire-fleet and supplier sources. The numeric edges are screening values, not measured limits: the ~2–3 m and ~450 mm common-reach figures come from advertised hire envelopes, and the "drilled depth plus about half a metre" headroom rule is a practical extraction allowance rather than a tested threshold — real reach and clearance vary with machine geometry, auger type and length, extension handling, spoil clearance and ground. Treat the bands as a prompt for when a tight-site detail has probably left common plant and needs contractor confirmation or a deliberate specialist-plant assumption, not as a proven capability rule.

relatedEntries.note: That entry decides whether access, spoil or open-hole burden justifies moving off bored piles; this one, once a tight site is established, checks whether the detail stays within common plant. Screw piles install off a common excavator torque head, so they often keep a tight-site job in the common-plant tier.
The rock-specific escalation into rock-drilling plant. This entry is the general tight-site plant screen; that one is the worked rock sub-case.
Both turn on whether plant and workers can physically reach and work the element on a constrained site — check site access together.
If tight access pushes the design toward screw piles, check whether the selected system can be verified as built before committing.

items.name: Waffle pod depth availability

data.question: Is the specified waffle pod depth a standard stocked size for local NSW suppliers, or does it need made-to-order manufacture and lead time?
data.parameter: Specified waffle pod depth, assessed against the depths normally stocked by local/regional NSW pod suppliers.
data.background:
Waffle pods are EPS (polystyrene) void formers, 1090 mm square, made in a range of depths — roughly 145–150, 175, 225, 300 and 375 mm, with cut-to-size pods available. The depth a designer can draw on without programme risk is regional and stock-driven, not the full theoretical menu. A deeper pod forms a deeper rib and a stiffer slab, so the depth a slab needs rises with how reactive the ground is, and suppliers stock to match where most of the demand sits. The mid-range depths (225 and 300 mm) are the everyday sizes, held as standard by effectively every supplier, while the shallow (around 150 mm) and deep (375 mm) ends are made but harder to source: regionally variable, sometimes held only at certain branches, and in some places made-to-order. A depth specified at design stage because it suits the floor level or the slab-rigidity demand can become a programme problem if it cannot be sourced locally in time, because off-standard or custom depths bring made-to-order lead time, minimum-order quantities and cartage.

In Australia this depth-to-ground relationship is formalised: ground reactivity is assessed as a site classification (under AS 2870), and suppliers commonly label pod depths by the class they suit — for example a 225 mm pod for moderately reactive sites, 300 mm for highly reactive, and 375 mm for extremely reactive. These labels are a procurement convenience, not a design method, and the exact mapping varies between suppliers. The pod depth follows the engineer's slab design, not a depth read off a catalogue table. This entry is purely about whether the depth the design lands on can actually be sourced; it does not choose the depth and does not displace the structural design. It is the supply-chain counterpart to the depth choice weighed in the stiffened-raft-vs-waffle-pod entry: that entry asks which depth suits the site; this one asks whether that depth is on the shelf.

thresholds.guidance: The everyday mid-range depths, and both are easy to get in NSW — held as standard by effectively every supplier. They cover the common-to-moderately-reactive ground that most residential slabs sit on (in Australia, the moderately and highly reactive site classes), so specifying within this range carries the lowest lead-time risk. Confirm structural adequacy of the depth in the usual way — availability is not the design driver.

The shallow and deep ends of the range — made by many suppliers, but harder to get in NSW than the core sizes. The shallow pods (around 150 mm, including the less common 175 mm) suit lower-stiffness slabs, and the deep 375 mm pod suits the most reactive ground; neither is held by every branch, and 375 mm in particular is a standard stocked size in some regions but made-to-order in others. Confirm local branch stock and lead time before the depth is locked into the design, and check minimum-order and cartage effects on a small job.

Any off-menu or cut-to-size depth, or any depth a local supplier cannot provide within the programme. These are made-to-order: they carry manufacturing lead time, minimum-order quantities and cartage, and a single non-standard depth can hold up the slab. Confirm supply in writing, or where the structural design allows, redesign to a stocked depth (225 or 300 mm) before the design is fixed.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to lightly-to-moderately loaded residential and small-commercial ground slabs using EPS waffle pods, where a pod depth has been selected and the question is whether that depth can be sourced from local/regional suppliers without made-to-order lead time. It is most relevant to single- and two-storey housing and additions in NSW, where the slab design lands on a standard pod depth.

checks.title: Structural design remains controlling
checks.description: The pod depth must first satisfy the engineer's slab design — the rib depth and slab stiffness required for the ground reactivity on this site (in Australia, designed to AS 2870). This entry does not justify changing a pod depth to suit availability where the structural design requires a particular depth. Where it permits a choice, prefer the more readily stocked depth.

checks.title: Depth follows the engineer's design, not a marketing table
checks.description: Supplier literature often maps depth to the reactivity of the ground (and, in Australia, to the site class) as a convenience, but the mapping varies between suppliers and is not a design method. The depth comes from the engineer's slab design. Use the depth-to-ground labels only to understand why the mid-range depths are the stocked ones, not to select a pod.

checks.title: Confirm local branch stock and lead time
checks.description: Availability is by supplier and by branch, not by the theoretical national range. Before relying on a depth, confirm the specific local branch holds it, and the lead time if it does not. This matters most for the shallow (145–175 mm) and deep (375 mm) pods, which are the regionally variable depths.

checks.title: Cartage and minimum-order on small jobs
checks.description: EPS pods are bulky and light, so cartage and minimum-order quantities can dominate the cost of a small or off-standard order. A custom-cut or deep-pod order for a single dwelling can carry disproportionate delivery and minimum-quantity penalties compared with a standard 225 mm order.

checks.title: Regional availability
checks.description: The stock pattern described here is for NSW supply and was reviewed against NSW-servicing suppliers. The standard-vs-made-to-order split (especially for 375 mm and the shallow pods) differs between states. Confirm the local pattern before applying the same ratings elsewhere.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a procurement and programme review prompt, not as a rule about which pod depth is correct. The preferred depth is the one the structural design requires; where the design allows a choice, the more readily stocked depth reduces programme risk.

alternatives.title: Design to a stocked depth where the structural design allows
alternatives.description: Where the structural design permits more than one depth, prefer 225 mm or 300 mm — the core stocked depths — over a shallow or custom depth that may be made-to-order. This keeps the slab on standard, readily available pods without changing the structural intent.

alternatives.title: Confirm stock and lead time before the depth is locked
alternatives.description: For the regionally variable depths (145–175 mm and 375 mm), confirm the local branch holds the size, or the lead time if it must be manufactured, before the depth is fixed in the design and on the drawings. This converts a silent procurement risk into a known one early.

alternatives.title: Allow cut-to-size only with lead time and cartage priced in
alternatives.description: Custom-cut pods are available and may be unavoidable on a particular design, but they are a made-to-order product. Where they are used, allow for manufacturing lead time, minimum-order quantities and cartage in the programme and budget rather than treating them as an off-the-shelf item.

data.evidence:
Evidence is based on a review of waffle pod supplier product information for NSW supply, read together with the physical relationship between pod depth, rib stiffness and ground reactivity.

Supplier product information
Pod ranges were reviewed across suppliers that manufacture for or service NSW construction, selected because they publish their stocked depth range and, in several cases, the ground reactivity each depth suits:

  • Saddingtons (Newcastle / Broadmeadow, NSW) — lists 175, 225, 300 and 375 mm pods, 1090 mm square.
  • Hunter Pods (Hunter region, NSW) — lists 145 and 225 mm for moderately reactive sites, 300 mm for highly reactive and 375 mm for extremely reactive, with custom sizes and cut-to-size available.
  • Polyfoam — lists 225 mm as standard for moderately reactive sites, 300 mm for highly reactive and 375 mm for extremely reactive, with 375 mm noted as a standard size in Queensland but made-to-order in Tasmania — a clear illustration of the regional standard-vs-made-to-order split.
  • Bildavoid (technical specification) — lists 150, 225 and 300 mm as standard depths, noting that other depths may be available depending on location.
  • Unipod — maps 225 mm to moderately reactive sites, 300 mm to highly reactive and 375 mm to extremely reactive, with cut-to-size custom pods available.

Designers should treat the named suppliers as indicative of the NSW stock pattern rather than an exhaustive market review, and confirm the specific local branch range before relying on a depth.

Supplier review findings
The reviewed information shows a consistent pattern:

  • 225 mm and 300 mm are the core stocked depths, listed as standard by every reviewed supplier, with 225 mm the most consistently held.
  • The shallow (145–175 mm) and deep (375 mm) pods are made but regionally variable. The clearest example is the 375 mm pod being a stocked standard in one region and made-to-order in another.
  • Cut-to-size pods are widely offered but are a made-to-order product, carrying lead time, minimum-order and cartage rather than off-the-shelf supply.

Why the mid-range depths are the stocked ones
A deeper pod forms a deeper, stiffer rib, so deeper pods are reached for as ground reactivity rises. The mid-range depths cover the common ground conditions most residential slabs are built on, which is why suppliers hold them as standard; the shallow and deep ends serve the less common low- and high-reactivity extremes and are stocked less consistently. In Australia this depth-to-ground relationship is formalised as a site classification (under AS 2870), and suppliers commonly label their depths by the class each suits. The framework reports this mapping as supplier practice rather than relying on it, and treats the observed stocking pattern — not the classification labels — as the evidence for the bands. The depth-to-class labels are a procurement convenience, not a substitute for the engineer's slab design.

Basis of the ratings
The availability bands are a reasoned supply-chain judgement from the supplier review and the depth-stiffness logic above, not a measured stock survey.
