They scale the procurement scrutiny a specified depth should attract — lowest for the core stocked depths, higher for the regionally variable ones, highest for off-menu or custom depths — and should be refined with current local branch stock data and project examples.

References
Supplier product information: Saddingtons; Hunter Pods; Polyfoam; Bildavoid technical specification; Unipod. Reviewed for NSW supply.
Standards Australia. AS 2870 Residential Slabs and Footings — cited only for the Australian site-classification system referred to above, not as the basis of the availability ratings.

examples.caption: PLACEHOLDER FOR YOUR SCREENSHOT — a slab detail calling up a non-standard or custom pod depth with no stocked alternative noted. If the depth cannot be sourced locally in time, the contractor must request a substitution or wait on a made-to-order run.
PLACEHOLDER FOR YOUR SCREENSHOT — a slab detail on a standard stocked depth (225 or 300 mm), readily available from NSW suppliers with no lead-time risk.

supportingImages.title:
PLACEHOLDER — Saddingtons pod range
175, 225, 300 and 375 mm pods, 1090 mm square. Add your screenshot of the supplier's listed depth range.

PLACEHOLDER — Hunter Pods depth-by-site-class

PLACEHOLDER — Polyfoam 375 mm regional split
375 mm shown as a standard size in one state and made-to-order in another — the clearest example of regional stock variability. Add your screenshot.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by a review of NSW waffle pod supplier ranges showing 225 mm and 300 mm as the core stocked depths held by every reviewed supplier, with the shallow (145–175 mm) and deep (375 mm) pods regionally variable or made-to-order, and by the physical link between pod depth, rib stiffness and ground reactivity that explains why the mid-range depths are the stocked ones. The availability bands are a reasoned supply-chain judgement rather than a measured stock survey, so the entry sits at Medium. Treat it as a procurement prompt, not a proven decision rule, and confirm current local branch stock.

relatedEntries.note: The design-side counterpart. That entry weighs which pod depth suits the site for floor level and rigidity; this one checks whether the chosen depth can actually be sourced locally in time.

dimensions.name: Safety in Design
dimensions.description: Design decisions affecting the safety of construction workers and other stakeholders
generalGuidance: Consider the top reinforcement mat as a temporary working platform — its spacing directly affects worker safety during concrete placement
Every detail must be physically executable — if a worker cannot safely access, reach, or work in the space the detail requires, the design must be reconsidered

items.name: Reinforcement mat fall-through risk

data.question: At what top reinforcement spacing does the risk of a worker falling through or stepping between bars during construction become unacceptable?
data.parameter: Top reinforcement bar spacing (mm)
data.background: Top reinforcement mats in slabs and similar reinforced concrete elements temporarily serve as working surfaces during construction until concrete is placed. When bar spacing is too wide, workers risk stepping between bars, leading to falls and injuries. The spacing of the top mat directly determines whether it can safely support foot traffic during concrete placement and finishing. Any measures to address fall-through risk must also facilitate concrete placement and not obstruct the pour.

thresholds.guidance: Top mat provides adequate support as a working platform. Workers can walk and kneel on the reinforcement with minimal risk of stepping through. This is the recommended maximum spacing where top mats will be used as working platforms.

Fall-through risk on larger pours and should be addressed. The exception is small, single-truck pours under approximately 7 m3, where the pour is self-paced and conditions are controlled enough that 250 mm spacing is acceptable. For example, if the slab thickness is 180 mm, this equates to slabs less than approximately 38 m2. For pours exceeding 7 m3 (i.e. requiring more than one truck), this spacing should be addressed.

Unacceptable fall-through risk for any mat intended as a working platform. Spacing this wide provides almost no resistance to a misplaced step. 300 mm is the maximum spacing permitted under AS 3600, so greater spacings should not be encountered. If 300 x 300 is specified, it must be addressed before construction proceeds.

alternatives.title: Specify 200 x 200 mm top reinforcement spacing
alternatives.description: Use 200 x 200 mm spacing, or closer, as the preferred solution where the top mat will be used as a working surface during concrete placement. This provides a more supportive surface for workers and avoids relying on supplementary controls.

alternatives.title: Assess 250 x 250 mm spacing against pour size and site conditions
alternatives.description: Where 250 x 250 mm spacing is proposed, consider the concrete pour volume and construction context. Small pours under approximately 7 m3 are generally more controlled and may be acceptable, while larger pours are more likely to involve faster movement, more workers and higher exposure time.

alternatives.title: Use supplementary top mesh or reduce the primary spacing
alternatives.description: Where 250 x 250 mm spacing is used on pours exceeding approximately 7 m3, or where 300 x 300 mm spacing is specified, provide an additional layer of top mesh such as SL82 or above to improve the working surface, or revise the primary top reinforcement spacing to 200 x 200 mm. Consider cover, reinforcement congestion and concrete placement before adding supplementary mesh.

data.evidence:
Site incident report: worker sustained a severe knee injury after stepping through 250 x 250 top reinforcement during slab pour. Supported by Safe Work Australia guidance on falls through openings and gaps in working platforms. Boot sole dimensions derived from ADF anthropometric surveys (Blanchonette, 2017). Slip and trip injury data from construction site research demonstrates that even non-fall slip events predominantly injure lower extremities (Lipscomb et al., 2006). Prevention through Design guidance for concrete construction recommends mat mesh over widely spaced rebar as a walking surface control (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, 2024).

Pour size threshold (7 m3)
The 7 m3 threshold corresponds to the typical capacity of a single concrete truck. Pours within a single truck are generally self-paced and controlled, because the rate of placement is set by the workers screeding and finishing rather than by trucks queueing on site. Pours requiring multiple trucks introduce time pressure to keep up with placement and faster worker movement across the reinforcement mat, increasing exposure time and the likelihood of a misplaced step.

References
Blanchonette, P. (2017). Australian Defence Force Anthropometry: A Summary of Historic Surveys (DST-Group-GD-0968). Defence Science and Technology Group.
Lipscomb, H. J., Glazner, J. E., Bondy, J., Guarini, K., & Lezotte, D. (2006). Injuries from slips and trips in construction. Applied Ergonomics, 37(3), 267–274. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2005.07.008
National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health. (2024). Prevention through Design Toolkit for the Construction Industry (DHHS (NIOSH) Publication No. 2024-124). U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. https://doi.org/10.26616/NIOSHPUB2024124

examples.caption: Top reinforcement at N12-250 spacing on a 14.26 m3 pour. While the matching top and bottom spacing simplifies detailing, the 250 mm top mat spacing creates unacceptable fall-through risk on a pour of this size.
Top reinforcement at N12-250 spacing on a small balcony pour of approximately 3.8 m3. The small pour volume means conditions are controlled and the 250 mm spacing is acceptable.

Top reinforcement at N12-200 spacing on a 12.7 m3 pour. Bottom mat at N12-300 is not a concern as it is not used as a working platform. The 200 mm top spacing provides a safe working surface during concrete placement.

supportingImages.title:
Blanchonette (2017) - ADF foot dimensions
Mean foot lengths of 264 mm (Army 1968) and 271 mm (Navy 2000), used as the basis for the boot sole sizes in this analysis.

Lipscomb et al. (2006) - construction slip and trip injuries
Of 751 incidents at Denver International Airport (1989-1994), 90.5% were same-level falls or slips, predominantly injuring lower extremities.

NIOSH (2024) - Prevention through Design controls
Recommends 4″ × 4″ (approximately 100 × 100 mm) mat mesh over widely spaced rebar as a walking surface during concrete pours.

Boot sole fit - normal stance
Boot sole overlaid on N12 grids. At 300 mm spacing the 288 mm clear gap exceeds the boot sole length.

Boot sole fit - angled foot placement
At an angled approach, 250 mm spacing presents a step-through risk for both 5th percentile and mean boot sizes.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to top reinforcement mats in slabs and similar reinforced concrete elements where workers are expected to walk, kneel or stand on the reinforcement during concrete placement, vibration, screeding or finishing. This includes suspended slabs, slabs-on-ground, raft slabs, balconies, podium slabs and other reinforced concrete pours where the top mat functions as a temporary working surface.

checks.title: Top mat used as a working surface
checks.description: This guidance is primarily concerned with the top reinforcement mat because this is the layer workers walk, kneel or stand on during concrete placement. Bottom reinforcement, wall reinforcement, starter bars and other non-trafficable reinforcement do not create the same working-surface risk. However, designers may still choose to match top and bottom reinforcement spacing where this improves detailing simplicity, ordering, placement consistency or reduces the likelihood of site errors.

checks.title: Pour volume is a practical indicator, not a fixed safety boundary
checks.description: The 7 m3 threshold is used as a practical indicator of pour intensity and exposure, not as a statutory or absolute safety limit. A smaller pour may still require closer spacing if access is awkward, the reinforcement is difficult to move across, or workers need to spend extended time on the mat.

checks.title: Structural and placement requirements remain controlling
checks.description: Any change to reinforcement spacing or addition of supplementary mesh must still satisfy structural design requirements, cover, bar layering, congestion, concrete placement and vibration requirements.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a safety-in-design review prompt, not as a blanket rule. The appropriate response is to consider whether the top mat will function as a working surface and whether the specified spacing creates an avoidable risk during construction.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by safety-in-design literature, anthropometric/boot-size reasoning, practical site experience, and a clear worker fall-through mechanism.

items.name: Confined-space entry at pier and shaft bases

data.question: When a pier or shaft is connected to rock or otherwise completed at its base, does the detail require a worker to enter the excavation to install it, or can the work be done from the surface?
data.parameter: Whether the pier or shaft base detail requires a worker to enter the excavation to install it (judged: socket into rock — cast from the top — entry into shaft).
data.background:
Piers and bored piles, including those socketed into rock, are normally reinforced by fabricating the reinforcement cage at ground level and lowering it into the bore by crane, splicing longer cages at the casing top as they are lowered. Worked this way, nobody needs to enter the shaft. A confined-space entry arises when the base detail cannot be completed from the surface and instead requires a worker to go down — most commonly to drill and grout starter bars or dowels into the rock at the bottom of the pier, or to make a connection by hand at the base.

A deep, narrow excavation with restricted entry and exit is the classic setting for a confined space. Under the WHS Regulations a confined space is not defined by its dimensions but by its hazards: an enclosed or partially enclosed space, not intended to be occupied, where there is a risk from an unsafe oxygen level, airborne contaminants, or engulfment. Such spaces can develop an oxygen-deficient or contaminated atmosphere, and their restricted access makes escape and rescue difficult — which is why confined-space work carries mandatory controls and remains a recurring cause of work-related death.

This is a design decision before it is a site procedure. The most effective control in the hierarchy is to eliminate the need to enter, and for a pier base that is usually achievable at the drawing board: socket the pier into the rock and run continuous reinforcement so the connection is made by the socket and a surface-placed cage, use couplers or cast-in connections rather than hand-fixing at depth, or select a pier system (such as a continuous flight auger pile, where the cage is plunged from the surface) that needs no entry. The contractor still controls how any confined space that does remain is managed; the designer controls whether the detail forces the entry in the first place.

thresholds.guidance: The base is completed without anyone entering the excavation, and socketing into rock achieves this at any depth, which makes it the default. The connection comes from the socket and a reinforcement cage placed from the surface — fabricated at ground level and lowered as a unit, or spliced at the casing top as it descends — so there is no bar-fixing to do at the bottom of the hole. The design has eliminated the need to enter, which is the most effective control, rather than relying on the site to manage an entry.

Acceptable only where nobody enters the excavation. On a shallow bucket pier, a worker can reach the base from outside the hole to place and finish it, so no one is inside a confined space — and that is fine. It carries one condition the designer must confirm: the pier must be genuinely shallow enough to reach and work from the surface. Where there is any doubt that the base can be completed without someone getting down into the hole, treat it as an entry and re-detail to socket.

As drawn, the base cannot be completed without a worker getting down inside a deep, narrow excavation — for example to drill and grout bars into the rock at the bottom. That may expose the worker to confined-space and excavation hazards, including unsafe or contaminated atmospheres, restricted entry and exit, engulfment or collapse, and difficult rescue. Re-detail so the base is made from the surface, normally by socketing into rock. If entry genuinely cannot be removed, treat it as a deliberate residual design hazard and provide the required safety information so the entry is planned, permitted and controlled rather than discovered on site.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to piers, bored piles, bucket piers, shafts and similar deep, narrow excavations where the design specifies work at the base — such as connecting the pier to rock, fixing starter bars or dowels, or making a base connection — and where that work could require a worker to enter the excavation. It is concerned with the design decision that determines whether entry is needed, not with the procedures for entering a confined space once one exists.

checks.title: The design decision, not the entry procedure
checks.description: This entry is about whether the detail forces a worker into the excavation, which is a design matter. How a confined space is then classified, permitted, ventilated, monitored and managed is the contractor’s responsibility under their own systems. The designer’s duty is to remove the need to enter so far as is reasonably practicable; the contractor’s duty to manage any space that remains applies regardless. Both duties coexist.

checks.title: A confined space is judged, not measured
checks.description: A shaft is not a confined space merely because it is deep. Under the WHS Regulations the classification depends on the structure and the specific circumstances — whether there is a risk from an unsafe atmosphere or engulfment and whether entry and exit are restricted. A shallow, open, well-ventilated excavation that a person can freely walk into and out of may not be a confined space. Use judgement rather than a depth cut-off.

checks.title: Standard piling already avoids entry
checks.description: For most bored piers the reinforcement cage is prefabricated and lowered or spliced from the surface, so entry is the exception rather than the rule. This entry targets the specific base details that break that pattern — rock-socket dowels, hand-made base connections, or base preparation that must be done by a person at depth.

checks.title: Some base work may be genuinely unavoidable
checks.description: Certain situations — hand-excavated piers, some rock-socket or remediation details, or inspection and clean-out requirements — may have no practical surface alternative. The rating is a prompt, not a prohibition. ‘Action required’ means review the detail and, if entry must remain, plan for it and communicate it, not that the detail cannot be used.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a safety-in-design prompt. The aim is to ask whether the base detail forces a worker into the excavation and whether a surface method would remove that need — not to rule out any particular pier type.

alternatives.title: Socket the pier into rock
alternatives.description: Make the connection by socketing the pier into rock and carrying continuous reinforcement from the socket up into the element above, so the fixity comes from the socket and a cage placed from the surface rather than from bars fixed at the base. There is then no work to do at the bottom of the hole, and it works at any depth. This is the detail shown in the recommended example.

alternatives.title: If entry remains unavoidable, provide a written safety report for the residual design hazard
alternatives.description: Where no surface method is practical and the pier base must be completed by worker entry, make that requirement explicit rather than leaving it to be discovered on site. WHS Regulation 295 requires a designer of a structure, or part of a structure, to provide a written report to the PCBU (Person Conducting a Business or Undertaking) who commissioned the design, specifying hazards relating to the design that create a health and safety risk to persons carrying out the construction work and are associated only with that particular design. For this entry, that means recording the residual pier-entry risk, the hazardous structural feature that creates it, the designer’s assessment of the risk, and the action taken to eliminate or minimise it. The client must provide the relevant safety report information to the principal contractor so the entry can be planned and controlled under the contractor’s confined-space and excavation procedures.

data.evidence:
This entry rests on design-stage safety logic and confined-space control duties, applied to a specific pier-base detail. The band states are a judged application of that evidence to pier and shaft details; they are not measured depth or diameter thresholds.

Design decisions influence construction safety. Behm (2005) reviewed 224 construction fatality investigations and found that 42% were linked to the design-for-construction-safety concept — meaning the risk that contributed to the incident could have been reduced or eliminated if construction safety had been considered during design. A pier base detail that makes safe construction depend on worker entry into a deep, narrow excavation is a construction-stage hazard influenced by design, not only a site procedure.

Confined-space fatalities are real and recurring. Selman et al. (2017) identified 59 work-related traumatic fatalities involving confined spaces in Australia over 2000–2012. Figure 4 from that paper shows the mechanisms of those fatalities, including four deaths from slide or cave-in mechanisms. This supports treating worker entry into deep pier excavations as a serious construction-stage safety risk, even though the exact confined-space classification of any particular excavation depends on its hazards and circumstances.

Eliminating the need to enter is the preferred control. The Safe Work Australia Confined spaces Code of Practice places the control logic upstream. It states that designers, manufacturers, importers, suppliers, installers and constructors of plant, substances or structures that include a space intended, or likely, to become a confined space must ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that the plant, substance or structure is without risks to health and safety. To carry out this duty, they must eliminate the need for any person to enter a confined space so far as reasonably practicable. If this is not reasonably practicable, the need for entry must be minimised, the space must be designed with safe means of entry and exit, and the risk to any person who enters must be eliminated or minimised.

Where entry remains, the residual design hazard must be communicated. The Safe Work Australia Safe design of structures Code of Practice sets out the information-transfer mechanism under WHS Regulation 295. A designer of a structure, or part of a structure, must provide a written report to the PCBU (Person Conducting a Business or Undertaking) who commissioned the design, specifying hazards relating to the design that create a health and safety risk to persons carrying out the construction work and are associated only with that particular design. The safety report should include the hazardous structural feature, the designer’s assessment of the risk, and the action taken to control it; the client must provide relevant safety report information to the principal contractor. For this entry, that means that if pier entry cannot be designed out, it should be identified as a residual design hazard rather than left as an unspoken construction assumption.

References
Behm, M. (2005). Linking construction fatalities to the design for construction safety concept. Safety Science, 43(8), 589–611. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ssci.2005.04.002
Safe Work Australia. (2024). Model Code of Practice: Confined spaces.
Safe Work Australia. (2024). Model Code of Practice: Safe design of structures.
Selman, J., Spickett, J., Jansz, J., & Mullins, B. (2017). Work-related traumatic fatal injuries involving confined spaces in Australia, 2000–2012. Journal of Health, Safety and Environment, 33(2), 197–215.

examples.caption: A suspended spa slab on reinforced bored or bucket piers (5N12 vertical with R10–200 ties), where the connection to the sandstone is made by drilling and grouting a single N28 galvanised starter bar 300 mm into the bedrock at the base of each pier. The drill-and-grout operation sits at the bottom of the pier shaft and tends to require a worker to enter the excavation to complete it. The same fixity into rock could be achieved by socketing the pier and running continuous reinforcement, keeping the work at the surface.

Piers 450 mm in diameter socketed a minimum of 150 mm into rock, supporting the slab over (with 3N12 extra each way at the top over each pier). The connection to rock is made by the socket itself rather than by bars fixed at the base, so the pier can be reinforced with a cage placed from the surface and nobody needs to enter the shaft to drill or grout into the bedrock.

supportingImages.title: Mechanisms of traumatic confined-space fatalities in Australia, 2000–2012. Selman et al. (2017) identified 59 work-related confined-space fatalities, including four deaths from slide or cave-in mechanisms. This supports treating worker entry into deep pier excavations as a serious construction-stage safety risk that should be eliminated or reduced through design where practicable.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by WHS duties and reporting requirements, confined-space guidance, fatality literature, and a clear safety-in-design elimination principle.

items.name: Reducing grinder cutting from large slab reinforcement bars

data.question: Are 20 mm and larger slab bars genuinely required, given that their use makes any on-site cutting more likely to involve an angle grinder rather than ordinary cutter/bender equipment?
data.parameter: Nominal slab reinforcement diameter (mm).
data.background:
This entry is the Safety in Design companion to the detailing, logistics and drawing-communication entries on large slab reinforcement bars.

The structural use of 20 mm, 24 mm or larger bars is not unsafe by itself. Larger bars may be necessary at supports, transfer zones, thickened strips, local bands, penetrations or concentrated-load areas. The safety issue is that specifying larger bars changes the likely site cutting class if those bars ever need to be cut on site.

A 12 mm or 16 mm slab bar is more likely to be within the range of ordinary low-energy site cutting and bending equipment, such as simple cutter/bender tools or smaller portable powered tools. A 20 mm or larger bar is much less likely to be handled by that ordinary cutter/bender class. If a 20 mm or larger bar has to be shortened on site, the practical cutting method may shift toward an angle grinder or specialist cutting equipment.

That matters because grinder cutting is a real safety exposure, not just an inconvenience. Angle grinders use high-speed rotating discs. They can kick back, discs can shatter, guards can be removed or misused, and severe injuries and fatalities are documented in clinical studies and regulator safety alerts.

The designer does not control the contractor's cutting method. That remains a construction method and site safety responsibility. However, the designer does control the reinforcement diameter selected. If 12 mm or 16 mm bars would satisfy the design, specifying 20 mm or larger bars can create an avoidable pathway to a higher-risk site cutting task.

The aim is therefore not to ban 20 mm and larger slab bars. The aim is to ask whether they are genuinely needed. Where they are not needed, using 12 mm and 16 mm bars is the safer design-stage choice because it avoids unnecessarily moving the work into a grinder-cutting or specialist-cutting class if cutting is required on site.

thresholds.guidance: General slab reinforcement is detailed with 12 mm or 16 mm bars where these sizes satisfy the structural design. These bars remain in the smaller-bar site handling range and are more likely to be cut, bent or adjusted with ordinary cutter/bender equipment if site work is required. This reduces the chance that reinforcement cutting shifts into angle-grinder work.

Slab reinforcement includes bars 20 mm diameter or larger. These bars may be structurally necessary, but they should not be used as a default because they are outside the ordinary 16 mm cutter/bender class. If they need to be cut on site, angle-grinder cutting or specialist cutting is more likely. Review whether the larger diameter is genuinely required, or whether 12 mm or 16 mm bars at closer spacing can achieve the design intent.

alternatives.title: Use 12 mm and 16 mm bars for general slab reinforcement where structurally practical
alternatives.description: Where 12 mm or 16 mm bars can achieve the design intent, prefer those sizes for general slab reinforcement. This keeps the work in the smaller-bar handling range and reduces the likelihood that site cutting of reinforcement becomes grinder cutting.

alternatives.title: Reserve 20 mm and larger bars for genuine structural demand
alternatives.description: Use 20 mm, 24 mm or larger bars where the structural demand justifies them, such as local support zones, transfer strips, punching reinforcement zones, heavy concentrated loads, thickened slab bands or other high-demand areas. Avoid using larger bars broadly where smaller bars at closer spacing would work.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to reinforced concrete slab reinforcement where the designer is choosing bar diameter and spacing, especially where a choice exists between 12 mm or 16 mm bars at closer spacing and 20 mm or larger bars at wider spacing.

checks.title: This is a bar-size selection issue
checks.description: The design control is the diameter selected for the slab reinforcement. The entry does not ask the engineer to manage site cutting methods, but it does ask whether the design has unnecessarily moved the work into a larger-bar cutting class.

checks.title: Larger bars may still be structurally required
checks.description: This entry is not a rule against 20 mm, 24 mm or larger reinforcement. Where larger bars are required for strength, serviceability, anchorage, congestion control or another real design reason, they should be used. The concern is unnecessary or broad use of large bars where 12 mm or 16 mm bars would work.

checks.title: Cutting risk is different from bending risk
checks.description: This entry focuses on cutting exposure and grinder use. Bending and rebending of larger bars are covered in the related detailing and logistics entries.

checks.title: Site methods remain the contractor's responsibility
checks.description: The contractor remains responsible for safe systems of work, tool selection, guarding, training and exclusion zones. The Safety in Design issue is whether the design unnecessarily creates a higher-risk cutting task in the first place.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a design-stage safety prompt, not as a prohibition on large bars. The question is whether 20 mm and larger slab bars are genuinely needed, given the likely increase in grinder-cutting or specialist-cutting exposure if they must be cut on site.

data.evidence:
Reinforcement handling hinge at 16 mm. SRIA's Bending Reinforcement on Site recommends special manual bending tools for bars up to about 16 mm diameter and electric bending tools for larger bars. The same document notes that the AS/NZS 4671 rebend test applies to deformed bars 16 mm and smaller, with no equivalent test for bars 20 mm and larger because rebending is intended for smaller bars. SRIA Technical Note 4 also states that cold bending bars greater than 16 mm requires considerable force, and that N20 and larger bars preferably require power-operated bending tools. This supports treating 12 mm and 16 mm bars as one practical site-handling class, and 20 mm and larger bars as a different class.

Tooling examples. The reviewed tool examples support the same practical distinction. The simple manual cutter/bender and portable powered bender examples are both limited to 16 mm. The larger-bar tools reviewed for 32 mm and 46 mm capacity are industrial equipment at a much higher cost and equipment class. This does not prove that every 20 mm bar will be cut with a grinder, but it supports the practical point that 20 mm and larger bars are outside the ordinary small-bar cutter/bender class available on many sites.

Angle-grinder injury severity. Sozbilen et al. (2018) conducted a prospective one-year emergency-department study of angle-grinder hand and forearm injuries. They recorded 82 angle-grinder injuries out of 742 acute hand and forearm injuries. The paper reports tendon, nerve, artery and amputation injuries, and classifies the grinder injuries using the Modified Hand Injury Severity Score. Figure 4 labels 40% of the recorded injuries as severe and 31% as major, a combined 71% by the figure percentages. By count, the paper reports 34 severe and 27 major injuries out of 82, or approximately 74%. Either way, the study supports treating grinder cutting as a serious injury exposure rather than a minor inconvenience.

Angle-grinder hazard mechanism. Sozbilen et al. describe angle grinders as high-speed disc tools, with discs rotating about 6000 to 15,000 rpm. The study identifies kickback, wrong disc selection, worn discs, vibration and disc shattering as relevant injury mechanisms. SafeWork NSW safety material also warns about broken discs, removed guards, incorrect discs and kickback. One SafeWork NSW safety alert describes a fatal incident in which part of a broken disc from a 230 mm angle grinder struck a worker's chest and caused fatal internal injuries. A separate SafeWork NSW incident release describes a fatal nine-inch angle-grinder incident on a residential construction site and warns that larger 230 mm grinders are harder to manoeuvre and can cause severe kickback.

Safety in Design link. Safe design guidance requires designers of structures used as workplaces to consider hazards created by design decisions and to work through the hierarchy of control. In this entry, the higher-risk task is grinder cutting of large reinforcement bars. The designer cannot control the contractor's cutting method, but can avoid specifying 20 mm and larger slab bars where 12 mm or 16 mm bars would be structurally practical. That is the design-stage control: reduce the likelihood that the hazardous cutting task is created unnecessarily.

References
Steel Reinforcement Institute of Australia. (2016). Bending Reinforcement on Site, Issue No. 42/4, December 2016.
Steel Reinforcement Institute of Australia. (2007). Fabrication and Site Handling of Reinforcing Bars, Technical Note 4, November 2007.
Sozbilen, M. C., Dastan, A. E., Gunay, H., & Kucuk, L. (2018). A prospective study of angle grinder injuries in the hands and forearms during a one-year period. Hand Surgery and Rehabilitation, 37, 300–304. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.hansur.2018.07.002
SafeWork NSW. (2018). Guards and disks on angle grinders safety alert.
SafeWork NSW. (2022). Angle grinder fatality incident information release.
Safe Work Australia. (2018). Model Code of Practice: Safe Design of Structures.

supportingImages.title:
Angle-grinder injury severity — Sozbilen et al. (2018)
In a prospective one-year study of angle-grinder hand and forearm injuries, Figure 4 labels 40% of recorded injuries as severe and 31% as major using the Modified Hand Injury Severity Score. Combined, severe and major injuries account for 71% of the angle-grinder injuries by the figure percentages. This supports treating grinder cutting as a meaningful construction-stage safety exposure, not merely a minor inconvenience. Source: Sozbilen et al. (2018), Hand Surgery and Rehabilitation, 37, 300–304.

evidenceSupport.summary: The hazard of angle-grinder cutting is strongly supported by clinical injury evidence and SafeWork NSW regulator material, and the 16 mm reinforcement-handling hinge is supported by SRIA guidance. The design link between specifying 20 mm and larger slab bars and increasing likely grinder-cutting exposure is a reasoned constructability pathway rather than a measured reinforcement-specific injury dataset, so the overall support level is medium.

relatedEntries.note: Both ask whether 20 mm and larger slab bars are genuinely required, or whether 12 mm and 16 mm bars can achieve the design more constructably.
Both use the same 16 mm hinge. This entry focuses on cutting safety, while the logistics entry focuses on bending equipment class and on-site adaptability.
If 20 mm and larger bars are required, making them visually clear reduces the chance of ordering, fixing or cutting errors that could lead to site correction.
Both concern the risk that additional or non-typical reinforcement is missed, misread or incorrectly fixed because the drawing does not make it clear enough.

items.name: Foundation selection and the latent safety risk of screw piles

data.question: Has the foundation system been chosen so the designer can verify its as-built adequacy — or have screw piles been adopted on speed and cost, leaving a latent, uninspectable risk that hasn't been weighed or communicated?
data.parameter: The chosen foundation system, assessed on whether the designer can verify its as-built adequacy.
data.background:
A foundation can be carried by a concrete pile (bored or CFA) or a screw (helical) pile. Both can be designed to have capacity, so that is not the safety question. The safety question is whether the designer who carries the duty for the structure can verify the foundation's as-built adequacy — and on that test the two are not equivalent.

A poorly built concrete pile fails visibly: the geometry is seen, the pour is witnessed, and its one covert risk (low strength) can be designed out by specifying a higher grade. A poorly built screw pile fails invisibly — low torque, soil loosened on installation, an out-of-position pile, a deficient connection — and none of it can be pre-empted with a conservative number. The first four images below show this gap directly.

The responsibility split sharpens it. The two most safety-critical elements — the pile's real capacity and its connection into the structure — are both designed and installed by the screw pile designer, usually the contractor's own cost-incentivised engineer, under their own producer statement, with no agency rating their competence. The structure's engineer designs the beam and supplies the loads, so their only real lever over that connection is to confirm it has been documented and certified before the beam is poured.

This matters because the deficiency is latent. An under-performing or off-position pile drives differential settlement, erodes the beam's punching-shear reliability, or overloads the connection — showing first as cracked finishes and jammed doors, but loading beams and columns the same way. At the severe end differential settlement has caused catastrophic failure and loss of life, and with screw piles it may not show until a large or seismic load, so warning and danger can arrive together.

None of this means screw piles never work — installed correctly they perform, and some sites suit nothing else. It means a verifiable concrete pile is the best-practice default, and screw piles are a considered last resort: used only where no verifiable system suits, with the pile-head connection required and reviewed, and the residual risk put squarely to the builder. When a builder proposes substituting them to save time or cost, the safe response is not a like-for-like swap but to spell out the risk — which, weighed against the saving, often steers the choice back.

thresholds.guidance: A foundation whose as-built adequacy can be seen and recorded — a bored or CFA concrete pile — has been chosen. The geometry is observable, the pour is witnessed, and the founding material can be confirmed, so the designer can stand behind what is in the ground. This is the safe-design default wherever a verifiable system is feasible.

thresholds.range: Screw piles as a last resort, risk owned
thresholds.guidance: Screw piles are used only because site access or disruption genuinely rules out a verifiable concrete system — not to save time or money. The designer has designed the beam for the loads and specified a placement tolerance it can carry, and has required the screw pile designer to provide a documented pile-head connection into that beam and issue a producer statement or certification covering the pile system and connection before anything is cast in. Install verification has been required, and the residual risk that cannot be designed out — unconfirmable founding material, an uninspectable install, no rated contractor — has been communicated to the builder. This is the most that can be done with screw piles, and it remains a compromise, not best practice, because that residual risk stays.

thresholds.range: Screw piles on speed or cost
thresholds.guidance: Screw piles have been chosen, or a substitution accepted, to save programme or money where a verifiable system would have suited — or with the pile-head connection left implied, undocumented or uncertified, the install unverified, or in ground that makes them more dangerous still (layered soils where torque over-reads capacity, sensitive or liquefiable soils, significant lateral or seismic demand). The latent, unverifiable risk is live and unowned. Revert to a verifiable system, or — if screw piles are genuinely unavoidable — require the screw pile designer's documented pile-head connection, certification and install verification, and put the residual risk to the builder, before proceeding. The flag marks an unexamined default, not a claim that screw piles can never be used.

alternatives.title: Default to a verifiable concrete pile
alternatives.description: Prefer a bored or CFA concrete pile whose adequacy is observable and recorded, and treat screw piles as a genuine last resort — used only where access or disruption rules out concrete plant. CFA in particular keeps much of the screw pile's appeal (low vibration and noise, little spoil, restricted access) while leaving a concrete element of known geometry with a monitored pour.

alternatives.title: Keep the beam yours, and confirm the pile-head connection exists
alternatives.description: The beam (or pile cap) is your reinforced-concrete element and stays in your design. The screw pile designer designs the pile and the pile-head connection into your beam and installs them under their own producer statement, so your control is not to design that connection but to confirm that it has been documented and certified before anything is cast into it.

alternatives.title: Require the interface protocol
alternatives.description: Specify that any change to your beam or ground beam needed to make the piles work must come back to you first, because it is your element. Do not let the pile system quietly drive a change to the structure without your sign-off.

alternatives.title: Require install verification, and be clear about its limits
alternatives.description: Require producer statements (design and construction, ideally with independent review), machine-logged torque and depth rather than hand-written records, founding confirmation by CPT or boreholes below the founding level, and load testing where deflection or capacity is critical. Recognise that this reduces but does not remove the risk, does not make the contractor independently rated because no such scheme exists, and is not yours to guarantee.

alternatives.title: If a builder or contractor asks to substitute screw piles, inform them of the risk
alternatives.description: Do not accept a substitution as a like-for-like swap to save time or money. Put the residual risks to the builder squarely and in writing: the unconfirmable founding material, the uninspectable install, the connection that depends on the contractor doing more than the minimum, and the latency — that a deficiency may not show until a large or seismic load. Weighed against the saving, this often steers the choice back to a verifiable system. Either way, record the connection requirement set, the review done, and the advice given, so the decision is an informed and traceable one.

alternatives.title: Avoid the ground conditions that amplify the risk
alternatives.description: Push toward a verifiable concrete system where the ground makes a screw pile harder to trust: layered soils, where installation torque can read higher than the capacity actually available; sensitive or liquefiable soils and deep weak deposits, where the slender shaft is prone to buckling and uplift deflection is large; and sites with significant lateral or seismic demand, where the narrow shaft is weakest. These are exactly the conditions where an unverifiable foundation is most dangerous.

applicability.appliesWhere: Applies to building foundations where a verifiable concrete pile (bored or CFA) is feasible and screw piles are being considered or proposed — typically lightly-to-moderately loaded footings, columns or ground-beam-supported slabs. It is most useful at concept and scheme stage, and whenever a builder or contractor proposes substituting screw piles for a designed concrete system. It is not aimed at sites where no verifiable system suits and screw piles are the only viable option; there the entry's role is the require-review-and-communicate duty, not avoidance.

checks.title: Can the as-built adequacy be seen or recorded?
checks.description: A concrete pile gives observable geometry, a witnessed pour, and spoil that confirms the founding material; a screw pile gives none of these — no spoil, and an installation torque that can over-read true capacity in layered soils. If the system's adequacy cannot be seen or independently recorded, the designer is trusting rather than verifying.

checks.title: Is this genuinely the only viable system, or a speed and cost choice?
checks.description: Distinguish a real last-resort case (access, headroom or disruption rules out concrete plant) from a substitution made to save programme or money. The first justifies the require-review-and-communicate path; the second is where the entry says revert to a verifiable system.

checks.title: The pile-head connection into your beam
checks.description: The pile-head connection between the steel pile and the beam is the screw pile designer's to design and install, and it may be delivered as a through-bar, protruding reinforcement, proprietary bracket, plate, sleeve, welded head or other nominated detail. The recurring risk is not that one detail type is automatically wrong; it is that the connection is left implied, undocumented or uncertified before the beam is cast. Confirm that the screw pile designer has established the connection detail and certification before pouring.

checks.title: Placement tolerance and the beam
checks.description: A screw pile is rarely installed exactly on position, and the deviation erodes the beam's reliability against punching shear. Because the placement is the contractor's, the designer's lever is to specify a tolerance the beam can carry and to require survey verification that piles land within it (Hoang, 2025).

checks.title: Ground conditions that amplify the risk
checks.description: Layered soils (torque over-reads), sensitive and liquefiable soils and deep weak deposits (buckling, large uplift deflection), significant lateral or seismic demand (slender shaft), and obstructions or hard layers (refusal, imprecise placement) all make an unverifiable screw pile more dangerous and push toward a concrete system.

checks.title: Verification and the absence of contractor accreditation
checks.description: No scheme rates a screw-piling contractor's competence or compliance, and the installation market is described in industry and parliamentary sources as under-regulated. Require producer statements and testing, but treat them as evidence to review, not as control the designer holds.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a safe-design prompt, not a prohibition. It does not say screw piles can never be used — correctly installed they perform. It flags that the design decision to use them transfers a latent, unverifiable safety risk the designer cannot control onto the structure's life, so they belong as a considered last resort. The beam (or pile cap) stays the designer's element; the pile and its pile-head connection are the screw pile designer's, documented and certified before casting; and the residual risk is communicated to the builder.

data.evidence:
Why this is a safety question. Engineering New Zealand's Practice Note 28 (2015) records the core hazard: with no spoil the founding material cannot be confirmed, installation torque can read higher than the capacity actually present where weak soil lies above or below the helix, and problems may not surface until a large or seismic load — in a largely unregulated installation market. It also sets the responsibility split this entry turns on: the pile system and its connection to the structure sit with the screw pile designer, while the structure's engineer designs the beam and provides the loads.

The harm pathway. An under-performing or off-position pile drives differential settlement, which Chen (2020) links to serious structural damage and, at the extreme, catastrophic failure and loss of life — redistributing load into beams and columns not designed for it, behind early symptoms that look merely architectural.

The connection and position. Helical pile-to-foundation connections can govern the system response and lack settled design provisions (Chiluwal & Guner, 2019), yet sit in the contractor's scope; the structure's engineer's lever is to confirm the detail is documented and certified before casting. Separately, from 510 field measurements, Hoang (2025) shows that standards-permitted positional deviation measurably reduces the beam's punching-shear reliability — so the lever is to specify a tolerance the beam can carry and require survey verification.

The installation reality. A screw-piling contractor's submission to the NSW Public Accountability Committee (Australian Foundation Systems Pty Ltd, 2019) states on the record that installation records can be falsified, that certifiers in almost all cases do not inspect, and that of the order of a hundred thousand screw piles were installed in NSW over a decade with many under specification — corroborating Practice Note 28 from inside the industry.

Basis of the bands. Because no body rates the contractor, the pile and connection sit in their scope, and per-pile checks are fallible, verification is not a lever the designer holds. The bands therefore turn on the levers that are: choosing a verifiable system, restricting screw piles to a genuine last resort, requiring a documented and certified connection into the designer's beam, requiring install verification, and communicating the residual risk.

References
Australian Foundation Systems Pty Ltd (2019). Submission No 82, Inquiry into the regulation of building standards, building quality and building disputes. NSW Legislative Council, Public Accountability Committee.
Chen, W. (2020). Structural Response of Multi-Story Buildings Subjected to Foundation Differential Settlements. PhD thesis, Concordia University.
Chiluwal, S., & Guner, S. (2019). Helical pile-to-pile cap connections subjected to uplift forces: improving future practice. Deep Foundations Institute Annual Conference.
Hoang, B. A. (2025). Reliability evaluation of the impact of pile positioning deviation on pile cap punching shear resistance: a case study from Vietnam. Engineering, Technology & Applied Science Research, 15(6), 28440-28446.
IPENZ / Engineering New Zealand (2015). Screw Piles: Guidelines for Design, Construction & Installation. Practice Note 28.

supportingImages.title:
Founding material visible versus hidden
Bored concrete piles bring excavated material to the surface and leave an open borehole before the cage and concrete are placed, allowing the founding material to be inspected. Screw piles do not create an open bore or equivalent spoil return, so the material supporting the helix remains hidden below ground.

Same site, different founding material
Nearby screw piles can look identical at ground level while their helices found in different subsurface materials. One pile may reach the intended competent rock, while another may terminate in weaker material where the rock level changes. The issue is not whether screw piles can work, but whether the founding condition of each pile can be verified.

Bored pile versus screw pile verification
Bored concrete piles provide directly observable construction evidence: bore depth, bore diameter, spoil or founding material, and reinforcement cage placement can be checked before concrete is placed. Screw piles provide less visible evidence below ground, so the designer must rely more heavily on surface set-out, installation records, torque information, testing and certification.

Why screw piles can be harder to independently verify
Screw piles can offer fast installation, low disruption, no spoil and no curing delay, but those same advantages reduce the amount of directly visible construction evidence. Without an open bore or spoil return, the founding material and helix position are not directly visible, so verification relies more heavily on installation records, torque data, testing and certification.

Example screw pile head linked into a ground beam
Example screw pile head linked into a reinforced concrete ground beam using a transverse reinforcing bar. This image is not shown as a defective detail; it illustrates the type of pile-head connection that should be established and certified by the screw pile designer before the beam is poured.

Hoang (2025) — pile position deviation from 510 field measurements
Field-measured pile position deviations showing the difference between actual and design pile-centre coordinates. Mean deviation was small (+1.2 mm in X and −3.6 mm in Y), but the spread was significant: standard deviation was about 51–54 mm, with recorded deviations up to +178 mm / −140 mm in X and +154 mm / −203 mm in Y. The study used these deviations to assess how pile misplacement affects pile-cap punching and shear reliability.

evidenceSupport.summary: A strong medium. Each harm pathway is well supported: differential settlement is linked to serious damage and, at the extreme, loss of life (Chen, 2020); positional deviation measurably reduces the beam's punching reliability from 510 field measurements (Hoang, 2025); helical pile-to-foundation connection research shows the connection can govern the system response and lacks settled design provisions (Chiluwal & Guner, 2019); and the verifiability gap, latency and the design-versus-install responsibility split are set out by an authoritative practice note (Engineering NZ Practice Note 28, 2015), with the under-regulated installation reality corroborated by a named industry submission to the NSW inquiry (2019). The recurring real-world pattern that the pile-head connection may be left implied, undocumented or uncertified rests on structural design engineer consultation rather than a published study. No single source runs the full chain from contractor behaviour to structural danger, and the band ordering is a reasoned call. Treat the rating as a safe-design selection prompt.

relatedEntries.note: Both are foundation-system decisions with a safety dimension: a bored pile creates a deep shaft with confined-space risk, while a screw pile trades that visible, manageable risk for a foundation whose adequacy cannot be verified. Weigh the safety trade-offs of the system together.

items.name: Temporary stability of retained, existing or part-built structure

data.question: Does the design identify whether retained, existing or part-built structure stays stable during the temporary construction condition?
data.parameter: Whether the temporary stability of retained or part-built structure is identified on the drawings or in the design notes.
data.background:
A structure can be stable once finished and still pass through an unstable temporary condition while it is being built. This is a documented hazard, not a theoretical one: regulators have recorded fatal and serious collapses of masonry and retained walls on construction and demolition sites where lateral restraint from permanent returns, floors or roof framing was missing, not yet installed, or removed during demolition.

The reason design-stage recognition matters is that temporary stability cannot be assumed to be handled by site judgement alone. The recorded collapses occurred where lateral support was missing, where temporary bracing was absent or inadequate, or where responsibility for it was unclear — and in the more dangerous cases the need for temporary stability design was not clearly identified, scoped or allocated in the first place, rather than simply being under-designed. A retained wall or part-built frame can be acting as a freestanding temporary structure even though nothing about the finished design makes that obvious. Making the dependency explicit at design stage keeps it from being left to site intuition, contract-scope assumptions or informal bracing.

There is also a recurring reason these elements end up retained, which can help explain how the dependency slips through. On renovation and alterations-and-additions projects, existing fabric is often kept for planning, heritage, streetscape or setback reasons, so a retained wall can enter the project as architectural or planning fabric rather than as a structural element to be designed for. The structural engineer is typically engaged for the new work — the ground slab, footings, beams, suspended slabs and connections — so the retained fabric’s temporary stability can fall into the gap between planning intent, permanent design scope, and builder-managed temporary works.

thresholds.guidance: The temporary condition has been determined and conveyed: the retained or part-built element is either confirmed self-stable in the temporary condition, or the drawings and notes clearly identify the temporary stability dependency. Where temporary support is required, the documents flag that a temporary stability assessment and bracing or support by a suitably qualified person is required before demolition, excavation or construction removes existing restraint or support, and that the support remain until the new permanent restraint is complete and capable of acting. Where the project retains existing fabric or is built in stages, the determination is made and recorded even where the conclusion is that the element is self-stable, so the assumption is visible rather than assumed.

Ordinary renovation, demolition, excavation or staged-construction sequencing appears to leave a retained or part-built element without the lateral restraint, support, retained ground or founding condition it previously relied on, and no note, sequence assumption, hold point, bracing requirement, exclusion zone or temporary-works interface requirement is shown. The dependency should be clarified and communicated before the design is issued or before construction starts.

A retained wall, façade, frame, retaining wall or similar element is left standing with a credible overturning, out-of-plane, sliding, undermining, buckling or progressive-collapse mechanism during a normal construction, excavation or demolition sequence, and the design gives no warning or requirement for temporary stability assessment. The stability assumption should be resolved before construction proceeds.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies where structural design is undertaken for renovation, alteration, demolition or staged construction — work that characteristically involves new concrete such as ground slabs, footings, tie beams or suspended slabs — and existing fabric is retained, or the structure is left part-built, before its permanent restraints are complete. The retained element need not itself be reinforced concrete; what brings it within scope is that the structural design and its construction sequence remove, change or rely on that element’s restraint. It does not apply where the issue is only conventional falsework or formwork sizing for wet concrete, where a temporary stability system has already been designed and checked by a suitable temporary-works engineer, or where the retained element is demonstrably self-stable under the temporary load case.

checks.title: Confirm what is retained and removed
checks.description: On renovation work the designer should normally ask what is being retained and what is being removed, rather than assume. The entry has limited force where the designer genuinely has no information that an existing element is to be retained or altered — but obtaining that information is itself part of designing the alteration.

checks.title: Planning-retained fabric
checks.description: Where existing walls, facades, verandahs, chimneys, slabs, columns or building portions are retained to support an alterations-and-additions pathway, preserve streetscape character, maintain setbacks or retain heritage fabric, check whether those elements remain stable once adjacent floors, roofs, returns, diaphragms or internal walls are removed. Planning value does not imply structural stability.

checks.title: What previously braced it
checks.description: Identify what actually restrained the element in its original condition — floors, roof, cross-walls, returns, a diaphragm or adjacent ground — and confirm whether the sequence removes that restraint before the new restraint can act.

checks.title: Sequence dependency
checks.description: Distinguish completed-condition stability from each construction stage. A frame stable once its diaphragm or braced bay is complete may be unstable before then.

checks.title: Adjacent and retained ground
checks.description: Excavation or changed levels can undermine a retaining wall or adjoining structure that was previously stable.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a safe-design review prompt. The step that matters at design stage is recognising and conveying the temporary condition — that the retained or part-built element is either self-stable or needs temporary support — before its restraint is removed. The engineer designing the permanent structure is well placed to see a foreseeable hazard and should recommend that temporary bracing or support be designed; that design can be done by the structural engineer if engaged, or by a temporary-works engineer or other competent person. The point is that the recommendation comes from the engineer who can see the hazard rather than being left to chance on site.

alternatives.title: Identify the retained or part-built elements that lose restraint
alternatives.description: Mark the existing walls, facades, frames, returns, roof or floor restraints or adjoining structures that are being retained, removed, undercut or temporarily isolated, so the elements whose restraint changes during the works are explicit on the design.

alternatives.title: Add a specific design note, not a generic one
alternatives.description: Where the instability is foreseeable from the design, tie the note to the actual element or stage rather than relying on a generic ‘contractor to brace as required’. For a retained wall, for example: ‘Existing wall to remain is not to be left unrestrained. Contractor to obtain a temporary stability assessment and bracing or support design by a suitably qualified engineer before removing existing lateral restraints (roof framing, floor framing, return walls or other connected structure). Temporary support to remain until the new permanent restraint is complete and capable of acting.’ For a retained facade, require the retention system, sequence, inspection and removal or transfer of support to be designed and certified by a suitably qualified temporary-works engineer before the internal structure is demolished.

alternatives.title: Recommend the bracing be designed by a competent person
alternatives.description: Recommend that a temporary-works engineer or competent structural engineer design and check the bracing before the existing restraint is removed. The structural engineer can take this on directly if engaged to do so; the key step is that the recommendation is made rather than assumed.

alternatives.title: Show the sequence assumptions the design relies on
alternatives.description: State the construction-sequence assumptions the permanent design depends on — for example ‘return wall to be built before the wall exceeds the nominated height’, ‘roof or floor diaphragm not to be removed until wall bracing is installed’, or ‘do not backfill or surcharge the wall until permanent restraint is complete’. Where feasible, sequence permanent stabilising elements early so they provide restraint sooner and reduce temporary bracing demand.

alternatives.title: Record a design-specific hazard in the WHS safety report
alternatives.description: Where the design creates an unusual or design-specific temporary stability hazard, record it in the written safety report under WHS Regulation 295 so the client and principal contractor cannot miss it.

alternatives.title: Use condition-based removal wording, not fixed dates
alternatives.description: Instead of ‘remove props after 7 days’, use condition-based wording such as ‘do not remove temporary restraint until the permanent stabilising element or load path shown on the drawings is complete and capable of acting’.

alternatives.title: Control undermining and collapse-zone exposure
alternatives.description: Where excavation, slab edge beams, footing trenches, service trenches or groundworks may undermine an existing retaining wall, boundary wall, footing or retained structure, do not rely on wall bracing alone as the default control. First identify whether the excavation removes bearing support, passive resistance, retained soil support or other ground conditions that keep the structure stable. Where that risk exists, provide a designed response such as staged excavation, underpinning, temporary ground support, redesign of the footing or edge beam, or an exclusion zone that keeps workers out of the potential collapse path until the structure has been assessed and made safe.

data.evidence:
This entry rests on regulator incident evidence and safe-design duties, applied to retained or part-built structure. The band states are a judged application of that evidence, not measured thresholds.

The hazard is real and recorded. SafeWork NSW reported fatal and serious masonry-wall collapse incidents on Sydney sites and identified the lack of lateral support from permanent returns, floor or wall frames or roof structures — missing, not yet installed, or removed during demolition — as the usual contributing factor. WorkSafe Victoria gives independent corroboration, warning that unstable or inadequately braced masonry walls expose workers and the public to death or serious injury, and that temporary bracing should be retained until the final structural elements provide adequate lateral support.

The recommended response is a design-stage one. The SafeWork NSW alert recommends identifying, before construction, which walls need temporary support, and including temporary support locations and designs in the construction drawings — placing the recognition step at design stage rather than leaving it to site judgement.

The communication duty sits with the designer. The NSW Safe Design of Structures Code of Practice says designers must provide information on the conditions necessary for the structure to be without risk during construction, expressly including temporary work required to construct or renovate the building as designed, such as bracing of frames. It also requires a written safety report for design-specific construction hazards under WHS Regulation 295.

Temporary-works guidance sets the boundary. The Engineers Australia / Temporary Works Forum Australia guidance states that the greatest opportunity to eliminate temporary-works risk is during permanent works design, and that permanent designers should communicate residual risks and temporary conditions so temporary-works designers and site teams can manage them — without making the permanent engineer responsible for designing every brace.

A responsibility precedent. Australian Steel Institute Technical Note TN002 describes a steel-frame collapse during erection where the engineer’s generic ‘contractor to brace as required’ note was reportedly held insufficient, the court finding that the engineer needed to make the need for temporary bracing explicit. It is steel-specific and is used as an analogue, not a general rule.

Taken together, the evidence supports a design-stage recognition and communication prompt. It does not support a broad claim that builders generally fail to understand freestanding-wall risk — in some recorded cases the risk was treated as obvious — so the entry rates the duty to identify and communicate the dependency, not any claim about how often it is missed.

References
SafeWork NSW. (2017). Dangers from masonry walls on construction or demolition sites [safety alert].
WorkSafe Victoria. (2014, reviewed 2022). Danger of freestanding masonry walls [safety alert].
SafeWork NSW. Safe Design of Structures: Code of Practice.
Engineers Australia / Temporary Works Forum Australia. (2020). Temporary Works Risk Guidance (TWfAU2020:01).
Hogan, T. J. / Australian Steel Institute. (2011). Technical Note TN002: Issues with Temporary Bracing of Steel Structures (Version 2).

supportingImages.title:
Temporary stability can be lost when permanent restraints are removed

A wall, retained façade or part-built structure may be stable in its completed condition because floors, roof framing, return walls or diaphragms provide restraint. During construction, those restraints may be removed or not yet installed, leaving the same element temporarily unbraced and vulnerable to wind or out-of-plane loading. The diagram shows why temporary stability needs to be identified at design stage and resolved with an appropriate temporary works/stability strategy.

Illustrative wall-collapse incident during shallow foundation works

Simplified site diagram based on a reported West End retaining-wall collapse on 2 December 2025, where two workers were trapped while working in a shallow trench beside a retaining wall. Workplace Health and Safety Queensland later described the trench as being prepared for a slab edge beam and stated that early investigations indicated approximately 10–12 m of retaining wall and supported earth collapsed. As of 23 June 2026, Workplace Health and Safety Queensland states that the incident remains under investigation. Source: 7NEWS report; WHSQ safety alert.

Illustrative freestanding masonry wall collapse incident

Simplified site diagram based on a WorkSafe Victoria safety alert published on 8 July 2014, following a fatal brick wall collapse on a construction site in Victoria. The alert explains that freestanding masonry walls can become unstable when the permanent lateral support from wall returns, floor framing or roof structure has not yet been installed, and stresses the need for temporary bracing and exclusion zones during construction. Source: WorkSafe Victoria — Danger of freestanding masonry walls.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by regulator alerts and safety reporting showing that freestanding or temporarily unsupported masonry walls have collapsed where final lateral restraints were missing, not yet installed or removed (SafeWork NSW, corroborated by WorkSafe Victoria), together with safe-design and temporary-works guidance placing the recognition and communication step in the designer’s lane (NSW Safe Design of Structures Code with the WHS Regulation 295 duty; Temporary Works Forum Australia), and a responsibility precedent that an engineer’s generic bracing note can be insufficient (ASI TN002). It sits at medium rather than high because the fatal-incident evidence is predominantly masonry rather than RC-specific and there is no dataset quantifying how often the dependency is missed; the entry rates the duty to identify and communicate the dependency, not any claim about frequency or about builders lacking knowledge. It does not require the permanent designer to design the temporary bracing.

relatedEntries.note: Both turn on a verifiability gap and a design-versus-install responsibility split — a hazard that can fall between parties.
Both turn on construction-stage behaviour differing from the completed-condition behaviour the design was checked for.

items.name: Formwork and falsework complexity

data.question: Does the permanent design keep the formwork and falsework it requires as simple to build and remove as the structure allows?
data.parameter: Whether the design minimises avoidable formwork and falsework complexity.
data.background:
Formwork and falsework are a temporary structure the builder has to design, erect, load and remove — and they routinely carry wet concrete, reinforcement, plant and people before the permanent element can support itself. The permanent design largely sets how big and complicated that temporary structure has to be. Every extra beam, set-down, upstand, deep edge, cantilever, transfer or one-off geometry becomes formwork that must be fabricated, propped, accessed, loaded and then removed, often at height.

Simpler formwork is not only cheaper to cycle; it is safer. Formwork is the dominant on-site activity in a concrete frame: Löfgren et al. (2001) attribute roughly 49% of concrete-structure labour to formwork against about 11% to concrete operations, so it is where worker exposure concentrates, and reducing its extent and complexity reduces that exposure. Fewer and more repetitive formwork conditions mean fewer complex temporary structures to assemble and brace, less awkward work at height around congested soffits, easier removal, and fewer points of failure. Safe Work Australia’s formwork and falsework guidance makes the design link directly: designers of the permanent structure can reduce risk by considering alternative designs, minimising columns and cantilevered floor sections, reducing variation in floor depth, and allowing sufficient clearance and safe access for moving large or heavy components.

Complexity here is something the designer can read straight off the drawings: beam and column count, floor-depth variation, cantilevers, deep edges, one-off geometry, and the access and lifting clearance around them. The number of unique beam depths, set-downs, upstands, cantilevers and transfers a design forces is a direct count of bespoke formwork conditions, which is why the parameter, though categorical, can be assessed quantitatively at design stage.

This entry does not ask the permanent works engineer to design the formwork or falsework — that is the contractor’s or temporary-works engineer’s role. It asks whether the permanent design has avoided imposing avoidable temporary-works complexity, has left room to build, and has communicated any unusual construction-stage support, loading or removal condition it creates rather than leaving it to be discovered on site.

thresholds.guidance: The design uses repetitive, regular geometry and has minimised beams, columns, floor-depth variations and cantilevers where the structure allows. Soffits and edges can be formed, braced, accessed and removed with standard equipment, and there is adequate clearance and lifting access. Any unusual support, loading or removal condition is identified, and temporary support is required to remain on a condition basis (until the supported element and permanent load path are complete and concrete strength is verified) rather than on a fixed time.

The design imposes formwork and falsework complexity that the structure does not obviously require — multiple one-off conditions, frequent floor-depth changes, deep or awkward edges, congested or hard-to-access soffits — without any sign that a simpler alternative was considered. Or a construction-stage loading or support dependency, such as backpropping cascading through partly-cured floors below the pour, is present but the drawings are silent on it.

The geometry or sequence forces formwork or falsework that is impractical to access, brace or remove safely, or that imposes a credible overload or collapse condition on the temporary works or on partly-cured concrete — and nothing in the design identifies it.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies where a reinforced concrete design — slabs, beams, walls, columns or frames — requires formwork and falsework to be built, loaded and removed during construction. It does not require the permanent works engineer to design the formwork or falsework, or to take over the contractor’s temporary-works design, checking, certification, inspection or permits to load; those remain temporary-works responsibilities.

checks.title: Was a simpler option weighed
checks.description: Look for evidence that a more regular or repetitive arrangement was considered — regularised depths, fewer beams, a rationalised grid — rather than the complex geometry being adopted by default.

checks.title: Access and removal
checks.description: Check that soffits, edges and voids can be reached, braced and removed safely with standard equipment, and that there is clearance to place and move large or heavy components.

checks.title: Construction-stage loading
checks.description: Identify where the partly-built structure or its falsework will carry wet concrete, reinforcement packs, plant or stacked material, and where backpropping will pass load through partly-cured floors below the pour.

checks.title: Removal condition
checks.description: Confirm that temporary support is tied to achieved strength and a completed load path rather than to a fixed number of days.

applicability.note: This is a categorical parameter: there is no single universal threshold, and the rating turns on whether avoidable complexity was weighed and any unusual condition communicated, not on a measured count. A ‘Concern’ or ‘Action required’ result is a review prompt, not a prohibition — a complex formwork condition may still be the right answer once the full design context is weighed; the rating asks that the simpler option was considered and that any residual condition was conveyed. The permanent designer’s role is to keep the temporary works as simple as the structure allows, leave room to build and remove it, identify foreseeable construction-stage loading, and supply the loads, stability and assumed-sequence assumptions the temporary-works designer needs.

alternatives.title: Count the one-off formwork conditions
alternatives.description: Tally the unique beam depths, set-downs, upstands, deep edges, cantilevers and transfers the design forces. Each is a bespoke piece of formwork that must be fabricated, propped, accessed and removed; the count is a quick measure of how much temporary-works complexity the design is creating.

alternatives.title: Reduce avoidable complexity where the structure allows
alternatives.description: Consider fewer beams, a rationalised column grid, regularised floor depths, trimmed cantilevers and deep edges, and geometry that repeats floor-to-floor. Simpler, more repetitive formwork is faster to cycle and safer to build.

alternatives.title: Provide access and lifting clearance
alternatives.description: Leave room for falsework, props and panels to be placed, braced and removed, and for large or heavy components to be moved safely. A detail that cannot be reached or removed safely is a safety problem, not just a cost one.

alternatives.title: Flag construction-stage loading and backpropping
alternatives.description: Where the partly-built structure will carry wet concrete, reinforcement packs, plant or stacked material — or where backpropping will cascade load through partly-cured floors below the pour — identify the dependency so the loading and the affected floors can be checked or a limit communicated. Safe Work Australia’s formwork and falsework guidance sets out the related controls: no one beneath falsework while it is loaded, and back-propping verified before any support is released.

alternatives.title: State temporary-support removal as a condition, not a time
alternatives.description: Prefer ‘support to remain until the supported element and permanent load path are complete and concrete strength has been verified’ over ‘remove after 7 days’. Time-only directions can authorise removal before the permanent element can act.
