Where dismantling sequence matters, note that falsework is removed in reverse order of erection and that formwork is generally removed before falsework so material does not fall onto people below.

alternatives.title: Record unusual conditions in the safety report
alternatives.description: Where a formwork, falsework, loading or removal condition is atypical or specific to this design, record it in the designer’s safety report / safety-in-design register, consistent with the Safe Design of Structures Code and WHS Regulation 295.

data.evidence:
Simplification is a recognised designer lever. Safe Work Australia’s General Guide for Formwork and Falsework states that designers of the permanent structure can simplify the temporary works and minimise risk by considering alternative designs, minimising columns and cantilevered floor sections, reducing variation in floor depth, and allowing sufficient clearance and safe access for moving large or heavy components. The same guide sets out the construction-stage load case the temporary works must carry — dead and live loads, workers, plant, stacked material, wet concrete, point loads, prestressing-related redistribution, and erection and dismantling sequence — and lists controls including building as designed, not altering without authority, inspecting and certifying before loading, avoiding point loads, providing adequate foundations and not exceeding prop working loads. Its falsework controls also require that no one be beneath falsework while it is loaded, that dismantling proceed in reverse order of erection, that back-propping be verified before support is released, and that formwork generally be removed before falsework so material does not fall onto people below.

Why complexity is a safety issue, not only a cost one. Formwork is the dominant on-site activity in a concrete frame: Löfgren et al. (2001) attribute roughly 49% of concrete-structure labour to formwork against about 11% to concrete operations, and Jarkas (2010) measured repetition and standardisation improving formwork labour productivity. Because formwork is where most of the on-site work — and therefore most of the worker exposure — sits, reducing its extent and complexity reduces exposure. Analyses of falsework failures find that the majority occur during concrete placement (Hadipriono & Wang, 1986), the moment of greatest load and the greatest number of people at risk, which is why an unflagged construction-stage loading or removal condition is consequential.

The construction stage of concrete is genuinely hazardous. Major construction-stage concrete collapses — 2000 Commonwealth Avenue, Boston (1971) and Skyline Plaza, Bailey’s Crossroads (1973) — were driven by loading or removing support before the concrete had gained strength, not by geometric complexity as such. They are cited here to support the removal-as-a-condition point (recommended action 5): temporary support tied to achieved strength rather than elapsed time.

Formwork design and certification quality is a current concern. SafeWork NSW’s 2024 formwork safety alert recorded an increased number of inadequate installation designs and certifications, with risks including formwork collapse, falls from height and falling objects.

The communication duty sits with the designer. The NSW Safe Design of Structures Code of Practice requires designers to provide information on the conditions necessary for a structure to be without risk during construction, expressly including temporary work required to build it, and a written safety report for design-specific construction hazards under WHS Regulation 295. A 2025 CROSS report reinforces this at the temporary-works interface: the permanent works designer should make clear the loading, stability and assumed construction-sequence assumptions so the temporary-works designer can manage construction-stage risk. Engineers Australia / Temporary Works Forum Australia guidance adds that the greatest opportunity to eliminate temporary-works risk is during permanent works design.

What the evidence does and does not show. The simplification levers, the formwork and falsework load case and the falsework safety controls are stated directly in the guidance, and the dominance of formwork in concrete labour and the timing of falsework failures are measured. The evidence does not quantify how often complex permanent design leads to an incident; the link from complexity to risk is a reasoned one — more and more bespoke temporary works to erect, brace, access and remove, against a backdrop where most falsework fails during the pour. The bands are therefore recognition and communication prompts, not measured thresholds.

References
Safe Work Australia. (2014). General Guide for Formwork and Falsework.
SafeWork NSW. (2024). Formwork installation design and certification safety alert.
SafeWork NSW. Safe Design of Structures: Code of Practice; NSW Work Health and Safety Regulation, s295.
CROSS Safety. (2025). Temporary works designers not given access to permanent works calculations (Report ID 1481).
Engineers Australia / Temporary Works Forum Australia. (2020). Temporary Works Risk Guidance (TWfAU2020:01).
Löfgren, I., Gylltoft, K., & Kutti, T. (2001). In-situ concrete building – Innovations in Formwork. Chalmers University of Technology / Thomas Concrete Group.
Jarkas, A. M. (2010). Buildability factors affecting formwork labour productivity of building floors. Canadian Journal of Civil Engineering, 37(10), 1383–1394.
Hadipriono, F. C., & Wang, H. K. (1986). Analysis of causes of falsework failures in concrete structures. Journal of Construction Engineering and Management, 112(1), 112–121.
Feld, J., & Carper, K. L. (1997). Construction Failure (2nd ed.). Wiley.

evidenceSupport.summary: The designer’s simplification levers, the formwork and falsework load case and the falsework safety controls are stated directly in Safe Work Australia guidance, and are corroborated by a current SafeWork NSW alert, the Safe Design of Structures Code with WHS Regulation 295, CROSS interface reporting and Temporary Works Forum Australia guidance. The supporting magnitudes are measured: formwork is roughly 49% of concrete-structure labour (Löfgren et al., 2001) and the majority of falsework failures occur during concrete placement (Hadipriono & Wang, 1986). It sits at Medium rather than High because the band ordering is a judged application of that guidance, there is no dataset linking permanent-design complexity to incident rate, and the safety benefit of simplification is reasoned rather than measured. The entry rates the duty to minimise avoidable complexity and to communicate unusual conditions, not any claim about frequency, and does not require the permanent designer to design the temporary works.

relatedEntries.note: Overlap to resolve: that draft counts distinct formwork configurations for productivity; this entry counts them for safety. Decide which owns the ‘count of one-off conditions’ lever, or scope them explicitly as the economy view and the safety view of the same count.
A specific instance of this general principle: a deep beam side form that forces an oversized board is exactly the avoidable formwork complexity this entry asks the designer to notice.
Formwork access and bracing feasibility for a particular formed element.
Formwork quantity and cycling, and backpropping through repeated floors.
Condition-based removal of temporary support depends on achieved strength, which curing affects.
Companion safety entry: that one covers an element being unstable before its permanent restraint exists; this one covers the formwork and falsework system the permanent design relies on.

items.name: Slab edges and openings that can accept fall protection

data.question: Can fall protection be attached to the slab edges and openings as detailed?
data.parameter: Whether the edge or opening provides an attachment path for fall protection.
data.background:
Temporary fall protection — whoever supplies it and whatever the system — has to attach to something. On a concrete structure there are only three ways it can: clamp onto an edge, parapet, upstand or beam that is thick and flat enough to grip; post-fix into the concrete with drilled anchors; or mount to cast-in sockets, sleeves or ferrules placed before the pour. The contractor chooses and installs the system — that is their means and methods, and the framework has no part in it. But whether any of those three attachment paths is available is decided by the permanent design. That is the design lever, and it sits entirely on the structural drawings.

Most ordinary reinforced-concrete edges are not a problem: they can be clamped or drilled without much thought, so the cheapest and best fix is usually nothing more than an edge geometry that a standard clamp can grip — which costs the designer nothing. The entry bites in the narrower set of cases where the design has quietly removed the simple paths: thin, sloped, rebated or congested edges that will not clamp, large internal openings where there is nothing to clamp around, and — most sharply — post-tensioned slabs.

A post-tensioned edge or opening is the spine of this entry because it is where the design can remove the safe options without anyone noticing at drawing stage. Post-drilled fixings into PT slabs are not impossible — services are routinely hung off PT soffits — but drilling, coring or cutting a PT slab is a controlled penetration problem: tendon positions, drilling depth, fixing forces and engineer approval all have to be managed, and a diamond core can sever a strand easily. So post-fixed anchors should not be assumed as the ordinary fallback on a PT edge. Where a PT edge or opening cannot be clamped and no cast-in provision has been shown, the design may have left the contractor with no safe, non-improvised way to protect the edge — and the only decision that could have prevented it (a clampable edge, or cast-in anchorage) was a design-stage one.

Large openings deserve top billing alongside perimeter edges. A lift shaft, stair void or service penetration in a PT slab is often the worst case at once: you frequently cannot clamp around an internal opening, and drilling right beside it is precisely the PT problem.

This is not a request to add embedded hardware everywhere. It is a prompt to check, before the edge is formed, that an attachment path exists — and to provide one deliberately only where the design has taken the simple ones away.

thresholds.guidance: The edge or opening geometry accepts a standard clamp or bracket system, or cast-in sockets, sleeves or anchors are shown where drilling is unsafe, undesirable or likely to clash with the structural system. The designer has left a safe, non-improvised path.

Protection is workable but depends on drilling a conventional reinforced-concrete edge. Confirm edge thickness, cover, reinforcement congestion, embedment depth, edge distance and fixing loads before relying on it as the intended path.

The edge is thin, sloped, rebated, irregular or congested, or the opening offers nothing to clamp around, and no cast-in provision is shown. Protection may still be possible, but the design is pushing the attachment problem onto the contractor after the concrete is formed.

Typically a PT slab edge or PT opening edge where post-fixing should not be assumed, the geometry will not accept clamp-on protection, and no cast-in provision is shown. There is no obvious safe, non-improvised way to protect the edge as drawn.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to suspended slabs, perimeter edges and large openings where temporary fall protection will be needed during construction. It is concerned with whether the permanent edge or opening provides an attachment path for that protection — not with selecting, designing or installing the contractor’s temporary system, which is means and methods and stays with the contractor.

checks.title: The design decision, not the contractor’s system
checks.description: This entry is about whether the permanent edge or opening lets fall protection attach. Which barrier, mesh or scaffold system is chosen, and how it is installed and removed, is the contractor’s responsibility. The designer’s duty is to leave an attachment path; the contractor’s duty to select and manage the system applies regardless.

checks.title: Ordinary edges are usually fine
checks.description: A regular reinforced-concrete edge can usually be clamped or drilled without difficulty, so most edges raise no concern. The entry bites on post-tensioned edges and openings, large internal voids, and genuinely awkward edge geometry — not on every slab edge.

checks.title: Geometry first, hardware second
checks.description: The cheapest path is an edge a standard clamp can grip, which needs no embedded hardware and no drilling. Cast-in provision is the deliberate fix for where drilling is unsafe — chiefly PT — not a default to add everywhere.

checks.title: The PT position is careful, not absolute
checks.description: Post-drilled fixings into PT slabs are not forbidden; they are a controlled penetration requiring tendon location, depth control and engineer involvement. The point is that post-fixing should not be assumed as the fallback where no clamp or cast-in path has been provided.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a safety-in-design prompt. The aim is to ask whether the edge or opening lets fall protection attach without improvisation, and to provide a path where the design has removed the simple ones — not to rule out any particular edge detail.

alternatives.title: Reach for a clampable edge first
alternatives.description: The cheapest fix is geometric: an edge a standard clamp can grip needs no embedded hardware and no drilling. Resolve it by geometry before reaching for anything cast-in.

alternatives.title: On PT edges and openings, show cast-in provision
alternatives.description: Where a PT edge cannot be clamped, place cast-in sockets or sleeves clear of tendons and stressing anchorages rather than assuming the contractor will drill, and coordinate their position with the likely system early.

alternatives.title: Treat large openings and voids as the priority case
alternatives.description: Check lift shafts, stair voids and major penetrations explicitly — internal openings are where clamping fails and drilling is most constrained.

alternatives.title: If post-fixing is the only path, confirm the edge can take it
alternatives.description: Check thickness, cover, edge distance and fixing loads, and that the fixing zone does not clash with reinforcement, tendons or anchorages.

alternatives.title: Where you cannot resolve it on the drawings, flag it
alternatives.description: If an atypical edge or opening makes fall protection difficult to attach, record it as a construction-phase hazard for the builder rather than leaving it unstated.

workedExample.title: Worked example — the same check, opposite answers
workedExample.caveat: Generic archetypes for illustration, not a real project. The verdict comes from asking which of the three attachment paths the design has left open.
workedExample.headline: The same three attachment paths, opposite answers — and you can see why the call flips.
tableRows: Available — a regular square edge a standard clamp grips
Not available — slim architectural edge, nothing to clamp
Available — ordinary RC, drilled with due care
Should not be assumed — PT slab; drilling is a controlled penetration
Best practice — an attachment path is open
Action required — no safe path left as drawn
workedExample.howToUse: Run the same three checks on your own edge or opening and read the result. If any attachment path is open, the design is fine. If the geometry will not clamp and drilling is unsafe or controlled and no cast-in provision is shown, the design has removed the safe options — fix it before the edge is formed.

data.evidence:
This entry rests on design-stage safety logic and fall-prevention duties, applied to the attachment path a permanent concrete edge leaves for temporary fall protection. The band states are a judged application of that evidence to edge and opening details; they are not measured thresholds.

Why it matters — fall prevention and the hierarchy of control. Safe Work Australia’s Managing the risk of falls at workplaces Code of Practice treats falls from height as a major construction fatality category and places physical fall-prevention — guardrails, barriers, edge protection — above harness-based fall-arrest in the hierarchy of control. The preferred control is therefore the very system that has to attach to the permanent edge, which is what makes the edge a safety-in-design matter rather than a contractor-only one.

The design link — compatibility with the supporting structure. Safe Work Australia’s Safe design of structures Code of Practice places a duty on building designers and engineers to consider hazards to construction workers, and to address unusual or atypical features that present construction-phase risk by removing the hazard or controlling it. An edge or opening that cannot accept fall protection is exactly such a feature. Temporary-edge-protection system literature reinforces the mechanism directly: a system’s performance depends on the structure it attaches to and the attachment method available — clamp, post-fixed anchor, or cast-in socket — so the permanent edge either accommodates those methods or it does not.

The post-tensioned position. Post-Tensioning Institute of Australia guidance on post-cut holes through post-tensioned slabs supports the careful PT wording: drilling, coring or cutting a PT slab is a controlled operation requiring tendon location, depth control and engineer involvement, not a casual site workaround. This grounds the ‘no safe path’ band — that post-fixing should not be assumed as the fallback on a PT edge — without overstating it as a claim that PT slabs can never be drilled.

The design-influences-safety principle. Behm (2005) reviewed construction fatality investigations and found a substantial share were linked to the design-for-construction-safety concept, meaning the contributing risk could have been reduced or eliminated had construction safety been considered during design. An edge detail that leaves fall protection no attachment path is such a design-influenced construction hazard.

References
Safe Work Australia. Model Code of Practice: Managing the risk of falls at workplaces.
Safe Work Australia. Model Code of Practice: Safe design of structures.
Post-Tensioning Institute of Australia. Post Cut Holes through Post-Tensioned Slabs (GN06).
Manufacturer temporary edge-protection system literature (attachment-method evidence).
Behm, M. (2005). Linking construction fatalities to the design for construction safety concept. Safety Science, 43(8), 589–611. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ssci.2005.04.002

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by WHS fall-prevention guidance, the safe-design duty, temporary-edge-protection system literature showing how protection attaches to a structure, and PTIA guidance showing that post-drilling or cutting PT slabs is a controlled operation. The band ordering is a reasoned constructability judgement, because no peer-reviewed study was found specifically on temporary edge-protection anchorage to PT slab edges, and no universal numeric edge threshold applies.

relatedEntries.note: Both concern keeping workers safe while the structure is incomplete — check edges, openings and access together.
Where post-tensioning has been chosen, check the PT edge and opening details for a fall-protection attachment path at the same time.

supportingImages.title:
Slab edge attachment path for fall protection

A robust slab edge gives the contractor straightforward options for temporary fall protection, such as clamping or cast-in fixing. Thin PT edges, congested anchorage zones or constrained façades may still be workable, but they need early review because simple post-fixing may not be practical.

dimensions.name: Design Communication
dimensions.description: Document clarity, visualisation, and coordination across stakeholders
generalGuidance: Reinforcement callouts should be clear and concise - if a callout needs decoding, critical information may be missed
Follow consistent drawing conventions - for example, use dashed lines for elements below the section cut to avoid confusion about what is being detailed
Communicate openly with stakeholders about project deliverables and methodologies early - assumptions made in isolation often lead to constructability issues during construction

items.name: Reinforcement callout clarity

data.question: Are reinforcement bar callouts clear enough that bar size, spacing, extent, laying order and additional reinforcement can be understood without missed or misread instructions?
data.parameter: Clarity of reinforcement bar callouts, extent markers, laying order notation and extra reinforcement notes.
data.background: Reinforcement callouts need to be readable by the people fixing, checking and inspecting the reinforcement. A callout may technically contain the required information, but still be poor communication if too much information is compressed into one notation string or if extra bars are not clearly separated from the standard reinforcement. This is particularly important where additional bars are required over columns, supports, slab thickenings, penetrations, openings or other local regions. If extras are buried inside the main callout or encoded into a compressed notation, they can be missed during construction. Clear callouts should show the main reinforcement, extent and extras in a way that is obvious at a glance.

thresholds.guidance: Bar size and spacing with extent shown on plan. Laying order noted separately in brackets where helpful, e.g. N12-200 (B1). Every element is independently readable at a glance. Extras are called out separately with their own callout and extent markers, e.g. 6N16 EXTRA.
Bar size and spacing only, with no laying order context, e.g. N12-200. Still clear, but the reader does not know the stacking sequence without checking elsewhere.

Bar count included in the callout, e.g. 10N12-200. The count is unnecessary where extent markers on the drawing already communicate where bars go. Adding the count clutters the notation without adding practical value on site.

Multiple instructions are compressed into a single notation string, e.g. 13N16 10-250+3. Extras or non-standard reinforcement are buried within the notation. Critical information is easily missed and was missed during construction in a real project.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to reinforcement bar callouts on structural plans where bar size, spacing, extent, laying order and additional reinforcement need to be communicated clearly.

checks.title: Callout style may vary by office
checks.description: Different offices may use different reinforcement notation styles. This guidance does not require one exact notation system, but the callout should be readable without relying on compressed or hidden instructions.

checks.title: Extent markers remain important
checks.description: Where bars or extra bars are required, the drawing should clearly show their extent using markers, zones, leaders or similar. A clean callout is only useful if the reader can also tell where the reinforcement starts and stops.

checks.title: Layering should be clear where it matters
checks.description: Where laying order affects bar placement, the layer should be shown clearly, such as using bracketed notation like B1, rather than being buried in a compressed callout.

checks.title: Extras should be separately visible
checks.description: Where additional reinforcement is required, the extra bars should be visually separated from the standard reinforcement callout. This reduces the risk that extras are missed because they were hidden inside the main notation.

applicability.note: Use this entry as a communication clarity check, not as a rule that every office must use the exact same notation. The aim is to make reinforcement requirements obvious to the person reading the drawing on site.

alternatives.title: Separate extras into their own callouts
alternatives.description: Separate extras into their own callouts on the plan with their own extent markers. For example, show N16-250 for the standard bars and a separate callout such as 3N16 EXTRA OVER COLUMNS (TYP) with its extent clearly marked.

alternatives.title: Use typical notation for repeated extras
alternatives.description: Use TYPICAL notation so that repeated extras only need to be detailed once on the drawing rather than repeated at every location. This keeps the drawing cleaner while still making the extra reinforcement requirement clear.

alternatives.title: Add a general note for repeated spacing rules
alternatives.description: Include a general note such as all extras evenly spaced unless noted otherwise to reduce the amount of information that needs to be conveyed in individual callouts.

data.evidence:
This entry is led by practical project experience and supported by general human-factors research on visual clutter and information overload. The literature explains why dense or buried notation is easy to misread, but does not study reinforcement callouts directly, so the bands below are reasoned rather than measured.

Practical project experience. On a real project, compressed reinforcement notation caused additional reinforcement to be missed during construction. Extras encoded inside a single notation string were overlooked rather than read as a separate requirement.

Why it happens — clutter and overload. The guiding principle is that a reader should be given what they need to place the bar correctly, and little more; information beyond that adds visual competition without adding decision value. Rosenholtz, Li and Nakano (2007) show that as a display becomes more cluttered, a target item becomes harder to find — excess or disorganised items cause crowding and degrade visual search — and that displays can be designed to reduce this. The same effect explains why a low-salience item such as a small ‘+3’ of extra bars is easily missed when crowded by surrounding characters. Information-overload research points the same way: the number of information cues is the most commonly cited driver of overload, and overload increases the time taken to interpret information and the likelihood of confusion (Speier, Valacich & Vessey, 1999). A callout that compresses count, spacing, layer and extras into one string is overload at the scale of a single notation. Both bodies of work concern displays and decisions in general rather than engineering drawings, so they support the principle, not the specific notation here.

Evidence gap. No study examined here tests how reinforcement callouts in particular are read on site, especially by steel fixers, who may have no formal design training and are decoding notation quickly under site conditions. This is a genuine gap: the cognitive mechanism is well established, but its specific application to reinforcement notation rests on project experience and reasoning rather than direct evidence. The thresholds should be weighed accordingly.

References
Rosenholtz, R., Li, Y., & Nakano, L. (2007). Measuring visual clutter. Journal of Vision, 7(2):17, 1–22. https://doi.org/10.1167/7.2.17
Speier, C., Valacich, J. S., & Vessey, I. (1999). The influence of task interruption on individual decision making: An information overload perspective. Decision Sciences, 30(2), 337–360. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-5915.1999.tb01613.x

examples.caption: Poor notation: 13N16 10-250+3 combines total count, spacing, and extras into a single encoded string. The +3 extra bars over columns were missed during construction.
Clear notation: N12-200 (B1) communicates bar size, spacing, and laying order at a glance. Extras called out separately as 6N16 EXTRA with their own extent markers.

supportingImages.title:
Rosenholtz et al. (2007) - visual clutter and search difficulty
A target is easy to find among a few well-separated items (top), but much harder once the same items are crowded together (bottom). This illustrates the mechanism behind the entry: a low-salience item, such as a small extra-bar note, is easily missed when buried in a dense notation string. The figure illustrates the general principle; it is not specific to reinforcement drawings.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by drawing examples and visual-search / communication literature, but the thresholds remain partly judgement-based and would benefit from further designer and contractor feedback.

relatedEntries.note: Both shape whether the builder can read, find and trust the drawings — review the documentation set's clarity together.
Both shape whether the builder can read, find and trust the drawings — review the documentation set's clarity together.

items.name: Details and schedule references

data.question: Are details and schedule references clear enough that footing, beam or reinforcement requirements are unlikely to be misread or missed during construction?
data.parameter: Location of critical dimensions and reinforcement information relative to the section/detail and governing schedule.
data.background: Structural drawings often use details together with schedules to reduce drawing repetition. For example, a drawing may show a footing, edge beam or ground beam detail, while the actual dimensions and reinforcement are controlled by a schedule using a mark such as EB1. This approach is efficient because changes can be made in one schedule rather than across several details. However, schedule-based communication can create a constructability risk when the detail is read without the accompanying schedule. This is especially likely where multiple footing, beam or slab types are used. A reader may interpret the drawn detail as complete, miss the schedule reference, or apply the wrong scheduled dimensions or reinforcement. The preferred approach is not to remove schedules, but to show critical dimensions and reinforcement requirements in both locations where practical: on the detail and in the schedule. Where office convention requires schedule-only communication, the schedule should be made difficult to miss. Where the governing schedule is missed, or the wrong scheduled line is applied, the realistic outcome is a clarification RFI, rework, or an undocumented site change, each carrying cost and delay (Tilley, Wyatt & Mohamed, 1997).

thresholds.guidance: Critical dimensions and reinforcement requirements are shown directly on the relevant section/detail and also included in the schedule. This reduces the chance that the detail is misread while still retaining the coordination benefits of a schedule.

Critical dimensions or reinforcement requirements are shown only in a schedule and not on the section/detail. This can be acceptable under some office conventions, but it increases the chance that the detail is read without the governing schedule. Where schedule-only communication is used, the governing schedule should be shown on the same drawing page as the relevant detail or mark wherever practical.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies where structural elements are communicated using details, marks and schedules, including footings, edge beams, ground beams, slab thickenings, walls or other repeated details.

checks.title: Schedules are not the problem
checks.description: Schedules are useful and should not be removed. The issue is relying on the schedule as the only place where critical information is shown, especially when the schedule is separated from the detail or mark it controls.

checks.title: Office drafting conventions may limit what can be changed
checks.description: Some workplaces intentionally use details and schedules because it reduces drawing duplication and makes revisions easier. An individual designer may not be able to change that convention. Where this is the case, the aim should be to make the schedule reference as clear and unavoidable as possible.

checks.title: Same-page schedule preferred where direct detailing is not possible
checks.description: Where critical dimensions or reinforcement cannot be shown directly on the detail, the governing schedule should be shown on the same drawing page as the detail or mark it applies to wherever practical.

checks.title: Multiple similar marks increase risk
checks.description: The risk is higher where several similar marks are used, such as EB1, because the wrong schedule line can be applied or the drawn detail can be mistaken for the complete requirement.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a drawing coordination prompt. The goal is not to ban schedules, but to reduce the chance that a detail is constructed without the schedule information that actually controls it.

alternatives.title: Show information in both locations where possible
alternatives.description: Show critical dimensions and reinforcement directly on the section/detail as well as in the schedule. This gives the reader the information at the point of use while still retaining the schedule for coordination.

alternatives.title: Put the schedule on the same page where schedule-only is required
alternatives.description: Where office convention requires schedule-only information, place the governing schedule on the same drawing page as the relevant section/detail or mark wherever practical.

alternatives.title: Make the mark obvious
alternatives.description: Clearly identify the relevant mark, such as EB1, on the plan, detail and schedule. The reader should not need to infer which schedule row applies.

alternatives.title: Resolve unclear or conflicting information
alternatives.description: If the section/detail and schedule conflict, or if the mark is unclear, revise the documentation before construction rather than relying on site interpretation.

data.evidence:
This entry is supported by practical project experience and is consistent with construction-communication and documentation research. The literature establishes the phenomenon — that separated, unclear or incomplete documentation is a recognised, measurable source of site confusion, clarification requests and rework — but does not set the specific thresholds below, which rest on the project experience described.

Practical project experience. On a real project, a detail read without its controlling schedule was treated as complete, with the risk that the wrong scheduled dimensions or reinforcement are applied.

Cross-referencing and distributed information. In a survey of US construction professionals, Foroughi Sabzevar et al. (2025) found that confusion caused by drawings not being self-contained — distributing information across different sheets and documents — was an equal highest-ranked challenge (mean 3.3 of 5), with around 70% of respondents agreeing with at least one such challenge; written instructions that reference other documents also ranked highly (mean 3.6). Their walkthrough of a section that sends the reader chasing details across several sheets to reassemble is the same class of problem as a detail whose dimensions and reinforcement sit in a separate schedule. The study is US-based, small (29 responses) and surveyed managers rather than the trades, so it supports the general principle rather than the specific guidance here.

Downstream consequence. Tilley, Wyatt and Mohamed (1997) analysed RFIs on two nine-storey Australian projects: information clarifications dominated (about 57% and 65% of all RFIs), and insufficient information was the largest single cause (roughly 70% and 56%). RFIs carry non-value-adding delay, tying unclear or incomplete documentation to a measurable cost in clarifications and rework — though the study is from 1997, covers two projects, and concerns documentation deficiency in general rather than schedules specifically.

References
Foroughi Sabzevar, M., Gheisari, M., & Lo, J. (2025). Analyzing the pros and cons of paper-based 2D drawings in construction: a survey of U.S. construction professionals. International Journal of Construction Management, 25(4), 428–438. https://doi.org/10.1080/15623599.2024.2331863
Tilley, P. A., Wyatt, A., & Mohamed, S. (1997). Indicators of design and documentation deficiency. CSIRO Building, Construction and Engineering / Griffith University.

examples.caption: Schedule-only: a raft edge beam detail with DEPTH and WIDTH shown as placeholders and the reinforcement and sizes referred entirely to the raft footing schedule, with bearing values referred to the notes sheet. Read without the governing schedule, the detail cannot be built and the wrong scheduled line could be applied.

Critical information shown on the detail: edge and internal beam sections give depth, width, cover, mesh (SL82) and tie size and spacing (R10 at 600 crs) directly on the section, readable at the point of use. A schedule can still be retained alongside it for coordination.

supportingImages.title:
Foroughi Sabzevar et al. (2025) - chasing details across sheets
A section that refers the reader to details on several other sheets, which must then be located and mentally reassembled. This is the same class of problem as a detail whose dimensions and reinforcement are held in a separate schedule.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by practical drawing examples and design-communication logic, but the evidence base is mainly practice-based and would benefit from further external literature or user feedback.

relatedEntries.note: Both shape whether the builder can read, find and trust the drawings — review the documentation set's clarity together.

items.name: Drawing clarity for isolated large slab reinforcement

data.question: Where 20 mm or larger bars are used locally in slab reinforcement, are those larger bars made visually obvious on the drawings, or are they hidden within the typical slab reinforcement notation?
data.parameter: Drawing clarity of isolated slab reinforcement bars 20 mm diameter and larger.
data.background:
The related detailing entry recommends using 12 mm and 16 mm slab bars where structurally practical, with 20 mm and larger bars reserved for locations where the larger diameter is genuinely required. The related logistics entry explains why larger bars have lower on-site adaptability if bending is required. This entry covers the drawing-communication consequence of using those larger bars.

In many slab plans, the general reinforcement may be 12 mm or 16 mm bars, with a small number of 20 mm, 24 mm or larger bars added at local supports, upstands, thickenings, transfer zones, penetrations or concentrated-load areas. These larger bars may be structurally important, but because they are local, they can be easy to miss if they are drawn in the same visual style as the surrounding typical reinforcement.

A large bar that is structurally important should not be visually treated like ordinary background reinforcement. If the drawing relies only on the reader noticing a different bar diameter in dense plan notation, the larger bars can be overlooked during checking, ordering or fixing. This is especially risky where there is no supporting section or local detail showing the larger bars.

The design issue is not whether the larger bars are required. If 20 mm, 24 mm or larger bars are needed, they should be used. The constructability issue is whether the drawing makes those bars obvious enough that they are not missed or confused with the typical slab reinforcement.

thresholds.guidance: Isolated 20 mm or larger slab bars are made visually distinct from the surrounding typical reinforcement. The drawing makes their size, quantity, spacing, extent and location reasonably clear using a separate callout, heavier lineweight, bold note, colour, hatch, shaded band, bracket, bar mark, local section or detail. This is acceptable because the larger bars are easier to identify during checking, ordering and fixing.

Isolated 20 mm or larger bars are shown in the reinforcement notation, but they are not visually distinct from the surrounding typical slab reinforcement. The larger bars may be structurally important, but the drawing presentation makes them easy to miss or confuse with the normal slab bar set. Review the drawing and make the larger bars more obvious.

alternatives.title: Make isolated larger bars visually distinct
alternatives.description: Where 20 mm or larger bars occur locally within otherwise typical slab reinforcement, make them visually stand out from the surrounding bar set. Use a separate colour, heavier lineweight, bold callout, shaded band, hatch, bracket, bar mark or local detail to show that these are not typical slab bars. Do not rely only on the reader noticing a different bar diameter buried in normal reinforcement notation. Where colour is used, do not rely on colour alone. The drawing should still be clear when printed in black and white, so pair the colour with a label, bar mark, bold note, hatch, bracket or heavier line.

alternatives.title: Show the extent of the larger bars
alternatives.description: Show exactly where the larger bars start and stop. Use a clear line, band, shaded zone, bracket, bar mark or local detail to define the extent. Do not leave the limits of the larger bars to be inferred from surrounding notation or from a dense reinforcement note.

alternatives.title: Separate typical and non-typical reinforcement
alternatives.description: Where the slab has typical 12 mm or 16 mm reinforcement plus local 20 mm or larger bars, separate the typical reinforcement note from the local larger-bar callout. The drawing should make it obvious which bars are typical and which bars are special or local.

alternatives.title: Add a section or local detail where the plan view is not enough
alternatives.description: Where larger bars occur at an upstand, support, transfer zone, penetration, step, thickening or edge condition, provide a section or local detail where needed. A section can make the larger bars much harder to miss, especially where the plan view is visually busy.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies where a slab is generally reinforced with 12 mm or 16 mm bars but includes local 20 mm, 24 mm or larger bars.

checks.title: Local larger bars are the target
checks.description: This entry is most relevant where the larger bars occur only in selected support bands, upstands, local thickenings, penetration trimming, transfer zones, concentrated-load zones, balconies, edge strips or similar local areas.

checks.title: Not a rule against larger bars
checks.description: This entry does not say larger bars should be avoided. The question of whether larger bars are needed belongs in the related detailing entry. This entry only asks whether the larger bars are communicated clearly once they are used.

checks.title: Whole-slab larger-bar layouts are different
checks.description: This entry is less relevant where the entire slab is consistently detailed with larger bars and the bar size is already obvious from the typical reinforcement note. The main risk is isolated larger bars being visually lost inside a smaller-bar slab layout.

checks.title: Plan views may need support from sections
checks.description: A plan view may technically show the bars but still fail to make them obvious. Where the plan is dense or the bars occur at a local section condition, a section or local detail may be the clearest way to communicate the requirement.

applicability.note: Use this entry as a drawing-communication check. The aim is to make isolated larger slab bars obvious enough for checking, ordering and fixing, not to prescribe one office's exact drafting style.

data.evidence:
This entry is based on drawing-communication logic and practical project examples. Isolated larger bars are more likely to be missed when they are shown in the same visual style as surrounding typical slab reinforcement, particularly where the plan is dense and no supporting section or local detail is provided.

Practical project examples. The concern example shows additional N16 and N20 bars in a slab plan where the main reinforcement is generally N12. The additional larger bars are shown, but they are visually similar to the surrounding notation and are not reinforced by a local section. This makes them easier to overlook. The acceptable example shows larger N24 reinforcement in a dedicated local section. The detail is centred on the larger bars, so they are more obvious to the reader.

Communication mechanism. This entry is consistent with the same communication principle used in the related reinforcement-callout clarity entry: important non-typical reinforcement should not be hidden inside dense or visually similar notation. Visual clutter and information overload make target information harder to find. A small local N20 or N24 callout can be structurally important even if it is visually low-salience on a busy slab plan.

Connection to related entries. The related detailing entry explains why 20 mm and larger bars should be reserved for where they are genuinely required. The related logistics entry explains why those larger bars have lower site adaptability if bending is needed. This communication entry adds the documentation control: when larger bars are used locally, make them visually distinct enough that they are not missed.

Recommendation supported by the examples. The recommendation is not to ban local larger bars. It is to make them clearer: use separate callouts, heavier lineweight, bold notes, colour with a black-and-white backup, hatching, brackets, bar marks, shaded bands, sections or local details where needed.

References
Rosenholtz, R., Li, Y., & Nakano, L. (2007). Measuring visual clutter. Journal of Vision, 7(2):17, 1–22. https://doi.org/10.1167/7.2.17
Speier, C., Valacich, J. S., & Vessey, I. (1999). The influence of task interruption on individual decision making: An information overload perspective. Decision Sciences, 30(2), 337–360. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-5915.1999.tb01613.x

examples.caption: Additional N16 and N20 bars shown in plan, but easy to miss. This slab plan includes additional 2N16 and 3N20 bars within a slab that is otherwise mainly N12 reinforcement. The larger bars are shown, but they are presented in a similar visual style to the surrounding notation and are not supported by a local section or visual emphasis. Because they are isolated and structurally important, they could be overlooked during checking, ordering or fixing.

Isolated N24 bars shown clearly in a local section. This section is focused on the larger bars, so the 2N24 and 4N24 reinforcement is reasonably clear and not buried only in the general slab plan. This is an acceptable communication approach because the larger bars are shown in a dedicated detail. However, the bars could still be made more visually distinct using a bold callout, heavier lineweight, bar mark, colour, hatch or other visual emphasis.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by practical drawing examples and communication logic, with indirect support from visual-clutter and information-overload literature. The threshold remains judgement-based because this is a drawing-clarity issue rather than a measured numerical limit.

relatedEntries.note: Companion Detailing entry. Decide there whether 20 mm and larger slab bars are genuinely required; this entry covers how to make those larger bars obvious when they are used locally.

Companion Logistics entry. Larger bars have lower on-site bending adaptability, so isolated larger bars should be made clear enough that they are not missed, substituted or treated as typical reinforcement.
Both entries concern reinforcement information being missed when important bars or extras are buried in dense or visually similar notation.

items.name: Isometric overview views in structural drawing sets

data.question: Where the overall structure is hard to grasp from the 2D drawings alone, does the set include an isometric overview to orient the builder?
data.parameter: Inclusion of an isometric overview where the 2D set is spatially hard to read.
data.background:
Structural drawings are normally issued as plans, sections, schedules and details, because those views are efficient for the information a builder must have exactly right: dimensions, levels, reinforcement, member marks, laps, cover and notes. They are, and should remain, the controlling source of construction information.

The constructability issue sits one step before that. Before any of those drawings can be used confidently, the builder has to form a three-dimensional picture of what is being built. Where the structure is simple, planar or repetitive, a well-keyed 2D set does this on its own. But where levels change, elements overlap in plan, staging matters, or several structural systems meet, the reader is left to assemble the whole structure in their head from several separate sheets before the detail makes sense. A simple isometric overview acts as a map: it shows the form, the major elements, the levels and the construction zones in a way that can be taken in quickly, while the 2D drawings continue to carry the exact dimensions and requirements.

How much this costs depends on how the set was produced, and that should guide the call. Where the structure has been modelled in a 3D environment — for example Revit, where a model is built and then turned into 2D sheets — an isometric is essentially another view of a model that already exists, and can be produced at little or no extra cost. This is not BIM: the set is still issued as printed 2D drawings, and the model is used only as a convenient way to generate a static orientation view. Nothing here asks anyone to issue, coordinate in, or hand over a model. Where a 3D model already exists and the structure has any real spatial complexity, there is little reason to leave the overview out. Where the set is simple enough to draft as a few 2D diagrams, an overview is rarely needed. The case that genuinely needs judgement is a complex structure drafted in 2D, where a clean isometric is real extra work and should be reserved for where it materially helps the reader.

The view is an orientation aid, not a dimensioned source of truth. Its job is to help the reader understand the structure and find the right plan, section or detail — not to instruct construction on its own.

thresholds.guidance: Either the structure is simple, repetitive or clearly communicated by well-keyed plans and sections (an overview is optional), or one or more simple isometric overviews are provided where the arrangement would not otherwise be obvious. Where the structure has been modelled in 3D, an overview is included as a near-zero-cost view off the existing model.

The arrangement, levels, staging or interfaces require the builder to mentally assemble several plans, sections and details, and no overview is provided — particularly where the structure was modelled in 3D and an overview could have been generated at little cost. Add a simple isometric overview or zone view.

The overall build, construction sequence, level relationships or system interfaces are unlikely to be understood correctly from the printed set as issued. Provide one or more isometric or staged overview views before issue.

alternatives.title: Add a simple isometric overview, sized to orient not to instruct
alternatives.description: Show the overall structural form, the main levels, the major walls, beams, slabs, columns, pile caps and retaining elements, and the key construction zones. Keep it clean and diagrammatic. Do not load it with dimensions or reinforcement notation, which belong in the 2D set.

alternatives.title: Where the structure is already modelled in 3D, include the overview by default
alternatives.description: If the set has been produced from a 3D model (for example Revit), an orientation view is nearly free to generate. Unless the structure is so simple that a view adds nothing, include one. The model is used only to produce a static printed view, not issued or coordinated in.

alternatives.title: Choose views that show how things relate, not each piece in isolation
alternatives.description: An isometric earns its value by showing vertical relationships and interfaces: how levels stack, what sits below or above, how systems meet. A whole-structure overview, or a small set of zone or per-level views where one view would be too busy, is usually more useful than a flat view of a single simple level. For a multi-level building modelled in 3D, an overview of each level or key zone is a low-cost, high-clarity option.

alternatives.title: Make the overview a map with callouts, not a picture
alternatives.description: Label the major elements and reference the relevant plans, sections and details on the isometric, so the view routes the reader to where the controlling information lives. An overview that does not cross-reference is decoration; one that points to the right sheet is doing the work.

alternatives.title: Keep all dimensions and reinforcement in the 2D views
alternatives.description: Do not rely on the isometric for exact levels, bar sizes, laps, cover or setout unless they are also shown in the controlling plans, sections or schedules.

alternatives.title: Use a staged isometric sequence where construction order affects understanding
alternatives.description: Where temporary stability, demolition, pour staging, retained walls, underpinning or access constraints affect the work, a short staged isometric sequence communicates the sequence more directly than separate plans.

alternatives.title: Add the orientation-only note
alternatives.description: Include a standard note near the view: "Isometric view is diagrammatic and for orientation only. Refer to plans, sections, schedules and details for dimensions, levels, reinforcement and construction requirements." This note is what makes the view safe to issue in a contractual set and stops a reader treating a simplified isometric as complete.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies where the overall structural arrangement is not immediately clear from the 2D drawings alone: multiple structural levels, setdowns, folds or steps; retaining walls, ground beams, slabs, pits, pile caps or deep beams overlapping in plan; construction staging affecting understanding; multiple systems meeting (concrete, steel, blockwork, permanent formwork, bored or screw piles); waterproofing, rebates or joints depending on three-dimensional relationships; or the builder needing to understand the overall form before the dimensions make sense. It does not apply where the structure is simple, planar, repetitive or already clearly communicated by the 2D drawings. This is not a rule to always add a 3D view.

checks.title: The drafting method is a practical proxy
checks.description: If the structure is complex enough to be modelled in 3D (or drafted as anything beyond simple 2D diagrams), an overview is both cheap to produce and likely to help. If it is simple enough for a few 2D diagrams, it usually is not needed. The only effortful case is a complex structure drafted in 2D, where the overview should be reserved for where it materially helps.

checks.title: This is not BIM
checks.description: The set is still issued as printed 2D drawings. A 3D model, where one exists, is only a convenient way to generate one static orientation view. No model issue, coordination or handover is implied.

checks.title: The target is the overall arrangement
checks.description: This entry is about understanding the spatial arrangement before hunting for detailed information. Whether an individual congested detail is clear is a different question.

checks.title: It must not imply completeness
checks.description: A clean 3D view can look authoritative while omitting critical information. It must be framed as orientation only, or it can create false confidence, where a reader assumes something not shown does not exist.

checks.title: Simple and repetitive structures are out of scope
checks.description: Adding an overview where the structure already reads easily creates clutter and weakens the entry's credibility.

applicability.note: Use this as a drawing-communication review prompt, not a drafting-style mandate. The aim is that a reader can understand the build quickly, not to prescribe how an office draws an isometric.

data.evidence:
Drawing standards and practice already recognise the role. Australian infrastructure drawing guidance (Transport for NSW's engineering drawing and CAD requirements) recognises that isometric views, photographs or rendered sections may be added to improve the clarity of an engineering feature. The Royal Architectural Institute of Canada's handbook of practice notes that construction documentation is traditionally orthographic — plans, elevations and sections — but that axonometric, isometric and perspective views are included where they better illustrate complex arrangements. The throughline is consistent: keep the orthographic set as the exact layer and add a pictorial view where it clarifies the arrangement. This entry applies that established split to ordinary structural sets.

The comprehension mechanism is supported by construction-information research. A recent survey of construction professionals on paper-based 2D drawings finds they remain widely used and are valued for needing no software or power and for being portable, but that a majority also report weaknesses around cross-referencing and dependence on other documents, which can lead to misinterpretation. Earlier work notes that individual 2D drawings often have to be combined and decoded by the reader, and that this is magnified when one element must be understood across several sheets — the mental-assembly burden this entry targets. Experimental work on craft-worker performance found that readers with lower spatial cognition performed worse with 2D information alone, and that supplementing it with 3D information reduced or removed that gap, so a clearer spatial representation makes the set more robust across a wider range of readers rather than only a convenience for strong spatial reasoners.

Limit on the evidence. The experimental studies are on pipefitting and MEP assembly, not reinforced-concrete structural sets, so the mechanism transfers but the exact performance figures do not. The standards establish that isometric views are permitted and useful for clarity, not when they become necessary, so the trigger of necessity remains a reasoned constructability judgement, which is why this is a Medium-support entry rather than high. The cost asymmetry between 3D-modelled and 2D-drafted sets is sound practitioner reasoning that strengthens the recommendation, but it is not established by the cited sources and so lives in guidance rather than in the evidence claim.

References
Transport for NSW. T MU MD 00006 ST — Engineering Drawings and CAD Requirements.
Royal Architectural Institute of Canada. Canadian Handbook of Practice for Architects — Appendix D: Drawings.
Foroughi Sabzevar, M., Gheisari, M., & Lo, J. (2024). Analyzing the pros and cons of paper-based 2D drawings in construction: a survey of U.S. construction professionals. International Journal of Construction Management. https://doi.org/10.1080/15623599.2024.2331863
Goodrum, P. M., Miller, J., Sweany, J., & Alruwaythi, O. (2016). Influence of the format of engineering information and spatial cognition on craft-worker performance. Journal of Construction Engineering and Management, 142(9). https://doi.org/10.1061/(ASCE)CO.1943-7862.0001157
Dadi, G. B., Goodrum, P. M., Taylor, T. R. B., & Maloney, W. F. (2014). Effectiveness of communication of spatial engineering information through 3D CAD and 3D printed models. Visualization in Engineering, 2(9). https://doi.org/10.1186/s40327-014-0009-8

examples.caption: 2D-only set requires mental assembly. The building has multiple retaining levels, ground beams, pits and setdowns. The plans and sections are each individually correct, but the reader must jump between many sheets to understand how the structure fits together, with no overview provided. A simple isometric overview would explain the build before the details are used for dimensions.

Isometric overview supports the 2D set. One simple isometric overview shows the key levels, the main elements and callouts to the relevant plans and sections, with a note stating the view is diagrammatic. It gives a quick mental model of the structure and points the reader back to the controlling drawings without replacing them.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by Australian and international drawing-documentation guidance that recognises isometric and axonometric views for clarifying complex arrangements, and by construction-information research showing 2D-only documentation increases cross-referencing and spatial-interpretation burden while 3D supplementation helps lower-spatial-cognition readers. The direction of the call is well-founded, but the trigger for when a structural set requires an overview remains a reasoned judgement, and the experimental evidence is from pipefitting rather than RC structural drawings, so the entry is a Medium-support review prompt rather than a hard rule.

relatedEntries.note: Companion Design Communication entry. Both concern whether the reader can connect information across the set; the overview is the wayfinding layer that routes them to the right detail and section.
Both prevent important information being technically present on the sheet but easy to miss.
Where staged isometrics are used, this Safety-in-Design entry is why the construction sequence matters.

guide.title: How to use this framework
guide.tagline: New here? Start with this. What the framework is, how to read an entry, and how the ratings are meant to be used.
guide.intro: Use this framework in two ways. First, as a searchable library of constructability entries: browse or search for a design decision and read the relevant guidance, thresholds, examples and evidence. Second, as a project review tool: save relevant entries to a project review list and export them as a project-specific constructability checklist. Use the framework to inform your own engineering judgement — not as fixed answers, but as prompts for thinking through how your design will actually be built.

whatIs.title: What is this framework?
whatIs.intro: Structural designers operate under two parallel bodies of obligation: compliance and professional duty. This framework helps with professional duty. It does not help you check codes, interpret Australian Standards, or determine whether a design is structurally adequate. It helps designers think about whether what they have drawn can be built safely and practically, and it now supports both single-entry review and project-specific constructability checklists. Just as importantly, the framework is a starting point for discussion rather than a settled rulebook. Its aim is to put practical constructability ideas into the open, rate them honestly, and encourage the field measurements, studies and literature that can test and refine them — bringing construction and design closer together.

columns.heading: Compliance
columns.foot: Clearly defined and enforceable — and a design can be checked against the codes.

card.label: Compliance software
card.text: Software already exists to check whether a design satisfies standards and codes. The compliance side of the job is well supported by established tools and processes — this is not what the framework does.

columns.heading: Professional duty
items: WHS Act — designer duty for construction-stage risk
columns.foot: Principles, not decision-point tools — until now there has been little that turns this duty into specific design-stage guidance.

card.label: This framework
card.text: Translates construction knowledge into design-stage guidance so professional duty can be considered at the decision point. It helps designers think about how a design will be built — not whether it complies.

whatIs.caption: Compliance tells you what the design must be. The framework helps with the other half of the job: whether what you have drawn can be built safely and practically.

dimensionsGuide.title: The five dimensions
dimensionsGuide.intro: The framework is organised by the root cause of the constructability issue. An issue may affect several parts of construction, but each entry is placed under the dimension that best explains the design decision that created it.

items.rootCause: The design assumes a method, or system that may not suit the project.
items.question: Has the design assumed a method that may not actually be feasible?

items.rootCause: The issue comes from the shape, layout or complexity of the detail.
items.question: Is the constructability problem caused by the detail itself?

items.rootCause: The issue comes from procurement, site access or equipment constraints.
items.question: Can the required materials, equipment and access be realistically provided?

items.rootCause: The design creates an avoidable safety risk during construction.
items.question: Could this design create a safety risk for the people who build, access, inspect or work around it?

items.rootCause: The issue comes from unclear drawings, notation or references.
items.question: Could the design intent be missed or misunderstood?

dimensionsGuide.note: Many issues touch more than one dimension. Use the dimension to ask: what design decision caused the constructability issue?

judgement.title: Read this first — how the ratings are meant to be used

points.text: Best practice, Consider, Concern and Action required are prompts for design-stage evaluation. An Action required rating does not necessarily mean the design cannot proceed — it means recognise the constructability challenge, consider the alternatives, and make an informed decision for your project. Sometimes the flagged detail is still the most practical solution when weighed against the full design context.
Thresholds reflect typical conditions and conventional practice. Your project may justify a different conclusion. The framework informs the decision; it does not make it for you.

Each entry has an Evidence support rating: High, Medium or Low. This rating describes how much supporting information sits behind the entry, not whether the issue is important or whether the recommendation must be followed. High-support entries are backed by a stronger mix of sources, examples or direct evidence. Low-support entries are still useful prompts, but they are more likely to benefit from further evidence, project examples, regional feedback or review. You can also open the Evidence Support Index from the home page to see which entries are strongly supported and which are still early-stage prompts.

points.lead: Check applicability before acting on a rating.
points.text: Most entries list where they apply and what to confirm first. Read the Applicability and Limitations section before treating a rating as settled.

Each entry is tagged with the location where its evidence was gathered. That is a record of provenance, not a claim that it applies only there. Judge relevance to your own context, and use the region filter within each dimension.

ratingLegend.title: The four ratings
ratingLegend.intro: Every entry rates your design against four levels. The colours are consistent across the whole framework.

items.meaning: The recommended approach for typical conditions. No constructability concern expected.
Generally acceptable, but worth a second look against your specific conditions.
Likely to create constructability difficulty. Review it and weigh the alternatives.
A constructability challenge to deliberately address before construction. A prompt to evaluate and decide — not an automatic prohibition.

anatomy.title: Anatomy of an entry
anatomy.intro: Every entry follows the same layout. Below is a real entry from the framework — Off-form concrete upturns near obstructions — with each field labelled. These are the live entry components, not screenshots, so they always match what you see in the framework itself.

fields.label: Design Question
fields.caption: The specific question the entry answers. If this is your question, the entry is relevant to your decision.

fields.label: Design Parameter
fields.caption: The project-specific condition checked by the entry. It may be measured from drawings, confirmed from site conditions, selected by the designer, checked with suppliers, or judged from the documentation.

fields.label: Design Guidance
fields.caption: Threshold bands or described states that rate the project condition as Best practice, Consider, Concern or Action required. This is the heart of the entry.

fields.label: Applicability and Limitations
fields.caption: Where the entry applies, what to check before relying on it, and a reminder that the rating is a review prompt — not a rule.

fields.label: Recommended Actions
fields.caption: Practical options to consider if you land in Concern or Action required. A starting point for the decision, not a fixed instruction.

anatomy.moreFields: Entries may also include a Background (why the issue matters), an Evidence Basis (how the guidance was established, with references), and Evidence Support (a Low, Medium or High indicator showing how much supporting information sits behind the entry). Entries may also include Examples and Supporting Images — real, de-identified drawings and site photographs showing the issue in practice. Some entries also link to Related Entries — other entries worth considering alongside this one. At the bottom of each live entry you can add the entry to a project review list, suggest a change, or add a reviewed comment.

trace.title: How an entry works: condition → band → response
trace.intro: Every entry runs the same way. Identify the relevant project condition, compare it with the threshold bands or described states, read the rating, and decide the appropriate response. Using the off-form upturn entry as a worked example:

steps.label: Project condition
steps.sub: measured between the upturn face and the nearest boundary or permanent obstruction

steps.label: Falls in band
steps.sub: compare the condition with the threshold list

steps.label: Rating
steps.sub: coil ties can't be used; threaded bar only, tightened at arm's length

steps.label: Response
steps.sub: go to Recommended Actions — permanent formwork, blockwork, reposition, or precast

shapes.title: The condition is not always a number
shapes.intro: The logic is always the same — condition → band → response — but the condition checked by an entry can come from different places. It might be measured from drawings, confirmed from site conditions, selected by the designer, checked with suppliers, or judged from the documentation.

items.desc: You read a number from the design documentation (here, bar spacing in mm) and find the range it falls in.
You compare a specified choice rather than a number — here, the bar type selected for the tie.
There is no number at all — you assess the documentation against described states, from clearest to least clear.

items.q: Does an 'Action required' rating mean I can't use the detail?
items.a: No. An Action required rating means the design decision should be deliberately reviewed before construction. It does not automatically mean the detail is prohibited or cannot proceed. In some cases, the flagged detail may still be the most practical solution when considered against the full project context.

items.q: Does this help me understand design codes or Australian Standards?
items.a: No. This framework is not a code guide or compliance tool. It does not explain AS 3600 or determine whether a design satisfies Australian Standards. It is design guidance for the professional-duty side of engineering: helping designers recognise how design decisions may affect construction safety, practicality, logistics, methods and communication.

items.q: Do all entries have the same level of evidence behind them?
items.a:
No. Entries are informed by different combinations of literature, supplier information, project outcomes, industry input and site experience. The Evidence support rating gives a quick indication of how much supporting information sits behind an entry: High, Medium or Low.

This rating does not say whether the constructability issue is important, and it does not replace engineering judgement. It simply shows how mature the evidence base is. A low-support entry may still be a useful design prompt, but it is a stronger candidate for further review, added examples, regional feedback or stronger references. Inside each entry, read the Evidence Basis and Evidence Support note to understand how the guidance was derived.

In practice, Medium is the level most live entries are expected to sit at, and that is by design. Reaching High generally means an entry’s specific thresholds or recommended call have been directly measured, derived, or matched to an authoritative source on that exact point — which is the exception rather than the norm. A Medium entry is a sound, actionable idea backed by real reasoning and at least one relevant source; it is also an open invitation for the field measurements, studies or project examples that could lift it to High.

items.q: Does the framework help with constructability or buildability?
items.a: Both terms are relevant. Buildability usually refers to how readily a design can be physically built. Constructability is broader: it includes buildability, but also considers how construction knowledge is integrated into design decisions, including safety, sequencing, logistics, methods, documentation and project constraints. This framework uses constructability because it is concerned with more than whether a detail can be built — it is concerned with whether design decisions support safe, practical and efficient construction.

items.q: An entry is tagged 'NSW, Australia'. Can I use it elsewhere?
items.a: Yes, but use judgement. The location tag records where the evidence or practical knowledge behind the entry was gathered. It is provenance, not a restriction. Material availability, labour practices, equipment access and construction conventions vary between regions, so users should judge whether the guidance is relevant to their own project context.

items.q: How is this different from BIM or AI design tools?
items.a: BIM and AI tools can support coordination, modelling, checking, optimisation and faster design production. This framework works earlier: it helps the engineer question the design decision before it becomes locked in. It asks whether the chosen detail, method, material or assumption is appropriate for the way the structure will actually be built. The focus is the decision itself, not only the review of a developing model.

items.q: What is the project review list for?
items.a: The project review list lets you collect framework entries that are relevant to a particular project. Instead of reading entries one by one and losing track of them, you can save the checks you want to apply and export them as a project-specific constructability review checklist.

items.q: Is my project review list public?
items.a: No. The project review list is stored locally in your browser. It does not require login and is not published to the framework. If you clear your browser data or use a different device, the list may not be there.

items.q: In the project review list, what is the project purpose field for?
items.a: The project purpose field appears in the exported PDF/checklist and can be edited to suit the project or audience. It explains why the selected entries are being reviewed and helps frame the export as a constructability review aid rather than a compliance certificate or formal sign-off.

items.q: What is the difference between a comment and a suggested change?
items.a: A comment is a practical observation, question or regional note that may be shown beneath an entry. A suggested change is a request to alter the entry itself — for example by correcting wording, changing a threshold, adding evidence, or improving a recommended action.

No. Comments are reviewed and de-identified before they appear. This keeps the framework useful and protects contributors, projects, clients and designers from being identified through submitted examples or observations.

items.q: Can I suggest a new entry or a change to an existing one?
items.a: Yes. You can suggest a new entry where the framework does not yet cover an issue, suggest a change where an existing entry needs correction or improvement, or add a comment where you want to share a practical observation or regional note without changing the entry itself. Suggestions should be de-identified and should include enough context to explain the design issue, the condition being checked, the recommended response, and any supporting evidence or example.

items.q: Are suggested entries, edits or comments added automatically?
items.a: No. Suggested entries, suggested changes and comments are reviewed before they are added to the framework. Submissions need to be relevant to constructability, de-identified, and clear enough to understand the design issue or feedback. Nothing submitted through a form appears on the site automatically.

items.q: Why show real drawings or site photographs? Isn't that sensitive?
items.a: Real examples make constructability issues easier to understand, but they are included as learning examples, not criticism of any designer, contractor, project or client. Project names, company names, site locations and identifying information should be removed before examples are included in the framework.

projectListWorkflow.title: Using the framework on a project
projectListWorkflow.intro: The framework can be used as more than a reference library. When you are reviewing a real project, you can collect the entries that matter to that job into a private project review list and export them as a project-specific constructability review checklist.

steps.label: Find relevant entries
steps.sub: Use the search box, dimension pages and region filter to find entries that match the design decisions on your project.

steps.label: Build a project review list
steps.sub: Open a relevant entry and add it to the project review list. The list stays in your browser and does not require login.

steps.label: Describe the review
steps.sub: Give the list a project name and edit the project purpose field so the exported checklist explains what the review is being used for.

steps.label: Use the output
steps.sub: Export the selected entries as a checklist or PDF. The output is a constructability review aid, not a compliance certificate or formal sign-off.

projectListWorkflow.note: The project review list is intended to turn selected framework entries into a project-specific constructability review checklist. It is local to the browser, temporary by nature, and should be treated as a prompt for design review rather than proof that a project complies with any standard.

contributing.title: Contributing feedback
contributing.intro: The framework is designed to improve over time. Use the contribution pathway that matches what you are trying to do.

items.label: Suggest a new entry
items.text: Use this when the framework does not yet cover the constructability issue. A useful suggestion describes the design decision, the project condition that triggers the issue, and any practical example or evidence behind it.

items.label: Suggest a change
items.text: Use this when an existing entry should be corrected, clarified or strengthened. This can include better evidence, adjusted thresholds, regional feedback, additional examples, or a clearer recommended action.

items.label: Add a comment
items.text: Use this for a practical observation, question or regional note that should sit beneath an entry without changing the entry itself.

contributing.note: Suggested entries, suggested changes and comments do not appear automatically. They are reviewed and de-identified before being added to the framework.
